questions.explanation: Iceberg supports identity, bucket, truncate, year/month/day/hour. Regex-based partitioning is not a built-in transform. Choose transforms that are deterministic, reversible-for-pruning, and produce balanced partitions. `bucket(N, key)` is the standard tool for high-cardinality keys that need parallel scans.

questions.prompt: **Partition evolution** in Iceberg means:
options: Rewriting all existing files when you change the partition spec
Iceberg records partition-spec versions in metadata; new data is written under the new spec while old data keeps the old spec, and queries plan correctly against both
The partition column type can be changed without notice
Partitions are recomputed on every read
questions.explanation: Iceberg can evolve a partition spec (e.g. `day(ts)` → `hour(ts)`) without rewriting old data. The manifest stores which spec each file was written under, and planning applies the appropriate transform per spec. Lets you correct early partitioning mistakes without a multi-TB rewrite.

questions.prompt: In Iceberg, the difference between **Copy-on-Write (CoW)** and **Merge-on-Read (MoR)** for row-level updates is:
options: CoW rewrites entire data files containing changed rows; MoR writes delete files (positional or equality) that readers merge with the base data files at query time
CoW is older and not supported anymore
MoR uses Avro; CoW uses Parquet
CoW supports time travel; MoR does not
questions.explanation: CoW: rewrite the affected data files — expensive writes, fast reads, clean files. MoR: append delete files describing changes — fast writes, slower reads (must apply deletes), and you need periodic compaction to keep read amplification bounded. Choose based on write-vs-read ratio and latency tolerance.

questions.prompt: The Delta Lake **`_delta_log`** directory contains: (Select all that apply)
options: Numbered JSON files describing each commit's added/removed files and metadata changes
Periodic Parquet checkpoint files that snapshot the log state for fast log replay
The actual data files of the table
A CRC file alongside each commit JSON for integrity verification
A separate index file for column statistics
questions.explanation: `_delta_log` holds the transaction log: per-commit JSONs (`00000000000000000000.json`...), periodic Parquet checkpoints to bound log-replay cost, and `.crc` files for integrity. Data files live alongside under the table root. Column stats are embedded in the JSON `add` actions, not a separate index.

questions.prompt: Delta Lake creates a **checkpoint** in `_delta_log` roughly every 10 commits because:
options: It enforces a maximum of 10 versions

Without checkpoints, opening the table requires replaying every JSON commit since creation — checkpoints let readers start from a recent Parquet snapshot of the log and apply only the JSON commits after it, bounding cold-read latency
Spark cannot read more than 10 JSON files at once
It triggers VACUUM
questions.explanation: Log replay cost grows linearly with commits. Checkpoints serialize the materialized log state (current set of files + metadata) to Parquet so readers only replay the tail of JSONs since the last checkpoint. For high-commit-rate tables (e.g. streaming sinks) tuning `delta.checkpointInterval` lower keeps cold reads fast.

questions.prompt: Running `VACUUM` on a Delta table with the default 7-day retention will:
options: Compact small files into larger ones
Permanently delete data files no longer referenced by any commit older than the retention threshold — which breaks time travel beyond that horizon
Re-cluster the table by Z-ORDER
Refresh the table statistics
questions.explanation: VACUUM removes orphaned/obsolete data files past the retention window. After VACUUM you can no longer query snapshots older than the retention bound. Don't confuse VACUUM with OPTIMIZE (file compaction) or ANALYZE/COMPUTE STATISTICS (stats refresh). Setting retention too short trades storage for losing time-travel and recovery.

questions.prompt: **Z-ORDER** in Delta is most useful when:
options: A single column drives 95% of predicate filters and you want strict sorted order on it

Multiple columns are commonly used together in predicates and you want files clustered such that any one of them can prune well — Z-ORDER multidimensionally interleaves the sort so files have tight min/max ranges on several columns
You want to migrate from Parquet to ORC
You want to enforce partition boundaries
questions.explanation: Z-ORDER (a space-filling curve / Morton order) interleaves bits of multiple columns so that data files are clustered by all of them simultaneously — less optimal than a single-column sort for that one column but much better than partitioning when several columns matter. For single-column filtering, a normal sort or partition wins.

questions.prompt: **Deletion vectors** in Delta Lake (and Iceberg's positional deletes) avoid the cost of CoW by:
options: Skipping VACUUM
Recording which row positions in existing data files are 'deleted' as a small auxiliary file; readers apply the bitmap on the fly so the base data file isn't rewritten
Encrypting deleted rows so readers can't decode them
Marking the entire file as tombstoned
questions.explanation: Deletion vectors store a compact bitmap of deleted row positions per data file. UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE no longer rewrites the whole file — it writes the vector. Readers skip masked rows. Faster mutations, but compaction must eventually merge vectors back into rewritten data files or query plans pay a growing tax.

questions.prompt: Delta's **Change Data Feed (CDF)** exposes:
options: Only inserts to the table

Row-level `_change_type` (insert / update_preimage / update_postimage / delete) plus `_commit_version` and `_commit_timestamp`, so downstream consumers can incrementally process changes without reading the whole table
Schema changes as DDL events
Compaction events from OPTIMIZE
questions.explanation: CDF turns a Delta table into a CDC source. Enabled at table or write time, it materializes per-row change records readable via `table_changes()`. Consumers can stream incremental diffs into other tables or stores without a full scan or external CDC tool — useful for SCD2 maintenance, fanout to feature stores, etc.

questions.prompt: True **exactly-once** end-to-end in a Flink job that reads from Kafka and writes to S3/HDFS requires:
options: Setting `enable.idempotence=true` on the source consumer
A combination of Kafka transactional source offsets committed in the same checkpoint as the sink's two-phase-commit, with sink files renamed atomically only after the checkpoint barrier completes
Replaying every message from offset 0
Disabling watermarks
questions.explanation: Flink achieves end-to-end exactly-once via aligned checkpoints + TwoPhaseCommitSinkFunction (or Kafka transactions). Source offsets, operator state, and sink-side staged outputs all commit atomically per checkpoint; mid-checkpoint sink files are pre-committed but invisible until the global commit. Without a transactional sink you get at-least-once at best.

questions.prompt: '**Effectively-once**' is often used (instead of 'exactly-once') in streaming pipelines because:
options: The network can drop packets and that's an unsolvable problem
Even with at-least-once delivery, an idempotent sink keyed by a deterministic event id produces the same observable end state — same outcome, lower cost than a transactional pipeline
Flink does not support exactly-once
Exactly-once is only available in proprietary systems
questions.explanation: Effectively-once: the user-visible state is identical to what exactly-once would produce, achieved cheaply via idempotent writes (upsert by primary key, deterministic event id, dedup state). For many warehouse/sink targets it's a better engineering tradeoff than a fully transactional 2PC path.

questions.prompt: A streaming job uses event-time windowing with a watermark of `event_ts - 5 minutes` and `allowedLateness = 2 minutes`. An event with `event_ts = T` arrives at processing-time `T + 9 minutes`. What happens?
options: The event is processed normally — it's within the allowed lateness
The event is dropped: the watermark has passed `T+5m`, allowed lateness extends only to `T+7m`, the window for T was finalized at `T+7m` (2 minutes ago), so this event is past lateness
It triggers a recomputation of every window
It is sent to the broadcast state
questions.explanation: Watermark = `event_ts - 5m` means the engine asserts no event with event_time ≤ watermark will arrive. Window for `T` closes when watermark passes `T`. Allowed lateness keeps the window state available for an extra 2m of late arrivals. After `T+5m (close) + 2m (lateness) = T+7m` the window is purged; an arrival at `T+9m` is dropped (or sent to a side output if configured).

questions.prompt: Which **watermark strategies** are commonly used in production stream engines? (Select all that apply)
options: Bounded out-of-orderness — watermark = max event_ts seen − fixed delay (e.g. 30s)
Punctuated watermarks — emit a watermark only when a specific marker event is seen
Per-partition watermarks aggregated to a single operator watermark (min across partitions)
Processing-time watermarks — watermark = wall clock
Watermarks set by a future ML model
questions.explanation: Bounded out-of-orderness is the default. Punctuated is useful when you control the source and can inject markers (e.g. end-of-batch sentinels). Per-partition watermarks (Flink) take the min across partitions so one stalled partition doesn't advance the watermark prematurely. Processing-time isn't really a watermark — it's the absence of event-time semantics.

questions.prompt: A long-running Flink job accumulates **keyed state** that grows unboundedly (one entry per user session, never expired). The right primary remediation is:
options: Increase heap size
Set a **state TTL** on the keyed-state descriptor so idle entries are evicted, and pair it with the appropriate cleanup strategy (in-background / on-access)
Disable checkpointing
Switch to processing-time windows
questions.explanation: Unbounded state is a death-spiral: checkpoints get slow, recovery times explode, RocksDB churns. State TTL with `cleanupInBackground()` or `cleanupFullSnapshot()` keeps state bounded. Without TTL, even RocksDB will eventually choke. Pair with metric on `state.size` per operator.

questions.prompt: Using the **RocksDB state backend** in Flink (vs the in-memory heap backend) is appropriate when:
options: State fits comfortably in heap and you want lowest latency
State exceeds heap (tens of GB to TBs) and you can tolerate ~10× higher per-access latency in exchange for incremental checkpointing and bounded memory
You don't need checkpoints
The job processes fewer than 1000 keys
questions.explanation: RocksDB persists state on local disk with an LSM tree, supporting much larger state than heap and enabling **incremental** checkpoints (only changed SSTs uploaded). Cost: per-access serde + disk I/O — ~order of magnitude slower than heap. Use it when state size is the bottleneck.

questions.prompt: A **savepoint** differs from a **checkpoint** in Flink primarily because:
options: A savepoint is automatic; a checkpoint is manual

Savepoints are user-triggered, retained indefinitely by default, and intended for planned operations (upgrade, code change, state migration); checkpoints are engine-managed for failure recovery and may be expired
Checkpoints support state migration; savepoints do not
They are the same concept under different names
questions.explanation: Both are consistent state snapshots, but their lifecycles and intents differ. Checkpoints are automatic and ephemeral (failure recovery). Savepoints are manual, immutable, format-stable across versions, and used to upgrade code/schema/operators with state intact.

questions.prompt: **Broadcast state** in Flink is designed for:
options: Storing each key's window state in every task
Distributing a small, slowly-changing reference dataset (e.g. rules, feature flags, lookup tables) to every parallel instance of a keyed operator so it can be joined locally without shuffling
Replicating Kafka topics across regions
Building leaderboards
questions.explanation: Broadcast state replicates a stream to every operator instance — ideal for control planes (a stream of rule updates, configuration, models). The keyed stream stays partitioned; the broadcast side is everywhere. Don't broadcast large or fast-changing data — you'll blow memory and lose the locality benefit.

questions.prompt: Increasing Kafka producer **`linger.ms`** from `0` to `10` typically:
options: Reduces throughput because the producer waits before sending
Improves throughput by letting batches fill up (more records per request) at the cost of a small added end-to-end latency
Causes message loss
Has no effect when `acks=all`
questions.explanation: `linger.ms` is the producer's allowed wait time to accumulate more records into a batch. Higher = bigger batches = better compression + fewer requests = higher throughput, with a bounded latency penalty. Common sweet spot 5–20ms for high-volume pipelines; near zero for low-latency interactive paths.

questions.prompt: Which producer settings are required for **idempotent + ordered** writes to a single partition? (Select all that apply)
options: `enable.idempotence=true`
`acks=all`
`max.in.flight.requests.per.connection ≤ 5`
`retries > 0`
Disable compression
questions.explanation: Idempotent producer (sequence numbers per partition) requires `acks=all`, `retries>0`, and `max.in.flight.requests.per.connection ≤ 5` (broker validates sequence). Together they prevent duplicates AND preserve order despite retries. Compression is orthogonal — keep it on (lz4/zstd) for throughput.

questions.prompt: Which statements about Kafka producer compression codecs are correct? (Select all that apply)
options: Snappy and lz4 prioritize CPU speed over compression ratio — workhorse defaults for most workloads
zstd typically achieves 20-40% better compression than snappy at the cost of more producer CPU
Compression is applied per record batch on the producer; brokers store the compressed batch directly when `compression.type=producer`
zstd is incompatible with Kafka
Compression always reduces end-to-end latency
Choosing a codec should be measured on real payloads — results vary wildly with field distributions
questions.explanation: Snappy/lz4: low CPU, moderate ratio. zstd: better ratio, more CPU — wins when network/storage dominates. Compression happens at the producer batch level and brokers can store as-is. zstd has been supported for years. Compression can ADD latency (more CPU time) even as it improves throughput — measure on real payloads.

questions.prompt: A Kafka consumer keeps triggering rebalances because of `max.poll.interval.ms` timeouts. The most appropriate fix is usually:
options: Increase `session.timeout.ms`
Reduce `max.poll.records` and/or move heavy processing off the poll loop (so each `poll()` call returns promptly), and only after that consider tuning `max.poll.interval.ms` upward
Disable auto-commit
Increase replication factor
questions.explanation: `max.poll.interval.ms` bounds how long between polls. Long batches or slow processing in-line causes the consumer to miss the deadline and be kicked from the group. Fixes: shrink the batch, pause/resume the consumer while async-processing, or push heavy work to a worker pool. Just raising the timeout masks the problem and slows failure detection.

questions.prompt: Under the Confluent Schema Registry **`BACKWARD`** compatibility mode, you may:
options: Add a new required field with no default
Add a new field with a default value, or remove a field that had a default — i.e. the new schema can read data written with old schemas
Change a field type freely
Rename any field
questions.explanation: BACKWARD: new schema reads old data. Safe additive changes are 'add an optional field with a default' and 'remove a field that had a default' (new readers know how to fill it). Required-no-default adds and type changes break backward compat. FORWARD inverts the relation; FULL is the conjunction of both.

questions.prompt: Which statements about Confluent **subject naming strategies** are correct? (Select all that apply)
options: `TopicNameStrategy` (default) ties one subject to one topic — simplest, but you can only have one schema per topic
`RecordNameStrategy` ties the subject to the Avro record's fullname, allowing **multiple event types in the same topic**
`TopicRecordNameStrategy` combines both — topic + record name — useful when the same record type appears in many topics with different evolution rules
Naming strategy can be changed at runtime without affecting consumers
The serialized message carries the schema ID; the consumer uses that to fetch the schema from the registry
questions.explanation: TopicNameStrategy is the most common but limits a topic to one schema. RecordNameStrategy + multi-event topics are essential for transactional outbox style fanout. TopicRecordNameStrategy combines both. Changing strategies mid-flight breaks consumers — it's a coordinated migration. Wire format: magic byte + 4-byte schema ID + payload.

questions.prompt: Compared to Avro, **Protobuf** for stream payloads tends to:
options: Be slower and larger

Be slightly more compact and faster to deserialize in compiled languages, with stricter schema-as-code workflow (.proto files compiled to types) — at the cost of evolution being trickier for added required fields and oneofs
Embed the schema in every message
Require JSON Schema validation
questions.explanation: Avro: schema-with-data (or via registry), great for dynamic readers; Protobuf: schema-as-code, often slightly smaller/faster, but evolution rules differ (everything is technically optional in proto3, but field numbers must not be reused). Choice often comes down to team workflow and language ecosystem more than wire-size.

questions.prompt: Where is the **strictest** place to enforce a data contract in a streaming pipeline?
options: At the consumer — because they're the ones experiencing pain
At the **producer** (pre-publish validation) — it stops bad data from entering the bus, prevents replay-driven outages, and keeps ownership of the contract upstream where the schema is authored
At the analytics dashboard
At the storage layer only
questions.explanation: Producer-side enforcement (linting in CI, schema-aware client libs, registry compatibility checks before publishing) catches violations before they propagate. Consumer-side validation catches what the producer missed, but it's reactive: by then the bus already holds bad data. Belt and braces in practice, but the producer is where the contract lives.

questions.prompt: Which checks are **deterministic** data-quality checks (vs anomaly/statistical)? (Select all that apply)
options: `NOT NULL` on a required column
`UNIQUE` on a natural key
Row count within ±3 standard deviations of the trailing 30-day mean
FK referential integrity to a parent table
Z-score anomaly detection on a numeric column
Regex pattern on an email column
questions.explanation: Deterministic checks: NOT NULL, UNIQUE, FK, regex/format — pass/fail on a single row or relationship. Statistical/anomaly checks (rolling z-score, drift detection, distribution KL-divergence) compare to history and tolerate noise. A good DQ framework runs both, with stricter SLOs on deterministic checks.

questions.prompt: Frameworks like **Great Expectations / Soda / dbt tests** typically catch which class of issues earliest?
options: Network partitions
Schema and content regressions in the transformation layer — nullability, ranges, uniqueness, referential integrity, freshness — by running assertions on the data after each pipeline step
Hardware failures
User authentication bugs
questions.explanation: These tools assert post-condition expectations on tables (row counts, value ranges, freshness, uniqueness, FK integrity). They catch regressions before downstream consumers do. They don't catch infra failures (those are observability/alerting). Pair: dbt tests at every model + Soda monitors on critical tables + Monte Carlo / Anomalo for anomaly detection on outputs.

questions.prompt: A fact table is loaded by two concurrent batch jobs that each assign surrogate keys via `MAX(sk)+1`. The likely failure mode is:
options: Slow queries
Surrogate-key collisions when both jobs read the same MAX value at the same time — producing duplicate keys that violate the PK or cause silent overwrites in upserts
Schema drift
Late-arriving facts
questions.explanation: `MAX+1` is not concurrency-safe. Two concurrent loaders both see `MAX=42`, both produce SK=43, and you get a PK collision (or worse, a silent overwrite with MERGE). Fix: use a sequence/identity column (DB-managed monotonic ids), a hash-based surrogate (hash of NK), or serialize loaders via leader election.

questions.prompt: A fact_orders table has columns for both `order_date` and `event_count` where some rows record one order, some record a daily aggregate of N orders. Which problems does this MIXED GRAIN cause? (Select all that apply)
options: SUM(event_count) double-counts because the same orders appear once as a detail row and again rolled into a daily row
Filters on dimension keys behave inconsistently — the aggregate row has no specific customer_sk, so customer-scoped queries silently drop those rows
BI users have to know which rows mean what — a leakage of pipeline complexity to consumers
It improves storage efficiency by avoiding duplicate rows
Joins to other facts (returns, shipments) at the order grain become incorrect
It improves query latency by pre-aggregating common reports
questions.explanation: Mixed grain is the cardinal sin of fact design: measures stop being additive, semantics depend on which subset you happen to filter to, and downstream users get burned. The right fix is two facts — fact_order (one row per order) and fact_order_daily (one row per day) — letting consumers choose explicitly. It does not save storage and the "performance" benefit is fake (you would build the daily aggregate as a separate model anyway).

questions.prompt: A dim_customer is joined to fact_sales on `customer_sk`, but ~3% of fact rows have a `NULL` customer_sk. A query `SELECT SUM(amount) FROM fact_sales f JOIN dim_customer d ON f.customer_sk = d.customer_sk WHERE d.country = 'US'` will:
options: Return the correct US total

**Silently drop** the 3% of fact rows with NULL customer_sk (because `NULL = NULL` is false) — underreporting totals; the fix is an outer join + an 'Unknown' (-1) sentinel customer row to preserve fact integrity
Fail with an error
Return all rows including non-US
questions.explanation: INNER JOIN on a NULL key drops the row — silently. The classic mitigation is the 'Unknown'/'Not Applicable' dimension sentinel row (SK = -1) referenced by every fact that doesn't have a real dim match. Now the inner join works, the country filter cleanly excludes Unknown, and totals reconcile.

questions.prompt: A dim_date dimension stores `date_id` as `YYYYMMDD` integer with no timezone column, while facts come from event servers in 30 countries. The most likely defect is:
options: BigInt overflow
Timezone-bucketing bugs: a single UTC instant maps to different dates in different zones, and reports that mix per-region 'local day' with UTC produce inconsistent daily totals
Slow joins
Date dim becomes too small
questions.explanation: Without explicit timezone in the fact or a per-region date dim, a transaction at 2025-11-01 23:00 UTC is 'Oct 31' in some regions and 'Nov 1' in others. Pin facts to UTC AND store local_date_id (computed at ingest from event_tz). Without this discipline, daily KPIs across regions never reconcile.

questions.prompt: In a Dynamo-style **leaderless** replication system with N=3, W=2, R=2, what consistency guarantee do you get?
options: Linearizable reads
Strong-consistency reads because W + R > N guarantees at least one read replica saw the latest write — conflicts (e.g. concurrent writes) still need application-level resolution
Eventual consistency only
Reads always see the most recent write regardless of W
questions.explanation: R + W > N means read and write quorums overlap on at least one replica, so reads see at least one copy of any committed write. This is 'strong consistency' in the Dynamo sense but NOT linearizability — concurrent writes still produce sibling versions to resolve. Conflict resolution (last-write-wins, vector clocks, CRDTs) is still application-level.

questions.prompt: In leaderless / Dynamo-style replication, which of the following help the cluster recover from temporary node failures? (Select all that apply)
options: Hinted handoff — a coordinator temporarily stores writes for an unavailable replica and forwards them when it returns
Read repair — inconsistent values discovered during a read trigger an asynchronous write to bring stale replicas up to date
Anti-entropy / Merkle-tree-based background reconciliation between replicas
Sloppy quorum — if the target replica is down, the coordinator accepts the write on a substitute node (still W replicas total) to maintain availability
Disabling replication
questions.explanation: Hinted handoff, read repair, anti-entropy, and sloppy quorum are the four pillars of Dynamo-style fault tolerance. Sloppy quorum is the most controversial: it preserves write availability at the cost of sometimes acknowledging a write on a 'temporary' replica that isn't in the preference list — trading durability semantics for availability.

questions.prompt: **Vector clocks** vs **version vectors** vs **hybrid logical clocks (HLC)**: the most accurate one-line distinction is:
options: They are interchangeable names for the same thing

Vector clocks track event ordering per process (size = #processes), version vectors track per-replica versions of a single object (size = #replicas), HLCs combine physical time with a logical counter so they approximate wall clock while still detecting causality
HLCs use atomic clocks; vector clocks don't
Version vectors detect concurrency; vector clocks don't
questions.explanation: All three capture causality, but they scale and integrate differently. Version vectors are commonly used for object replication (Dynamo). HLCs (Kulkarni et al.) give monotonic, physical-time-close timestamps that detect causality — used in CockroachDB, YugabyteDB — and avoid needing TrueTime hardware to get near-linearizable timestamps.

questions.prompt: **CRDTs (Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types)** are most appropriate when:
options: You need general-purpose distributed transactions

Your conflicts can be modeled by a mathematical structure (commutative/associative merge) such that any merge order produces the same result — e.g. counters (G-Counter, PN-Counter), sets (OR-Set, LWW-Set), sequences (RGA, Yjs)
You want SQL semantics
You only have one writer
questions.explanation: CRDTs let multiple replicas accept writes concurrently and merge automatically without coordination, because the data type's merge operation is commutative, associative, and idempotent. Great for collaborative editing, presence, counters, sets. Bad for anything needing strict ordering (inventory decrement that must not go negative, financial postings).

questions.prompt: With plain consistent hashing on the ring, adding a new shard often causes **uneven** redistribution. The standard mitigation is:
options: Resharding the entire cluster
**Virtual nodes (vnodes)** — each physical node owns many tokens spread around the ring, so adding a node steals a small slice from many existing nodes rather than one big slice from a neighbor
Disabling consistent hashing
Switching to range partitioning
questions.explanation: With one token per node, adding a node pulls keys from a single neighbor — large move and uneven post-state. With vnodes (e.g. 256 per node), the new node steals a few keys from each of many existing nodes — small, even, parallelizable. Cassandra, ScyllaDB, and Dynamo all use vnodes.

questions.prompt: You shard a multi-tenant SaaS by `tenant_id` (hash). One tenant becomes 100× larger than the median. The cleanest fix that minimizes blast radius is:
options: Re-hash everyone with a new function
**Pin the elephant tenant to its own dedicated shard(s)** and continue using hash sharding for everyone else — isolating the heavy tenant without disrupting the rest
Switch to range sharding
Cache more aggressively
questions.explanation: Hybrid sharding: a tenant-isolation list (or explicit shard pinning table) overrides the hash function for known elephants. Doesn't touch the other 99% of tenants and gives you a knob to scale the loud one independently (more capacity, separate maintenance windows, even a separate region).

questions.prompt: A **vectorized** OLAP execution engine (DuckDB, ClickHouse, Velox) achieves much higher throughput than row-at-a-time engines because:
options: It uses GPUs
It processes columns in fixed-size batches that fit in CPU cache and SIMD lanes — amortizing per-row interpreter overhead and enabling auto-vectorization of tight loops
It avoids hash joins
It runs queries in parallel across threads
questions.explanation: Vectorized execution operates on batches (often 1024 or 2048 values at a time) per column. CPU pipelines stay full, branch predictors win, SIMD instructions apply. The Volcano-style row-by-row interpreter dispatches per row — vastly more overhead. JIT compilation (HyPer-style) is the next step beyond vectorization but isn't strictly required.

questions.prompt: **Late materialization** in a columnar engine means:
options: Computing the result lazily after the query returns to the client
Applying filters and joins on column references / row-ids before reconstructing whole tuples — columns not needed by the final projection are never read from disk
Computing aggregates at the end of the plan
Caching results in the engine
questions.explanation: Late materialization keeps execution in the columnar / row-id domain as long as possible. A predicate on column A produces a row-id bitmap; a join produces another. Only at the end (or when needed for a function) does the engine 'materialize' the value columns. Saves I/O, decompression, and memory — vs early materialization that reconstructs rows up front.

questions.prompt: Spark / BigQuery / Snowflake all support a **broadcast join** when one side is small. The defining property:
options: It always uses sort-merge

The small side is replicated to every executor; the large side is scanned locally with a hash probe — avoiding a shuffle of the large side, but limited by the small side's size fitting in worker memory
It produces a Cartesian product
It requires both sides to be partitioned identically
questions.explanation: Broadcast hash join is the go-to when one side is small (≤10–100 MB typical default, tunable). Shuffles a huge fact table can dominate runtime; replicating a small dim avoids the shuffle entirely. If 'small' is misjudged (skew, stale stats), the broadcast OOMs the cluster — hence adaptive runtime decisions in AQE / Snowflake's auto-broadcast.

questions.prompt: Spark **Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)** can change a plan at runtime by:
options: Re-reading the data each time

Coalescing shuffle partitions when actual shuffle sizes are smaller than expected, switching sort-merge join to broadcast when one side turned out small after filtering, and splitting skewed partitions
Disabling the cost-based optimizer
Compressing intermediate results
questions.explanation: AQE collects shuffle statistics mid-query and rewrites the plan: coalesce tiny partitions to reduce task overhead, dynamically pick broadcast join when a side falls under threshold post-filter, and break apart skewed partitions to even out parallelism. Tunable via `spark.sql.adaptive.*` knobs.

questions.prompt: **Two-phase commit (2PC)** is rarely used as the foundation of a modern distributed system because:
options: It is slower than 3PC
It blocks indefinitely on coordinator failure (participants hold locks awaiting the commit decision), is sensitive to slow participants, and doesn't tolerate network partitions gracefully
It cannot be implemented over TCP
It is incompatible with replication
questions.explanation: 2PC's prepare-then-commit protocol is correct in normal conditions but degenerates badly on failure: a crashed coordinator leaves participants 'in-doubt' holding locks; a slow participant blocks everyone. Modern systems prefer consensus (Raft/Paxos) over fewer transactional participants, or saga-style decentralized coordination, or Spanner-style TrueTime + Paxos shards.

questions.prompt: **Sagas** model a long-running business transaction across services as:
options: One big distributed transaction with 2PC
A sequence of local transactions, each emitting an event that triggers the next; failures trigger compensating transactions to undo prior steps
A queue of work for a single consumer
A snapshot of the entire system state
questions.explanation: Saga: T1 → T2 → T3 (commit locally each step); on failure at Tk, run C(k-1), C(k-2), … C(1) compensating transactions in reverse. No 2PC, no global locks. Tradeoffs: compensations are application-level, eventual consistency is exposed to callers, and you must design idempotency into every step.

questions.prompt: **Orchestration** vs **choreography** for sagas: the key tradeoff is:
options: Orchestration is always faster

Orchestration centralizes the workflow in a saga coordinator (clear flow, easier to reason about, single point of complexity) — choreography distributes the decisions via events (loosely coupled, harder to trace, no single owner of the flow)
Choreography requires Kafka
Orchestration cannot use compensating transactions
questions.explanation: Orchestration: a central coordinator (Temporal, Camunda, Cadence) drives the saga. Easier debugging and visibility; coordinator becomes a critical service. Choreography: each service listens for events and decides its next action. Highly decoupled; emergent flow that's hard to discover or change globally. Most production sagas evolve into orchestration once flows get nontrivial.

questions.prompt: The **transactional outbox** pattern solves which problem?
options: Service discovery

Atomically updating the database AND emitting a corresponding event — by writing the event into an `outbox` table in the same DB transaction, then letting a CDC reader (or relay) publish it to the message bus exactly once per row
Encryption at rest
Schema evolution
questions.explanation: Dual writes (DB + Kafka) without 2PC are unsafe — you can crash between them and lose/duplicate events. Outbox: write the event into an `outbox` table in the same local transaction. A CDC process (Debezium) tails the DB log and publishes outbox rows to Kafka. Atomic with the business write, and idempotent on the replay path via the row id.

questions.prompt: Which design choices make an HTTP API's idempotency-key implementation robust? (Select all that apply)
options: Accept a client-supplied `Idempotency-Key` header rather than deriving from body hash alone
Store the (key -> first response code/body/headers) mapping in durable storage with a TTL of 24h-7d
On replay return the stored response without re-executing the side effect
Detect "same key, different body" via a stored request hash and return 409/422
Always retry until the call succeeds and let the database UNIQUE constraint reject duplicates
Coalesce concurrent first-attempts via row-level lock or advisory lock so a retry while the first is in-flight waits for the result
questions.explanation: Stripe-style idempotency: client supplies key, server caches the response for replay. Pair with body-hash check to catch reuse with different payloads, and concurrency-coalescing locks so two parallel retries do not both run the side effect. "Retry-until-success + UNIQUE rejection" wastes throughput and risks dropping legitimate writes.

questions.prompt: Google Spanner's **TrueTime** is special because:
options: It is the fastest clock in the world

It exposes uncertainty: `TT.now()` returns an interval `[earliest, latest]`, and writes wait out the uncertainty so that any later read with `TT.now().earliest > write.latest` is guaranteed to see the write — enabling external consistency without per-shard coordination on the read path
It is a software-only clock
It only works in one region
questions.explanation: TrueTime makes the clock's uncertainty a first-class API (`epsilon`). Writes commit with a timestamp and wait the epsilon out (commit wait); subsequent reads with timestamps strictly greater than that see the write. This gives Spanner externally consistent (linearizable) globally-distributed transactions without contacting every shard on read. Yugabyte/CockroachDB approximate this without atomic clocks via HLCs + bounded staleness.

questions.prompt: **Write-behind (write-back)** caching:
options: Writes synchronously to the database and then to the cache
Writes only to the cache; flushes to the database asynchronously — lowest write latency, but on cache failure unflushed writes are lost; requires durable cache or write-ahead log to be safe
Bypasses the cache for writes
Encrypts every write
questions.explanation: Write-behind: app writes to cache, cache acks immediately, a background process flushes to the backing store. Excellent latency and write absorption, dangerous durability profile. Mitigation: durable cache (Redis with AOF), WAL on the cache layer, or batched-with-checkpoint flushes. Don't use for systems-of-record without those safety nets.

questions.prompt: **Negative caching** (caching the fact that 'no value exists for this key') primarily prevents:
options: Hot-key stampedes on existing data
Backend overload from repeated lookups of nonexistent keys — e.g. an attacker probing for usernames, or a buggy client calling missing endpoints in a loop
Cache eviction
TLS handshake costs
questions.explanation: Without negative caching, every miss hits the database. Bots and bugs can DoS your DB by querying for missing keys forever. Cache a short-TTL 'not found' marker so the second-through-N-th lookup returns from cache. Keep the negative TTL short (seconds-minutes) to avoid masking newly-created data.

questions.prompt: **Request coalescing** (single-flight) for a hot cache key:
options: Splits the key across multiple cache nodes
Ensures only one concurrent backend lookup for a given key while N requests are in flight; the lookup result is fanned out to all waiters — eliminating stampede on expiry
Increases the TTL on hot keys
Logs every cache miss
questions.explanation: Even with a perfect cache, the moment a hot key expires, N concurrent requests will all miss. Single-flight (Go's `singleflight`, Caffeine's loading cache) deduplicates: first request fetches, others wait, all receive the same result. Pair with soft TTL / probabilistic early refresh to remove the spike entirely.

questions.prompt: A Redis Cluster slot migration (`MOVED`/`ASK` responses) is most likely to cause a latency spike when:
options: The migration runs at night
Many large keys must be transferred during the resharding window — the source node blocks on `MIGRATE` for each big key, and clients chase MOVED/ASK redirects, inflating tail latency
TLS is enabled
There are too few keys
questions.explanation: Redis `MIGRATE` is synchronous and blocks the source instance for the duration of the key transfer. A handful of multi-MB keys (large hashes / lists / streams) is enough to spike p99. Mitigations: pre-split or shrink big keys before migration; use `--cluster reshard --pipeline` with smaller batches; schedule for low-traffic windows.

questions.prompt: When moving 100TB of warm data from S3 Standard to S3 Glacier Deep Archive, which cost surprises commonly bite teams? (Select all that apply)
options: Per-GB retrieval cost on restore can dwarf the per-GB storage savings if the data ends up being accessed
Lifecycle transitions themselves incur a per-object request cost — significant when objects are small and numerous
Minimum storage duration penalties (e.g. 180 days for Deep Archive) — deleting early triggers a pro-rated charge
Restore time of 12+ hours violates downstream RTO assumptions
Glacier Deep Archive does not support encryption
AWS recomputes ETag for archived files
questions.explanation: Glacier Deep Archive is cheap to keep but expensive to read and to transition into/out of. Minimum-duration billing punishes "I just wanted to try it" experiments. Restore SLAs are measured in hours. Encryption works normally. Wrong-tiering active data turns "cheap storage" into "expensive surprise" — always confirm access patterns first.

questions.prompt: A 50TB lake has 500 million Parquet files because writes are 100KB micro-batches. The **dominant** cost component at S3-scale is often:
options: Per-GB storage — the files together are big
Per-request charges (PUT/LIST/GET) and increased metastore catalog cost — a billion file-listings per query, multiplied by S3's per-request pricing, can dwarf the storage line item
Egress
Encryption
questions.explanation: Small files punish you on TWO axes: query performance (planning + S3 listings dominate scan time) AND cost (per-request pricing, control-plane operations, increased metastore load). Compact to 128MB-1GB files (sweet spot for Parquet row groups and S3 multipart). It's the cheapest optimization you'll ever do.

questions.prompt: **Crypto-shredding** (cryptographic erasure) supports GDPR's right to be forgotten in immutable systems by:
options: Recomputing all data with a new password

Encrypting each subject's data with a per-subject key; when the subject requests deletion, the key is destroyed, rendering all that data unreadable forever — even if the encrypted bytes remain in immutable storage (Iceberg snapshots, event logs, backups)
Compressing the data
Moving data to cold storage
questions.explanation: Physical deletion in immutable systems is expensive or impossible. With per-subject (or per-record) keys held in a KMS, deletion = key destruction. Bytes remain but are unrecoverable ciphertext. Tradeoff: huge key-management blast radius, every read becomes a key lookup, and you must include cryptographic erasure into your threat model (what if the algorithm is broken in 20 years?).

questions.prompt: **k-anonymity** of `k=5` on a dataset means:
options: Only 5 people can access the dataset

Every record is indistinguishable from at least `k-1` others on the quasi-identifier columns — mitigating re-identification, but vulnerable to homogeneity and background-knowledge attacks (hence l-diversity, t-closeness as extensions)
The dataset has at most 5 columns
Encryption uses a 5-byte key
questions.explanation: k-anonymity protects against linkage attacks by ensuring quasi-identifier groups have size ≥k. But if all 5 records in a group share a sensitive attribute (homogeneity attack) the attacker still learns it. l-diversity ensures sensitive value variety within each group; t-closeness bounds the distance between group and overall distribution.

questions.prompt: **Differential privacy** gives a quantitative privacy guarantee by:
options: Encrypting individual rows

Adding calibrated noise to aggregate queries such that the probability of any output changes by at most a multiplicative factor e^ε whether or not any single individual is in the dataset — the smaller ε, the stronger the guarantee and the more noise
Tokenizing PII
Anonymizing rare values
questions.explanation: DP is a mathematical guarantee, not a heuristic. Choose ε (privacy budget) and the mechanism (Laplace, Gaussian) is calibrated so the answer's distribution barely changes when any one record is removed. Used by US Census 2020, Apple/Google for telemetry. Tradeoff: smaller ε = more noise = less accuracy for the same query; a finite budget across many queries.

questions.prompt: An **active-passive** multi-region database deployment differs from **active-active** primarily because:
options: Active-passive uses async replication; active-active uses sync

Active-passive serves all writes from one region and a standby promotes on failover (RPO > 0 with async replication); active-active accepts writes in all regions and must reconcile concurrent writes, trading RPO for write-conflict complexity
Active-active does not require replication
Active-passive cannot serve reads
questions.explanation: Active-passive: simpler, single writer, predictable consistency, but cross-region latency for writers from the standby's region; RPO determined by replication lag. Active-active: lower write latency everywhere, but you inherit multi-leader conflicts (LWW / CRDT / app-merge). Most enterprises start active-passive and only adopt active-active when write geography demands it.

questions.prompt: Cross-region replication lag of 30 seconds on a critical OLTP database means your **RPO** during a region failure is:
options: Exactly 0 — no data loss
Up to ~30 seconds of acknowledged writes are at risk of loss — because async replication had not shipped the trailing changes when the source region failed
Exactly 30 minutes
Unbounded
questions.explanation: RPO (Recovery Point Objective) bounds acceptable data loss. With async replication and a 30s typical lag, a region loss could discard ~30s of writes. Synchronous cross-region replication achieves RPO=0 but adds significant write latency. Common compromise: sync within region (RPO=0 in-region), async across regions, with careful failover playbooks.

questions.prompt: **DNS-based failover** (e.g. Route53 health checks) typically has the drawback that:
options: It requires a CDN
Client and intermediate DNS resolvers cache the DNS record — even with low TTLs (60s), legacy clients and resolvers may cache for many minutes, delaying failover beyond your RTO target
It cannot point to multiple regions
It does not support HTTPS
questions.explanation: DNS caching makes the announced TTL a hopeful lower bound, not a hard ceiling. Misbehaving resolvers, JVMs with infinite DNS caches, and corporate gateways have been observed caching for hours. For sub-minute failover use anycast routing (BGP/Global Load Balancer) or client-side load balancers that detect failure directly.

questions.prompt: Which practices most reliably **attribute warehouse compute cost** to teams/jobs? (Select all that apply)
options: Per-team warehouses / virtual warehouses (Snowflake) with usage tags
Query tagging via session parameters that flow into the audit log (e.g. `query_tag = team:growth, dag:fact_orders`)
Cost-attribution dbt models that join warehouse usage views to job metadata
Allocating cost evenly across all employees
Disabling auto-suspend
questions.explanation: Real FinOps starts with attribution. Per-team warehouses, query tags propagated through orchestration, and join-back models on warehouse_usage are the building blocks. Even allocation is the absence of attribution. Disabling auto-suspend wastes money — the opposite of FinOps.

questions.prompt: A Snowflake warehouse runs many small ad-hoc queries throughout the day. Lowering `AUTO_SUSPEND` from 600s to 60s usually:
options: Reduces cost by suspending the warehouse during idle gaps so you don't pay for unused compute
Increases query latency dramatically because every query restarts the warehouse
Has no effect
Breaks query result caching
questions.explanation: Snowflake bills per second of warehouse runtime (min 60s). A workload of bursty ad-hoc queries with 5–10 minute gaps wastes a huge fraction of paid time. Shorter auto-suspend saves money. Tradeoff: warm-cache loss between sessions; can be partially mitigated by query result caching (which lives in metadata, not the warehouse).

questions.prompt: Materializing a daily aggregate as a table (vs running it as a view on the fact) is a FinOps win when:
options: The view is queried more often than the underlying fact is updated, AND the cost of one rebuild < the savings from N consumer queries hitting a pre-aggregated table
Storage is more expensive than compute
The fact never changes
Every consumer wants raw data
questions.explanation: Materialization shifts compute from many reads to a few writes. Worth it when reads-per-rebuild is high and the rebuild is cheap. Bad when the fact rebuilds frequently and few consumers query the aggregate. Materialized views with incremental refresh (Snowflake MVs, BigQuery MVs) automate this tradeoff; classic dbt incremental models are the manual analog.

questions.prompt: Which signals are essential to monitor for a streaming pipeline? (Select all that apply)
options: End-to-end event-time-to-emission latency (p50, p95, p99)
Per-source / per-sink consumer lag
Watermark progression
Number of late events and side-output volume
Average JVM heap size of a specific operator
Internet weather
questions.explanation: User-facing SLO is end-to-end latency. Consumer lag and watermark progression are the leading indicators (something will stall before latency moves). Late events / side outputs measure correctness. Heap size of one operator is sometimes useful but isn't a universal must-have. 'Internet weather' is folklore.

questions.prompt: **Tail-based sampling** in a tracing system is preferred over head-based sampling when:
options: You want lowest collector overhead

You want to keep all traces with errors or high latency by deciding to sample only AFTER the full trace is assembled — head-based sampling has already discarded most traces before knowing whether they were interesting
You only have one service
Traces are short
questions.explanation: Head sampling decides at trace start (cheap, but blind to outcome). Tail sampling waits for spans and decides post-hoc, keeping high-latency or error traces at high rate while subsampling boring ones. Cost: collector must buffer in-flight traces, which is expensive at scale — the typical OpenTelemetry pattern uses a tail-sampling processor running near the services.

questions.prompt: An SLO of '99% of dashboard queries return in <2s, measured over 28 days' implies an **error budget** of approximately:
options: 0 seconds of slow queries per month

~1% of queries, which over 28 days is ~6.7 hours of cumulative violations if traffic is even — the team's burn rate against this budget should trigger alerts long before exhaustion (e.g. fast-burn at 2% per hour)
100% of queries
Unbounded
questions.explanation: Error budget = 1 − SLO over the window. 1% of 28 days of query-seconds is your tolerable burn. Multi-window multi-burn-rate alerts (Google SRE book) fire at 2%/hr (fast burn) and 5%/6hr (slow burn) so you act before exhausting the budget. Budgets pace risk: lots of budget left = ship; budget depleted = freeze and stabilize.

questions.prompt: When choosing between **Iceberg, Delta Lake, and Apache Hudi** for a new lakehouse, the strongest reason to pick **Hudi** specifically is usually:
options: It is the only format with ACID
First-class **upsert/merge-on-read** performance for high-volume mutable workloads (e.g. CDC ingest) with built-in record-level indexes and compaction scheduling
It is the only one with time travel
It is the only one usable from Spark
questions.explanation: All three have ACID, time travel, schema evolution. Hudi's differentiator is its mutation story — it was built around CDC upsert workloads with record-level indexes and async compaction. Iceberg leads on engine-agnostic SQL semantics and partition evolution; Delta leads on Databricks integration and CDF. Pick by workload, not religion.

questions.prompt: Comparing **Kafka, Kinesis, and Pub/Sub** for a new event bus, Pub/Sub differentiates itself most by:
options: Being the cheapest

Server-managed push subscriptions with no partition/shard concept — the service hides parallelism entirely; consumers scale automatically, no rebalances to manage, at the cost of weaker ordering guarantees (per-key ordering requires explicit ordering keys)
Strict per-topic ordering
Being open source
questions.explanation: Kafka and Kinesis expose partitions/shards — you scale by partition count, manage rebalances, and reason about per-partition order. Pub/Sub hides partitions: consumers receive messages without thinking about distribution. Pay for it with weaker default ordering (must opt into ordering keys per region) and less fine-grained throughput control. Choose by operational comfort + ordering needs.

questions.prompt: Between **Flink, Spark Structured Streaming, and Kafka Streams**, Flink most clearly wins when:
options: You want SQL only

You need true low-latency event-by-event processing, complex event-time windowing, and long-running stateful operators with savepoints and managed state migration — i.e. when you're building a streaming system, not adding streaming to a batch one
You only consume from Kafka and want minimal infra
You want serverless
questions.explanation: Flink: real streaming engine, event-by-event, mature event-time + watermarks + state. Spark Structured Streaming: micro-batch (Continuous mode is limited), great if you already live in Spark. Kafka Streams: library, no cluster to operate, perfect if 'everything is in Kafka' and you don't need a separate processing tier. None is universally best; the question is the SHAPE of your workload.

questions.prompt: Between **ClickHouse, Druid, and Pinot** for a real-time OLAP layer, ClickHouse most clearly differentiates by:
options: Being the only one that supports SQL

General-purpose SQL with broad join support, MergeTree storage with mutations, and strong ad-hoc query flexibility — vs Druid/Pinot which are tuned for low-latency time-series-dashboard workloads with limited joins
Native exactly-once semantics
Being managed-service-only
questions.explanation: ClickHouse: closer to a general OLAP DB — rich SQL, joins, MergeTree mutability. Druid/Pinot: segment-based, near-instant ingest and segment-level pruning for time-bounded queries with simple aggregations; joins are limited or recently added. For 'fast dashboards on streaming data' the latter two are sharper; for 'fast SQL with joins on big tables' ClickHouse leads.

questions.prompt: In an **event-sourced** system, snapshots of an aggregate's state are taken because:
options: Events are unreliable

Replaying every event from the beginning to materialize current state is O(n) in event count — periodic snapshots let you replay only the events after the snapshot, bounding cold-load and recovery time
Disk is cheap
Events lose meaning over time
questions.explanation: Pure event sourcing requires replaying the log to rebuild state — fine for short-lived aggregates, painful for long-running ones (a user account with 10y of events). Snapshots = periodic materialized state checkpoint, plus replay of subsequent events. The snapshot itself is derivable from events, so it's safe to regenerate.

questions.prompt: **CQRS** is naturally paired with event sourcing because:
options: Both require Kafka

Command side mutates the aggregate by appending events; query side maintains derived read-optimized projections from the same event stream — read and write models can evolve independently and the read model can be rebuilt by replay
It is required by GDPR
It eliminates the need for indexes
questions.explanation: Command-Query Responsibility Segregation splits the model: writes go through the aggregate root and produce events; reads consume different projections (per-query views) of those events. Event sourcing supplies the source of truth; CQRS supplies the consumer-shaped views — each rebuildable from the log.

questions.prompt: A **compacted topic** in Kafka retains:
options: Only the most recent message per key (older messages with the same key are eventually compacted away), plus all messages in the active head segment
All messages forever
Only the first message per key
Only messages from a specific producer
questions.explanation: Log compaction is per-key retention: at any time the topic contains AT LEAST the latest value per key. Older values are GC'd by a background compactor. Used for changelog topics (Kafka Streams state, configuration topics) where downstream consumers need 'final state' replay, not full history. Pair with a `null` value (tombstone) to delete a key.

questions.prompt: Deleting a record from a compacted Kafka topic is done by:
options: Calling `DELETE FROM topic`
Producing a **tombstone** — a record with the same key and a NULL value; the compactor eventually removes both the tombstone and prior values for that key after `delete.retention.ms`
Stopping the producer
Recreating the topic
questions.explanation: Compacted topics use tombstones for deletes: produce `(key, null)`. After `delete.retention.ms` the compactor may purge both the tombstone and prior versions. Consumers see the null and treat it as 'key removed'. Tombstones are how Kafka Streams state stores propagate deletes; tuning `delete.retention.ms` low risks consumers missing the tombstone.

questions.prompt: Which strategies does dbt offer for `materialized = "incremental"` models, and when is each appropriate? (Select all that apply)
options: `append` — fastest, used when source rows are immutable and uniqueness is irrelevant (event logs)
`merge` — default on Snowflake/BigQuery/Databricks with a `unique_key`; upserts by key
`delete+insert` — useful where MERGE is expensive or unsupported; deletes matching keys then inserts
`insert_overwrite` — overwrites whole partitions; idempotent partition-level reprocessing
`truncate_reload` — drops and rebuilds; the only safe option for incremental models
`stream_only` — runs only on streaming engines
questions.explanation: Real dbt incremental strategies: append (immutable events), merge (default upsert), delete+insert (where MERGE is poor), insert_overwrite (partition-level). truncate_reload is not an incremental strategy (it is exactly what incremental avoids). stream_only is not a thing. Pick by warehouse cost model, key availability, and partition shape.

questions.prompt: A Trino query joining a 5TB fact to a 50MB dim is slow because the planner does a partitioned hash join, shuffling the fact. The fix is typically:
options: Reduce the fact table size

Hint or rely on auto-selection of a **broadcast** join for the small side (`/*+ broadcast(d) */` or session `join_distribution_type = 'BROADCAST'`) so the dim is replicated to workers and the fact stays put
Use a different SQL dialect
Cluster the fact more
questions.explanation: Trino defaults can be conservative; explicit broadcast hints or session knobs help when statistics are stale. For multi-fact joins, partitioned join can still win if fact-fact join keys have good locality. Always check the EXPLAIN plan: a 5TB shuffle for a 50MB dim is the classic 'forgot to broadcast' tell.

questions.prompt: Snowflake's **automatic clustering** vs manually defining a clustering key is best characterized as:
options: They are the same

Manual clustering keys + automatic clustering = Snowflake background-re-clusters micro-partitions to satisfy your declared key, charging credit-hours for the work; without a clustering key, Snowflake clusters by load order only — fine for time-series but suboptimal for queries with non-load-order predicates
Manual clustering disables compression
Automatic clustering requires a special warehouse type
questions.explanation: Snowflake's micro-partitions are clustered by load order out of the box. You define a clustering key (CLUSTER BY) and Snowflake reorganizes micro-partitions asynchronously to maintain it — invisible to readers but billed. Worth it for very large tables with predictable filter patterns; overkill for tables that auto-cluster well by load order.

questions.prompt: BigQuery **slot-based pricing** vs **on-demand** is most often preferred when:
options: You have unpredictable, ad-hoc workloads with very low monthly TB scanned
Workloads are large and continuous (you'd otherwise pay TB/scanned every day), and predictable enough that you can size reservations to cap cost — trading off elasticity for cost predictability
You only run point queries
You only run DDL
questions.explanation: On-demand: pay per TB scanned — great for spiky, low-volume workloads. Slots: pay for compute capacity by the hour — great for heavy steady workloads where TB-scanned costs would exceed reservation costs. Modern BigQuery editions add autoscaling slot reservations to ease that boundary.

questions.prompt: Adding a new column with default `NULL` to an Iceberg table is safe, but adding it to a **Parquet-only** Hive table can silently break older queries because:
options: Parquet doesn't support new columns

Hive's schema-on-read against Parquet doesn't reconcile the table-level schema vs each file's footer schema by default — readers querying older files see no column or fail, depending on engine and `parquet.column.index.access` settings
Hive caches schemas forever
Default values are not allowed in Hive
questions.explanation: Iceberg tracks schema-evolution rules per-column with stable IDs in its metadata, so reading an old file with a new column is well-defined (the missing column returns null). Plain Hive/Parquet relies on the engine to match name- or position-based and the behavior across files of different schemas is brittle, especially with `MAP`/`STRUCT` evolution.

questions.prompt: Type-widening an `INT32` column to `INT64` in a Parquet/Iceberg table is:
options: Always safe

Safe under Iceberg's promotion rules (int→long, float→double, decimal precision-up) because old data still reads correctly; narrowing (long→int) is unsafe and rejected by spec-compliant implementations
Always unsafe
Requires rewriting all data
questions.explanation: Iceberg defines a small whitelist of safe type promotions: int→long, float→double, decimal(P,S)→decimal(P',S) with P'≥P. These let old files be read with the new schema without rewriting bytes. Narrowing risks data loss and is forbidden. Cross-family conversions (int→string) are explicitly out of scope.

questions.prompt: **OpenLineage** standardizes lineage emission across tools by:
options: Replacing dbt with a different framework

Defining a vendor-neutral JSON event schema (run / job / dataset, input/output facets) that orchestrators and engines emit during job execution — collectors (Marquez, DataHub, Atlan, etc.) consume these events to build a graph
Encrypting lineage data
Running queries faster
questions.explanation: OpenLineage is a spec + client libraries: Airflow, Spark, dbt, Flink, etc. all emit standardized events about jobs and the datasets they read/write. The collector (Marquez is the reference) assembles the cross-tool graph. Killer feature: a single column-level lineage view across orchestrators without each tool reinventing the wheel.

questions.prompt: A query that ran in 30s for months suddenly takes 30 minutes after a small data increase. Which root causes are commonly responsible for such PLAN FLIPS? (Select all that apply)
options: A small table crossed the broadcast-join threshold and the planner switched to sort-merge / shuffle
Statistics became stale and the planner mis-estimated cardinalities, picking a different join order
A new index that the planner now prefers turns out to be worse for this query
Adaptive execution recompiled the plan based on actual shuffle sizes
Cosmic-ray bit flip in the optimizer code
A skewed key now produces a hot partition that breaks parallelism even on the same plan
questions.explanation: Plan flips at scale: threshold crossings (broadcast -> sort-merge), stale stats, planner choosing a new (worse) index, AQE re-deciding, and skew-induced hot partitions that look like a plan flip but are really a skew problem. Cosmic rays are not on the diagnostic checklist.

questions.prompt: Your service's p50 latency is steady at 5ms but p99 has spiked from 50ms to 500ms. The MOST common cause of a tail-only spike is:
options: The server has more queries
Contention on a shared resource (lock, hot key, GC, single-threaded compactor) that affects only the unlucky requests — p50 is dominated by the common path, p99 captures the slowest tail
A bug in p50 measurement
TLS misconfiguration
questions.explanation: Tail latency reveals resource contention invisible at the median: hot-key lock, GC pause, slow compaction stealing IO, head-of-line blocking on a connection pool, a single overloaded shard. Fix the bottleneck, not the median. Useful tools: per-key flame graphs, async-profiler, percentile-aware tracing, and tail-based sampling to surface the slow traces.

questions.prompt: You must backfill 5 years (~50TB) of corrected data into a partitioned Iceberg fact table. Which approaches MINIMIZE consumer disruption? (Select all that apply)
options: Run the backfill into a parallel branch / staging table; once verified, atomically swap via `ALTER TABLE ... RENAME` or Iceberg branch fast-forward
Backfill partition-by-partition using `INSERT OVERWRITE` with idempotent merge semantics; downstream readers see one corrected partition at a time
Issue UPDATE statements row by row in production
Use Iceberg branches: backfill into a branch, validate, then `fast_forward` main to the branch tag
Truncate the table and rebuild from scratch during business hours
questions.explanation: Safe playbook: write to a parallel destination (shadow table, branch), validate, atomic swap. Partition-by-partition rebuilds work where the downstream is partition-aware. Row-by-row UPDATE on 50TB is operationally hostile. Truncate during business hours violates the 'minimize disruption' premise. Iceberg branches make the swap a metadata-only commit.

questions.prompt: Migrating 100TB across cloud providers with minimal downtime. Which steps belong in the playbook? (Select all that apply)
options: Bulk-seed a consistent snapshot via parallel S3<->GCS copy or appliance (Snowball/Transfer Appliance) — network-bound, dominates initial cost
Stand up ongoing CDC from source to destination once the snapshot is loaded, to keep them in sync
Establish a short dual-write window with row-count and key-aggregate reconciliation before cutting writers over
Plan for egress cost explicitly — at this scale, egress is the dominant migration line item
Take the source offline for the duration of the migration to guarantee consistency
Re-derive all data from upstream sources to avoid migrating at all
questions.explanation: The durable cross-cloud recipe is snapshot + CDC + reconciled dual-write window + cutover, with explicit egress budgeting. Taking the source offline for days is not realistic at 100TB. Re-deriving from upstream is sometimes the right answer but is not a migration strategy — it sidesteps the question.

questions.prompt: Design an **outbox** table in Postgres for the transactional-outbox pattern: a service writes a business row + an outbox row in one transaction; a relay reads the outbox and publishes events to Kafka. Include columns needed for ordered, idempotent publishing and for relay observability.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE outbox (
  outbox_id          BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,                -- monotonic id for ordering & relay cursor
  event_id           UUID         NOT NULL UNIQUE,            -- producer-side idempotency key
  aggregate_type     VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,                   -- e.g. 'order', 'invoice'
  aggregate_id       VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,                   -- business key (used as Kafka key for ordering)
  event_type         VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,                   -- e.g. 'order.placed'
  payload            JSONB        NOT NULL,                   -- the event body
  headers            JSONB,                                   -- correlation / trace ids
  schema_version     SMALLINT     NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
  created_at         TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  -- relay state (or store this in a separate cursor table)
  published_at       TIMESTAMPTZ,
  publish_attempts   INT          NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  last_error         TEXT
);

-- Relay index: cheapest scan of unpublished events in insertion order.
CREATE INDEX ix_outbox_unpublished
  ON outbox (outbox_id)
  WHERE published_at IS NULL;

-- Useful for partition-by-aggregate ordering when fan-out matters.
CREATE INDEX ix_outbox_aggregate ON outbox (aggregate_type, aggregate_id, outbox_id);
key_points: Single BIGSERIAL provides monotonic ordering for the relay cursor
event_id (UUID) is a producer-side idempotency key so re-publish is safe at the consumer / broker
aggregate_id captured for use as the Kafka message key (preserves per-aggregate order downstream)
Payload as JSONB (or schema-bound type) plus schema_version to evolve safely
Relay state columns (published_at, publish_attempts, last_error) for observability and DLQ logic
Partial index on unpublished rows keeps the relay's hot path cheap as the table grows
Same DB transaction inserts the business row AND the outbox row — atomic by construction

questions.prompt: Design a **point-in-time (PIT) feature store** offline table for an ML feature 'user_7d_purchase_count'. The table must support: (a) point-in-time correct joins for training (no leakage), (b) latest-value reads for serving, (c) backfills that don't break already-trained models.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE feature_user_7d_purchase_count (
  user_id          BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- entity key
  feature_value    DOUBLE       NOT NULL,                  -- the feature value
  event_ts         TIMESTAMP    NOT NULL,                  -- when this feature value became true
  ingest_ts        TIMESTAMP    NOT NULL,                  -- when the row was written (audit)
  source_version   VARCHAR(32)  NOT NULL,                  -- pipeline / code version that produced it
  feature_set_ver  VARCHAR(32)  NOT NULL,                  -- feature definition version
  is_backfill      BOOLEAN      NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,    -- distinguishes initial backfill from live updates
  PRIMARY KEY (user_id, event_ts, feature_set_ver)
)
PARTITION BY (event_ts);

-- Point-in-time training join (pseudo-SQL):
-- SELECT label.*, f.feature_value
-- FROM labels label
-- LEFT JOIN feature_user_7d_purchase_count f
--   ON f.user_id = label.user_id
--  AND f.feature_set_ver = 'v3'
--  AND f.event_ts <= label.label_ts
-- QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY label.row_id ORDER BY f.event_ts DESC) = 1;
key_points: Stores (entity, feature_value, event_ts) so PIT joins use `event_ts <= label_ts` to prevent leakage
Latest-value read for serving = ORDER BY event_ts DESC LIMIT 1 per user_id
feature_set_ver in the key allows multiple versions of the feature definition to coexist (don't invalidate old models)
source_version + ingest_ts capture pipeline lineage for debugging
is_backfill flag distinguishes initial bulk loads from live-published values (handy for excluding from drift monitors)
Partition by event_ts for time-bounded backfills and retention
Composite PK ensures idempotent re-publish per (entity, event_ts, version)

questions.prompt: Design an event-sourced **events** table for an order aggregate. Include columns required for: ordered replay per aggregate, optimistic concurrency, snapshot pairing, and idempotent producer-side writes.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE order_events (
  aggregate_id      UUID         NOT NULL,
  sequence_no       BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- monotonic per aggregate (1, 2, 3, ...)
  event_id          UUID         NOT NULL,                  -- idempotency key (one row per event_id)
  event_type        VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,                  -- e.g. 'OrderPlaced', 'ItemAdded'
  event_version     SMALLINT     NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,        -- event schema version
  payload           JSONB        NOT NULL,                  -- the event body
  metadata          JSONB,                                  -- correlation id, causation id, user id
  occurred_at       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,                  -- wall-clock event time
  recorded_at       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),    -- ingest time
  PRIMARY KEY (aggregate_id, sequence_no),
  UNIQUE (event_id)
);

CREATE INDEX ix_order_events_by_recorded_at
  ON order_events (recorded_at);

-- Companion snapshot table
CREATE TABLE order_snapshots (
  aggregate_id     UUID        NOT NULL,
  sequence_no      BIGINT      NOT NULL,                    -- snapshot taken AT this sequence_no
  state            JSONB       NOT NULL,
  state_version    SMALLINT    NOT NULL,
  created_at       TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  PRIMARY KEY (aggregate_id, sequence_no)
);
key_points: (aggregate_id, sequence_no) primary key gives strict ordered replay per aggregate
Sequence_no enables optimistic concurrency: writer asserts 'last seen N' and INSERT fails if another writer already wrote N+1
UNIQUE on event_id makes idempotent producer retries safe (no duplicate events)
event_version + payload version pattern supports schema evolution per event type
Companion snapshot table keyed by (aggregate_id, sequence_no) lets replay start from the snapshot and apply tail events
metadata column carries correlation/causation ids for distributed tracing across aggregates
Separates occurred_at (domain time) from recorded_at (ingest time) — essential for late events and audits

questions.prompt: Design a **late-arriving dimension** placeholder pattern: a dimension `dim_product` that supports facts referencing products that haven't yet been registered in the source. Include the columns needed to identify, reconcile, and audit such placeholder rows.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE dim_product (
  product_sk        BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- surrogate PK
  product_nk        VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,                  -- natural key from source
  product_name      VARCHAR(200),
  category          VARCHAR(100),
  brand             VARCHAR(100),
  price             DECIMAL(10,2),
  -- placeholder mechanics
  is_placeholder    BOOLEAN      NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,    -- TRUE when fact arrived before dim
  placeholder_seen_at TIMESTAMP,                            -- first time a fact referenced this NK
  reconciled_at     TIMESTAMP,                              -- when real source row backfilled the attributes
  reconciled_by     VARCHAR(64),                            -- pipeline run id
  -- audit
  effective_from    TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  effective_to      TIMESTAMP,                              -- NULL for current
  is_current        BOOLEAN   NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
  ingested_at       TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  PRIMARY KEY (product_sk),
  UNIQUE (product_nk, effective_from)
);

-- A reserved 'Unknown' row for facts whose NK is genuinely absent.
INSERT INTO dim_product (product_sk, product_nk, product_name, is_placeholder, effective_from, is_current)
VALUES (-1, '__UNKNOWN__', 'Unknown Product', TRUE, '1970-01-01', TRUE);
key_points: is_placeholder flag distinguishes rows created to hold a fact reference vs fully-attributed source rows
placeholder_seen_at and reconciled_at capture the time-to-reconcile window for SLOs
reconciled_by ties the reconciliation back to the pipeline run for audit
Reserved -1 'Unknown' row absorbs facts whose NK is null or genuinely unknown
effective_from/effective_to and is_current support SCD2-style versioning so the row evolves from placeholder → real
UNIQUE (product_nk, effective_from) prevents double-creating a placeholder for the same key
Lineage column (ingested_at) for debugging

questions.prompt: Design the **table-format metadata schema** that the question (not Iceberg itself, your own simplified version) would need to support snapshot isolation and time travel over a set of Parquet data files in object storage. Provide CREATE statements for: snapshots, manifest entries, and data file entries. Storage engine is Postgres acting as the metastore.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE table_snapshot (
  table_id          BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  snapshot_id       BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- monotonic per table
  parent_snapshot_id BIGINT,                                -- nullable for the first snapshot
  committed_at      TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  committed_by      VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
  summary           JSONB,                                  -- e.g. files-added, rows-added
  schema_id         INT          NOT NULL,                  -- references a schema-version table
  partition_spec_id INT          NOT NULL,                  -- references a partition-spec-version table
  PRIMARY KEY (table_id, snapshot_id),
  FOREIGN KEY (table_id, parent_snapshot_id) REFERENCES table_snapshot (table_id, snapshot_id)
);

CREATE TABLE table_manifest_entry (
  table_id          BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  snapshot_id       BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- which snapshot owns this entry
  data_file_id      BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- references table_data_file
  status            SMALLINT     NOT NULL,                  -- 0 = EXISTING, 1 = ADDED, 2 = DELETED
  added_in_snapshot BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  deleted_in_snapshot BIGINT,
  PRIMARY KEY (table_id, snapshot_id, data_file_id),
  FOREIGN KEY (table_id, snapshot_id) REFERENCES table_snapshot (table_id, snapshot_id),
  FOREIGN KEY (data_file_id) REFERENCES table_data_file (data_file_id)
);

CREATE TABLE table_data_file (
  data_file_id      BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  table_id          BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  file_path         TEXT         NOT NULL,                  -- s3://... or hdfs://...
  file_size_bytes   BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  row_count         BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  partition_values  JSONB        NOT NULL,                  -- e.g. {"event_date":"2025-05-12"}
  column_stats      JSONB        NOT NULL,                  -- per-column min/max/null counts
  created_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE INDEX ix_manifest_by_table_snapshot ON table_manifest_entry (table_id, snapshot_id);
key_points: Snapshots are chained via parent_snapshot_id, enabling time travel and rollback
Manifest entry has status (EXISTING/ADDED/DELETED) so deletes are recorded without rewriting files — essential for transactional semantics over immutable object storage
Data file rows store partition values (drives partition pruning) and per-column stats (drives predicate pushdown)
Schema-version and partition-spec-version IDs on the snapshot support schema and partition evolution
committed_at + committed_by + summary capture audit metadata
FKs maintain referential integrity across snapshots and data files
Bonus: explicit index on manifest entries by (table_id, snapshot_id) for fast plan-time enumeration

questions.prompt: Design a Postgres **idempotency key** table to back an HTTP API that creates payments. The table stores the first response so retries return the same body without re-executing the side effect. Include TTL housekeeping.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE idempotency_key (
  api_path           VARCHAR(128) NOT NULL,                  -- scope keys per endpoint
  idempotency_key    VARCHAR(128) NOT NULL,                  -- client-supplied
  request_hash       CHAR(64)     NOT NULL,                  -- hash of the request body
  status             SMALLINT     NOT NULL,                  -- 0 = in-flight, 1 = completed
  response_code      SMALLINT,                                -- HTTP status of stored response
  response_body      JSONB,
  response_headers   JSONB,
  created_at         TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  completed_at       TIMESTAMPTZ,
  expires_at         TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,                   -- TTL (e.g. created_at + 24h)
  PRIMARY KEY (api_path, idempotency_key)
);
