CREATE INDEX ix_idempotency_expires ON idempotency_key (expires_at);

-- Daily housekeeping:
-- DELETE FROM idempotency_key WHERE expires_at < now();
key_points: Composite PK (api_path, idempotency_key) so the same key is allowed for different endpoints
request_hash lets the server detect 'same key, different body' — a 409 / 422 condition
status flag distinguishes in-flight vs completed so concurrent retries can wait/serve the cached response
Stored response_code / body / headers let the server return the exact original response on replay
expires_at + index supports TTL housekeeping; 24h-7d typical
Created/completed timestamps for audit and to compute upstream latency
Bonus: explicit advisory lock or row-level lock on the PK during the first execution to coalesce concurrent first-attempts

questions.prompt: Design a **time-bucketed metrics** table for storing per-service request counts + latency percentiles at 1-minute granularity, partitioned for retention. Optimized for last-N-minutes dashboard reads and ~30-day retention.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE metric_request (
  service_name      VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
  endpoint          VARCHAR(128) NOT NULL,
  bucket_ts         TIMESTAMP    NOT NULL,                  -- floor(event_ts, '1 minute')
  request_count     BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  error_count       BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  p50_ms            DOUBLE,
  p95_ms            DOUBLE,
  p99_ms            DOUBLE,
  -- (optionally a t-digest blob for re-aggregation across buckets)
  td_digest         BYTEA,
  ingested_at       TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  PRIMARY KEY (service_name, endpoint, bucket_ts)
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (bucket_ts);

-- Daily partitions, easy retention via DROP PARTITION.
-- CREATE TABLE metric_request_20250512 PARTITION OF metric_request
--   FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-05-12') TO ('2025-05-13');
key_points: Pre-aggregated grain (1 minute per service-endpoint) keeps storage and read cost predictable
Composite PK ensures idempotent re-aggregation by bucket_ts
Stores p50/p95/p99 as final values for dashboard reads, plus a t-digest blob so percentile values can be RE-aggregated across buckets (averaging p99s is statistically wrong)
Range partitioning on bucket_ts enables fast retention drops and partition pruning for last-N-minutes queries
Daily partitions are a good default at this volume; finer for very high cardinality, coarser for low
Lineage column (ingested_at)
Bonus: a clustering or secondary index on service_name for service-scoped dashboard reads

questions.prompt: Design a **multi-tenant SaaS** table for customer support tickets. Must support: tenant isolation (row-level), per-tenant retention policies, and efficient queries scoped to a single tenant.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE support_ticket (
  tenant_id         BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- first column on every index for isolation
  ticket_id         BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- unique within tenant
  created_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,
  updated_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,
  status            VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,                  -- open / pending / resolved / closed
  priority          SMALLINT     NOT NULL,
  assigned_user_id  BIGINT,
  subject           VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
  body              TEXT,
  retention_until   DATE,                                    -- per-tenant policy result
  PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id, ticket_id)
)
PARTITION BY HASH (tenant_id);  -- spreads tenants across partitions to avoid one giant partition

CREATE INDEX ix_ticket_open ON support_ticket (tenant_id, status, created_at DESC)
  WHERE status IN ('open', 'pending');

-- Postgres RLS:
ALTER TABLE support_ticket ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON support_ticket
  USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::BIGINT);
key_points: tenant_id is the leading column in the PK and every index — every query plan starts by pruning to one tenant
Hash partitioning by tenant_id prevents an elephant tenant from monopolizing one partition while still allowing tenant-scoped queries
Partial index on (tenant_id, status, created_at) for the hot 'my open tickets' UI path
retention_until column captures the per-tenant retention deadline (driven by tenant's contracted policy)
RLS policy enforces isolation at the database, not just the application — defense in depth
Composite PK (tenant_id, ticket_id) lets ticket_id restart per tenant (vendor flexibility)
Bonus: a per-tenant retention sweeper that DELETEs (or VACUUMs to cold) rows past retention_until

questions.prompt: Design a **graph edge** table for a social graph (users follow users) in Postgres, optimized for both directions of traversal (followers of X, followees of X), bulk export to a graph DB, and idempotent upserts under heavy concurrency.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE follow_edge (
  follower_id     BIGINT       NOT NULL,                    -- the one doing the following
  followee_id     BIGINT       NOT NULL,                    -- the one being followed
  created_at      TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  source          VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL DEFAULT 'app',      -- audit: app / import / api
  PRIMARY KEY (follower_id, followee_id),
  CHECK (follower_id <> followee_id)                         -- no self-follows
);

-- Reverse-direction lookup: followers of X.
CREATE INDEX ix_follow_by_followee
  ON follow_edge (followee_id, follower_id);

-- Idempotent upsert:
-- INSERT INTO follow_edge (follower_id, followee_id, source)
-- VALUES ($1, $2, $3)
-- ON CONFLICT (follower_id, followee_id) DO NOTHING;
key_points: Composite PK (follower_id, followee_id) covers one direction of traversal and gives natural uniqueness
Reverse index on (followee_id, follower_id) covers the other direction — both lookups are index-only
CHECK constraint forbids self-follows (a common dirty-data source)
ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING makes follow idempotent under retries / dedup
created_at + source columns capture audit metadata for compliance / abuse review
Bonus: a separate `follow_event` append-only log captures unfollows for analytics without storing them in the live edge table
Bonus: bulk export query is a simple SELECT * with offset/limit pagination by (follower_id, followee_id)

questions.prompt: Design a **changelog (CDC sink)** table that lands Debezium-style change events into a Postgres staging schema before silver-layer MERGE. Must capture before/after images, source LSN, and op type, with idempotency on replay.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE staging_orders_cdc (
  source_db          VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
  source_table       VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
  source_lsn         NUMERIC(20)  NOT NULL,                  -- e.g. Postgres LSN or MySQL binlog file+pos as integer
  source_txid        BIGINT,
  op                 CHAR(1)      NOT NULL,                  -- c = create, u = update, d = delete, r = read (initial snapshot)
  source_ts_ms       BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- event time at source
  ingest_ts          TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  primary_key        JSONB        NOT NULL,                  -- e.g. {"order_id": 1234}
  before_image       JSONB,                                  -- null for c, present for u/d
  after_image        JSONB,                                  -- null for d, present for c/u
  schema_version     INT          NOT NULL,                  -- Debezium schema id
  PRIMARY KEY (source_db, source_table, source_lsn, primary_key)
);

CREATE INDEX ix_staging_orders_cdc_pk
  ON staging_orders_cdc USING GIN (primary_key);
key_points: PK includes source_lsn so re-publish of the same change is idempotent (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING)
Stores both before_image (pre-update / pre-delete) and after_image (post-create / post-update) — needed to compute deltas and to apply deletes downstream
op column distinguishes create / update / delete / initial-read so the silver MERGE can branch correctly
source_ts_ms separates event time from ingest_ts (lag analysis)
JSONB primary_key supports tables with composite PKs without table-specific staging schemas
schema_version captures Debezium's schema id so payload shape changes are reconcilable
GIN index on primary_key supports key-scoped lookups during reconciliation

questions.prompt: You operate a 30TB Snowflake warehouse with monthly compute spend of $200K and rising. The CFO asks for a 40% reduction in three months without breaking dashboards. Describe the **FinOps playbook** you'd execute, the order of operations, and the tradeoffs of each lever.
questions.model_answer:
**Phase 1 — Attribute (week 1–2).** You can't cut what you can't see. Stand up cost attribution: per-team virtual warehouses with query tags propagated from orchestration (`query_tag = team:growth, dag:fact_orders, env:prod`). Join Snowflake `query_history` to job metadata in a `fct_warehouse_usage` model. End state: per-team / per-dag / per-mart monthly cost rollups in a dashboard that engineers actually look at.

**Phase 2 — Quick wins (weeks 2–4).** Without changing any models:
- **AUTO_SUSPEND** to 60s on ad-hoc warehouses (10-30% saving on bursty workloads).
- **AUTO_RESUME on**, and consolidate one-off warehouses where they don't conflict.
- **Result caching** — ensure session-level result reuse is on; identify dashboards rebuilding identical results minute-after-minute and route them through a layer that respects 1–10 min result cache TTL.
- **Right-size warehouses** — a workload running on XL for 90 seconds may run on L for 110 seconds at half the credit-rate; XS for short ad-hoc.
- **Identify and kill the top-10 cost queries.** Almost always 80% of cost is 20% of queries; many are scheduled but unused, or scanning a 5TB table when a 30GB aggregate would suffice.

**Phase 3 — Modeling changes (weeks 4–8).**
- **Materialize hot aggregates** that consumers re-derive on every query: dbt incremental models or Snowflake materialized views for the top-N consumed marts. Compute the rebuild-vs-read ratio first.
- **Partition + cluster** the largest fact tables on the columns the query history shows are filtered most often — measure the pruning improvement; if it's <30%, the cluster key is wrong.
- **Migrate cold partitions** to lower retention or to a Snowflake external table backed by S3 — trades query latency for storage cost (mostly storage, since compute is the dominant line).
- **Search optimization service / clustering** on point-lookup tables only if the workload justifies it.

**Phase 4 — Governance (ongoing).**
- **Cost budgets per team** with auto-alerts on burn-rate.
- **CI checks on PRs** that estimate the cost of new dbt models against a sample to avoid PR-time regressions.
- **Quarterly mart audits** — deprecate models with zero downstream usage in 30 days.
- **Reserve discount / committed-use** for the steady-state baseline once the new floor is established (only AFTER cuts — don't lock in a high baseline).

**Tradeoffs:**
- Quick wins are low-risk but bounded — typically 15–25% saving without code changes.
- Modeling changes save the next 15–20% but require coordination with consumers; communicate via deprecation windows, not surprise breakage.
- Cold tiering trades latency for cost — sell it to consumers with concrete latency numbers, not vague 'archive' talk.
- Reservation/committed discounts are only safe AFTER the new steady state is measured; locking in early defeats the purpose.
- Don't disable auto-suspend or merge teams' warehouses if it creates contention — the savings can be undone by spillage and slowness that drives engineers to ask for bigger warehouses.

**Expected outcome.** A disciplined sequence usually finds 40–55% savings within a quarter on a warehouse that hasn't had a cost review in 12+ months. The hardest part is sustaining the new baseline: without governance, costs creep back within two quarters.
key_points: Starts with cost ATTRIBUTION (per-team warehouses, query tags, usage joined to job metadata) — 'measure first' framing
Sequences quick wins (auto-suspend, result caching, right-sizing, top-10 query elimination) before model changes
Calls out materializing hot aggregates with explicit rebuild-vs-read math, not gut feel
Mentions partition / cluster verification by measuring pruning improvement, not assumption
Discusses cold tiering and its latency tradeoff explicitly
Mentions governance / budgets / PR-time cost CI to prevent regressions
Warns against locking in reserved capacity before the new floor is established
Discusses tradeoffs of each lever (low-risk vs high-coordination, latency vs cost)
Sets a realistic outcome expectation (40–55% is achievable on a stale warehouse)
Communicates a sustaining mechanism so the savings don't regress

questions.prompt: A daily dbt run feeding the executive KPI dashboard occasionally **flips a query plan** at scale: most days `fact_sales` is joined to `dim_customer` via broadcast; once a week, post-deduplication, the dim crosses a threshold and the plan switches to sort-merge, blowing the runtime from 5 minutes to 90 minutes and missing the morning SLA. Describe how you'd diagnose, mitigate short-term, and fix root-cause.
questions.model_answer:
**Diagnose first.**
1. Confirm: pull `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` (or warehouse equivalent: Snowflake `QUERY_HISTORY` + `EXPLAIN`, BigQuery `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT`, Spark UI for Databricks). Compare a 5-minute run plan vs a 90-minute run plan side by side. You expect the join operator to differ (BroadcastHashJoin → SortMergeJoin) along with the input estimates.
2. Identify the *exact* trigger. Is `dim_customer` actually crossing the broadcast threshold (default ~10MB Spark, configurable; auto in Snowflake), or are stats stale so the planner *thinks* it crossed? Run the EXPLAIN on a small reproduction with the actual dim sizes to confirm.
3. Check whether the dim's de-dup step has a SKEW: the runs that flip might also produce a hot key that breaks the broadcast hash join even if it had been chosen.

**Short-term mitigation (same week).**
- **Force the join strategy** via a hint: in Spark / Databricks, `BROADCAST(d)` hint; in BigQuery, write the join shape as a CTE that the planner is more likely to broadcast; in Snowflake, the optimizer typically gets this right — if it doesn't, pin the warehouse size up one notch for that step.
- **Stabilize the input size** by writing the dim to a small materialized table BEFORE the join, with explicit `OPTIMIZE` / `ANALYZE TABLE COMPUTE STATISTICS` to refresh stats. Now the planner sees deterministic size.
- **Add a CI check** on the dim's row count vs a baseline so a 30% week-over-week spike triggers an alert before the morning run.
- **Move the run earlier** to give more recovery time if the SLA is at risk, while root-cause is being fixed.

**Root-cause fix.**
The underlying issue is a brittle threshold-based plan. Three durable fixes:
1. **Make the dim's size deterministic.** If it's drifting because de-dup grouping changes, the right fix is upstream: stabilize the dedup logic so the dim doesn't accidentally retain duplicates one week and not the next. Use a hash diff and an SCD2 approach so the dim doesn't fluctuate.
2. **Architect around the threshold.** Either ensure the dim stays comfortably below the broadcast limit forever (split into sub-dims; or build a curated 'broadcastable' projection), or commit to a sort-merge plan and *tune for it* with proper partitioning of the fact by `customer_sk_bucket`. Whichever side of the threshold you choose, you choose deterministically.
3. **Adopt adaptive execution where possible.** Spark AQE (`spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true`) re-evaluates the plan at runtime based on actual shuffle sizes, often catching the flip without manual hints. It's not a silver bullet — it doesn't help if the warehouse engine doesn't expose AQE (older versions, some non-Spark engines) and it adds startup cost — but it's the modern default for this exact class of bug.

**Tradeoffs.**
- Hints are precise but brittle: someone refactors the model, the hint goes stale, and the bug returns silently. Document and lint for them.
- A 'broadcastable' projection (mini-dim of just the join keys + necessary columns) is the cleanest long-term answer if the full dim is genuinely too big.
- Forcing sort-merge requires you to ALSO repartition / bucket the fact correctly. Doing one without the other is worse than letting the planner choose.
- AQE is great but adds variance in execution time across runs; make sure your SLA dashboards plot the right metric.

**Preventive measures.**
- Pin warehouse / cluster size for the SLA-bearing DAG so day-to-day capacity variation doesn't compound the plan flip.
- Snapshot the chosen plan in CI for the top-N most expensive queries; alert when the plan signature changes.
- Treat the dim row count, dedup yield, and post-join row count as data SLOs and monitor week-over-week.
key_points: Starts with diagnosis: comparing EXPLAIN plans across 'good' and 'bad' runs
Identifies the trigger as a threshold crossing (broadcast → sort-merge) and considers stale stats as a confounder
Considers skew as a secondary trigger that could break the broadcast plan independently
Short-term mitigations: explicit join hints, materializing a stabilized dim, refreshing stats, CI alarms on size drift
Root cause: make the dim size deterministic upstream (dedup logic, SCD2 stability)
Architectural alternative: commit to one side of the threshold and tune for it (broadcastable projection OR proper partitioning for sort-merge)
Mentions adaptive execution (AQE) as a modern mitigation and notes its limits
Discusses hint brittleness and the need for documentation / lint
Adds preventive measures: pinned capacity, plan-signature CI, data SLOs on the dim
Demonstrates ownership end-to-end: triage → patch → root cause → prevention

questions.prompt: You inherit a 3-year-old data platform: nightly batch into a Redshift warehouse, hand-built Airflow DAGs, no schema registry, ad-hoc PII handling, dashboards on stale data. Leadership wants 'a modern data platform' in six months with stricter governance and 1-hour data freshness for the top-20 dashboards. Lay out the **migration strategy** — sequencing, what to leave alone, what to replace, and how you measure success.
questions.model_answer:
**Anchor: the strategy is iterative replacement, not a parallel rebuild.** Promise no big-bang migrations — they fail in this kind of environment because tribal knowledge is concentrated in the existing DAGs.

**Month 0 — Discovery (2 weeks).**
- Inventory the top-20 dashboards (the only ones with the 1-hour SLA). Trace each to upstream sources. Most will share a small core of facts and dims — that core is your migration target.
- Inventory PII columns and current handling. Tag them, even if the handling is wrong today.
- Establish an attribution / observability baseline: per-DAG run times, per-table freshness, dashboard load times. Without this you can't prove improvement.

**Month 1 — Foundations.**
- **Ingestion.** Introduce CDC for the top 5 source systems (Debezium / DMS) writing to Kafka. Keep the legacy batch ingestion for everything else — don't fight battles outside the top-20.
- **Storage.** Stand up an S3 + Iceberg lakehouse alongside Redshift. Don't migrate data yet; just create the bronze landing zone.
- **Catalog + lineage.** Adopt a catalog (DataHub / Atlan / Unity Catalog) and emit OpenLineage from Airflow. This is the spine of governance; the value compounds with every model added.
- **Schema registry.** Run a Confluent / equivalent registry. Mandate it for all NEW topics; legacy producers grandfathered.

**Month 2–3 — Migrate the core marts.**
- For each of the top-20 dashboards:
  1. Identify the underlying mart (often only 3–5 distinct ones).
  2. Rebuild the mart in dbt against the lakehouse (silver → gold).
  3. Add dbt tests (uniqueness, not-null, freshness, accepted_values) for each model.
  4. Backfill the gold table in the lakehouse to match Redshift; reconcile row counts and key aggregates.
  5. Cut the dashboard's data source over with a 2-week dual-write/dual-read overlap.
- The 1-hour SLA is achieved by CDC + streaming silver + scheduled (5-15 min cadence) gold incremental refresh — not by full streaming everywhere.

**Month 4 — Governance.**
- Centralize PII tagging in the catalog and propagate via column-level lineage.
- Implement column masking / row-level security at the gold layer for sensitive marts. Tokenize at the silver layer for the most sensitive fields.
- Stand up a DSR (data subject request) workflow that uses the tags + lineage to identify affected tables.
- Define data contracts for the top 5 source systems with their owners; back them with CI checks on the producer side.

**Month 5 — Observability + SLAs.**
- Freshness, completeness, schema-stability, job-success SLOs on every gold table with alerts.
- End-to-end p99 latency tracking from source-event to dashboard-row-visible.
- Cost attribution dashboard live; per-team budgets in place.
- A runbook + on-call rotation for the platform team — governance only works if someone is paged for SLO breaches.

**Month 6 — Sunset path.**
- Decommission migrated dashboards' Redshift dependencies. Don't shut Redshift down — it still serves the long tail of internal queries.
- Publish a deprecation timeline for the remaining Redshift marts (6–12 months out) with a clear migration template (the same one used for the top-20).
- Reserve a small budget line for ongoing governance: catalog curation, contract reviews, dbt test coverage targets.

**What to leave alone:**
- The long tail of low-priority dashboards on Redshift — they cost little and pay no SLA. They migrate opportunistically.
- Source-system schemas — don't ask source teams to refactor for the new platform; the contracts and CDC absorb the impedance.
- The existing Airflow scheduling layer where it works — add OpenLineage emission, but don't replace the orchestrator just to replace it.

**Success metrics (measured against the Month 0 baseline):**
- 1-hour freshness achieved for ≥ 18/20 top dashboards by end of Month 5.
- 90% of new gold tables have dbt tests; 95% have column-level lineage; 100% have PII tagging.
- Mean dashboard p99 load time reduced by ≥ 30%.
- Zero unhandled DSRs older than 30 days.
- Cost per gold table refresh tracked, and a 20% cost reduction on the top-10 most expensive models.

**Tradeoffs to call out explicitly:**
- The CDC path costs more in steady-state ($/month) than nightly batch but delivers the SLA leadership asked for. Frame it as 'this is what 1-hour freshness costs'.
- Two warehouses in parallel for 6 months is overhead. Cap the dual-run window per dashboard at 2–4 weeks.
- Building the catalog/governance before showing dashboard wins makes leadership nervous. Sequence so a small set of migrated dashboards is visible by end of Month 2.
key_points: Frames the migration as iterative replacement, not a big-bang rewrite
Starts with inventory + observability baseline so improvement is measurable
Sequences foundations (CDC, lakehouse, catalog, registry) before model migration
Scopes the 1-hour SLA to the top-20 dashboards rather than promising platform-wide streaming
Migrates marts in waves, each with reconciliation + dual-read overlap
Addresses governance (PII tagging, column-level lineage, RLS, DSR workflow) as a distinct phase, not afterthought
Decides explicitly what to LEAVE ALONE (long tail, source schemas, existing scheduler)
Defines concrete success metrics tied to the Month 0 baseline (numbers, not adjectives)
Calls out cost / dual-run / political tradeoffs honestly
Plans a sustaining phase (deprecation timeline, on-call, governance budget) so the platform doesn't regress in year 2

questions.prompt: A team proposes a Lambda architecture (separate batch + streaming pipelines that converge in a serving layer). The strongest argument FOR Kappa (single streaming pipeline, replays for backfill) instead, in 2026, is:
options: Streaming engines are slower than batch

Modern table formats (Iceberg/Delta) and stream-batch unification in engines (Flink, Spark Structured Streaming, Databricks DLT) make a single pipeline with replay capability achievable — eliminating the two-code-path maintenance burden of Lambda, with backfills served by replaying the log over the same operators
Kappa doesn't require any storage
Lambda was deprecated by the cloud vendors
questions.explanation: Lambda's original justification — batch was the only way to do correct large-scale processing, streaming for low-latency approximations — has eroded as streaming engines gained windowing, exactly-once, and lakehouse storage gained ACID + replay. Kappa-style pipelines now deliver both freshness and correctness from one codebase. Lambda still wins when your batch toolchain is irreplaceable or replay storage costs are prohibitive; otherwise the maintenance savings of one code path are compelling.

questions.prompt: Stripe's checkout team is rolling out a new payment method. Latency tests show the p99 of `create_charge` jumps from 120ms to 800ms whenever they add a single LEFT JOIN to the `disputes` table. EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows the planner switched from a hash join to a nested loop because it estimates the disputes table at 12 rows when it actually has 4M. Which change is MOST LIKELY to fix p99 without changing the query semantics?
options: Rewrite the query as two separate statements and join in application code
Run ANALYZE / refresh statistics on the disputes table so the planner uses the correct row estimate
Add a covering index that includes all SELECTed columns from disputes
Switch the database isolation level from READ COMMITTED to REPEATABLE READ
questions.explanation: The planner picked a nested loop because its cardinality estimate (12 rows) is wildly wrong. Stale statistics is the dominant cause of plan flips after a table grows; refreshing them lets the optimizer pick the hash join again. A covering index helps but doesn't address the wrong join algorithm; splitting the query moves logic without fixing the underlying estimate; isolation level is unrelated to plan choice.

questions.prompt: Your B2B SaaS has 4,800 customer tenants. Customer 'Acme Corp' generates 78% of total event volume (1.2B events/day) while every other tenant generates <0.1%. Your Snowflake warehouse hits credit cap every Tuesday around 14:00 UTC because Acme runs heavy ad-hoc queries during their business hours. What design change addresses this LEAST disruptively?
options: Move all 4,800 tenants to their own dedicated warehouses
Provision a dedicated virtual warehouse for Acme and route their queries to it, leaving the shared warehouse for everyone else
Force Acme to query only between 02:00 and 06:00 UTC via a query queue with hard rate limits
Drop event-level grain and persist only hourly aggregates so Acme's queries return faster
questions.explanation: Carving Acme onto a dedicated warehouse is the canonical 'noisy neighbor' isolation pattern in Snowflake: zero migration, zero schema change, and the rest of the fleet stops contending for their workload. Per-tenant warehouses for all 4,800 explodes operational cost; rate-limiting an enterprise customer to nights breaks the value prop; dropping grain loses analytical flexibility for everyone.

questions.prompt: DoorDash needs to compute dasher pay each Friday based on Mon-Sun delivery events. Late events trickle in for ~36 hours after a delivery (signal upload, in-app tip edits, customer rating finalization). What's the safest pattern to ensure pay is correct AND processed by Friday noon?
options: Run the pay job at Friday 00:01 right after the week ends, accepting whatever events have arrived
Hold pay calculation until Friday 11:00 (giving 35+ hours of late-event grace), then freeze the window and pay; reconcile any later corrections via a separate retro-pay batch
Stream pay updates continuously and let the wallet be eventually consistent until Friday noon
Re-run the pay job hourly until Friday noon, overwriting prior runs
questions.explanation: Real payroll pipelines almost always use a cut-off pattern: wait for the watermark / late-data grace period to elapse, freeze the window, pay, and reconcile rare straggler corrections in a follow-up cycle. Paying immediately at 00:01 misses most adjustments; streaming pay creates ledger churn and user confusion; hourly overwrites cause unpredictable amounts on consumers' apps.

questions.prompt: Your Snowflake bill doubled overnight. Yesterday it was $1.2K, today it's $2.6K and climbing. Nothing in the dbt repo changed. Where do you look FIRST?
options: Audit recent dbt model changes in the last 30 days
Open `snowflake.account_usage.query_history` and group by `warehouse_name, user_name, query_tag` for the last 24h to find the new top consumer
Lower AUTO_SUSPEND on every warehouse to 60s
Open a Snowflake support ticket
questions.explanation: The fastest diagnostic for a sudden cost spike is to surface the new top-cost queries/users in the last 24h via account_usage. Cost spikes almost always trace to a single newly-deployed query, a runaway notebook, or a new BI dashboard hitting a non-clustered table. Auditing dbt models won't catch BI/ad-hoc usage; blindly lowering AUTO_SUSPEND across the board may not target the actual cause.

questions.prompt: A fintech's payment API receives a retry from a merchant 2 seconds after the first request (network blip). The original request is still in-flight (DB write committed, response not yet sent). The merchant uses the same `Idempotency-Key` header. What should the server do?
options: Reject the second request with HTTP 429 and let the client retry later
Process the second request as new — the client never got a response
Detect the in-flight key via a row lock or status flag and either block-and-return-the-eventual-response, or return 409 Conflict so the client backs off
Return HTTP 200 with an empty body since the original is still processing
questions.explanation: Stripe-style idempotency uses an 'in-flight' status: the first request takes a row-level lock on the idempotency key; the second request finds the in-flight marker and either waits for the first to complete (then returns the cached response) or returns 409 to signal the client to back off. Returning 429 wrongly tells the client to retry. Processing as new double-charges. Returning empty 200 silently corrupts the merchant's view.

questions.prompt: Your ingestion lag jumped from 5s to 8h overnight. Kafka producer rates look normal, consumer lag is climbing linearly. CPU on consumer pods is 15%. Network throughput from Kafka is well below cluster cap. What is the MOST likely cause?
options: Kafka broker disk is full
A downstream sink (the database/warehouse the consumer writes to) is throttling or rate-limiting, causing the consumer loop to block on each write
Producers are emitting messages with corrupt headers
Consumer code is in an infinite loop
questions.explanation:
Linear lag growth with **idle consumer CPU** and **idle Kafka throughput** is the classic signature of a **slow sink**: the consumer polls a batch, blocks on the downstream write, polls another batch, blocks again. The bottleneck is downstream, not Kafka or the consumer itself.

- Disk-full would surface as producer-side errors and producer back-off, not consumer lag with idle pods.
- Corrupt headers would *crash* the consumer or stall it on bad messages — CPU spikes and partitions stop advancing, not "linear lag with 15% CPU."
- Infinite loop would peg consumer CPU near 100%.

**Why this pattern is so common at scale**

*Uber — driver earnings reconciliation.* A streaming job consumes Trip-Completed events from Kafka and updates `driver_earnings` in MySQL/Postgres. During a surge (NYE 23:55–00:05) the table hits row-lock contention. Consumer threads spend 90% of wall-clock waiting for COMMIT to return. CPU stays low — the consumer is in `wait(2)`, not running code. Meanwhile producers don't slow down: trips keep completing, so the lag grows linearly until surge subsides or the write path is widened.

*Netflix — Keystone playback ingestion.* A consumer of the playback-events Keystone pipeline writes to a Cassandra cluster keyed by `user_id`. One Cassandra node enters a Stop-The-World G1 GC pause (or hits compaction threshold). The synchronous write to a quorum of replicas slows to the speed of the slowest node. Lag jumps from seconds to hours. The consumer is just passing data through — no heavy compute — so CPU stays idle.

**Architectural mitigations (in order of typical adoption)**

| Pattern | How it works | What it buys you |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Async / batched writes** | Consumer fires writes without waiting per-record; commits Kafka offsets only after a batch confirms. | Decouples consumer throughput from sink p99. |
| **Bounded write queue + back-pressure** | Internal queue between consumer and sink with a max depth; when full, consumer pauses Kafka polling. | Prevents OOM and gives Kafka clear back-pressure signal. |
| **Dead-Letter Queue (DLQ)** | Records that repeatedly fail or whose key is "hot" are diverted to a side topic for offline handling. | Eliminates head-of-line blocking by one bad key. |
| **Per-key partitioning + parallel writers** | Within one consumer, fan out writes across worker threads keyed by something other than the Kafka partition key. | Lets one stalled key stop only its own lane. |
| **Sidecar / service-mesh throttling + circuit breaker** | The proxy in front of the sink trips when sink p99 crosses a threshold; consumer sees fast failures instead of slow waits. | Prevents the "death spiral" where a struggling sink gets hammered by retries and stays slow. |

**The circuit-breaker trade-off**

Circuit breakers don't make the lag disappear — they relocate it. When the breaker trips, the consumer fast-fails or buffers; Kafka lag continues to grow but the consumer stays responsive and the sink gets a chance to recover. The right design is usually:

1. Circuit-break the sink so the consumer is honest about the constraint
2. Send "tripped" records to a retry queue or DLQ (don't drop on the floor)
3. Auto-scale consumers AND surface lag-on-source as the SLO — accepting that "the lag was visible at the source" is a feature, not a bug.

The wrong design is to retry-with-exponential-backoff inside the consumer with no breaker: a slow sink gets DDoSed by its own clients, and lag continues to grow.

questions.prompt: Airbnb hosts can change their nightly price daily, and the search team needs to reconstruct 'what was the price shown on listing X on 2024-09-15 to a guest in Berlin?' for analytics. The price table has 800M rows with timestamped updates. What modeling pattern is most appropriate?
options: Overwrite the price column on each change (Type 1)
SCD Type 2 on the listing—price relationship: row per (listing_id, price, valid_from, valid_to, is_current) with a non-overlapping validity window
Append-only event log only; reconstruct on demand from the raw event stream
Materialized view that always reflects the current price
questions.explanation: Reconstructing historical state-as-of-a-date is the exact use case for SCD Type 2. Searching the raw event log on every analytics query is too expensive at 800M rows. Type 1 erases history. A materialized view of current state can't answer 'what was the price last September'. Many teams pair SCD2 with the event log: raw events feed into a curated SCD2 dimension.

questions.prompt: Discord shards messages by `guild_id` (server ID). One guild ('Twitch streamer drop event') suddenly attracts 4M concurrent users and emits 80K messages/sec, while average guilds emit 2/sec. The shard holding that guild is at 95% CPU and the rest of the fleet is at 8%. What is the SAFEST way to mitigate without taking the chat down?
options: Stop accepting new messages to that guild until traffic subsides
Promote the hot guild to its own dedicated shard / cluster, moving only that guild's data while keeping the rest of the fleet untouched
Re-hash the entire sharding key space to distribute load evenly across all shards
Increase the replication factor on the hot shard
questions.explanation: The standard 'celebrity tenant' mitigation is to split a single hot key off to its own shard—small blast radius, no global resharding, no downtime. Stopping writes breaks the user experience. Resharding the entire keyspace is expensive, risky, and overkill for one hot guild. Replication factor addresses durability/read load, not write CPU.

questions.prompt: A DSP must enrich bid requests with audience-segment data and reply within 100ms p99. The audience segment store has 600M user-segment edges. Currently you do a Postgres lookup per bid (median 8ms, p99 240ms). What change MOST reliably hits the 100ms budget?
options: Add more read replicas to Postgres
Materialize per-user segment vectors into an in-memory K/V store (e.g., Redis or Aerospike) keyed by user_id, refreshed by a streaming pipeline, and look up by exact key
Move the bid-handling code to faster instances
Cache the most recent 10K bids' Postgres responses in process memory
questions.explanation: Ad-bid p99 budgets in the 100ms range are won or lost on the lookup-store choice: in-memory, single-key, microsecond-level fetch. Postgres replicas help throughput but won't shave tail latency (p99 dominates). Per-bid LRU in-process cache has very low hit rate at scale. Faster CPU doesn't fix I/O-bound tail spikes from disk-backed reads.

questions.prompt: A hospital system is consolidating patient records from 6 EHR vendors. The same patient might appear as 'JOHN SMITH 1982-04-12 MA001' in one source and 'J. Smith 04/12/1982 Phone 617-555-0102' in another. What master data management approach is most defensible for matching?
options: Trust the external `master_patient_id` field that one vendor provides, and require all others to populate it

Use deterministic-then-probabilistic matching: exact match on a small set of fields first; for remaining candidates, use a probabilistic scorer over (name similarity, DOB, gender, address, phone) with a human review queue above a threshold
Append all records with a UUID and never attempt to deduplicate — keep separate patient views per source
Merge any two patients whose DOB matches
questions.explanation: Patient identity (Master Patient Index) is the canonical MDM problem in healthcare. The standard approach is exact match on strong identifiers (SSN if present, MRN+source) for the easy 90%, then probabilistic matching with a human-in-the-loop for ambiguous cases. Single-source trust fails when vendors don't share IDs. Never deduplicating ruins care continuity. Matching solely on DOB merges huge numbers of distinct people.

questions.prompt: A user invokes GDPR right-to-be-forgotten. Their data exists in: Postgres (current), S3 silver layer (parquet, 90-day partitions), and a Kafka topic with 5-year retention used for replay. Which deletion strategy is most practical?
options: Tombstone the user in Postgres; rewrite affected silver-layer parquet files to exclude them; ignore Kafka because the topic is append-only

Tombstone in Postgres; rewrite silver-layer parquet; in Kafka, use crypto-shredding—encrypt PII per-user with a key kept in a separate KMS, then delete the user's key so the messages become unreadable ciphertext
Delete the user's records from Postgres only and accept that downstream copies will eventually expire
Rewrite all 5 years of Kafka messages to remove the user's records
questions.explanation: Crypto-shredding is the de-facto pattern for immutable / append-only stores: encrypt per-subject, delete the key. Rewriting 5 years of Kafka topics is prohibitively expensive and breaks ordering guarantees for consumers replaying. Ignoring Kafka is a GDPR violation. Postgres-only deletion leaves discoverable copies elsewhere.

questions.prompt: An IoT fleet emits 100K msgs/sec from 2M devices. Each device's firmware version determines the message schema (v1: 8 fields, v2: 12 fields, v3 ships next month). Where should the schema version be carried, and how should the warehouse handle multiple versions?
options: Embed schema version in each message header; land messages versioned in bronze; normalize to a superset schema in silver, with explicit per-version unpack logic and tests
Enforce a single schema and drop any message that doesn't match
Auto-detect schema from each message at warehouse load time
Run separate parallel pipelines per firmware version forever, with no convergence
questions.explanation: Versioned bronze + normalizing silver is the canonical IoT pattern: producers stamp schema_version on every message, bronze keeps raw payloads losslessly, silver implements explicit unpack-per-version into a superset schema. Dropping non-matching messages loses device data permanently. Auto-detection at load time is brittle and untestable. Parallel pipelines forever explodes maintenance burden as firmware versions accumulate.

questions.prompt: You're cutting over from a 4TB Postgres OLTP database to Snowflake for analytics with zero downtime to the application. Today, analytics queries run on the Postgres primary and slow down the app. What's the safest cutover path?
options: Snapshot Postgres at midnight, restore into Snowflake, switch BI dashboards to Snowflake the next day
Bootstrap Snowflake with an initial Postgres snapshot, then enable Debezium CDC into Snowflake; once lag is stably <5min and row counts reconcile, switch dashboards over with a fallback path
Dual-write from the application to both Postgres and Snowflake going forward; analytics moves to Snowflake immediately
Stop nightly analytics for a week while you copy data manually
questions.explanation: The CDC-bootstrap-then-cutover pattern is the textbook safe migration: snapshot for initial load, log-based CDC to catch up, reconcile, then cut readers over with rollback in place. Midnight snapshot freezes data and skips deltas during cutover. Dual-write from the app couples the OLTP path to a warehouse and is rarely worth the operational pain. Stopping analytics is dishonest about the business cost.

questions.prompt: Your product page caches product details in Redis with TTL 600s. After a sale event drops, 200K users hit the same out-of-stock SKU URL in 30 seconds. Redis hits are 4ms, Postgres fallback is 80ms. You observe Postgres CPU spike to 95% every 600s for 8 seconds. What pattern fixes this?
options: Lower the TTL to 60s so cold misses are smaller
Implement request coalescing (single-flight): only one request per key fetches from Postgres on miss; concurrent requesters wait for the in-flight result
Increase Redis memory by 4x
Switch from cache-aside to write-through caching
questions.explanation: This is a classic cache stampede / dogpile: when the key expires, hundreds of concurrent misses all hammer Postgres simultaneously. Single-flight (request coalescing) limits the database to one fetch per key during a window. Lowering TTL makes stampedes more frequent. More Redis memory doesn't help when the key is missing. Write-through doesn't address the read stampede pattern.

questions.prompt: An omnichannel retailer (online + 1,200 physical stores) needs to display 'available to ship from nearest store' for BOPIS. Store inventory is updated by point-of-sale + receiving events. Stale inventory means oversold orders (~$3M/yr in cancellations). What architecture best balances freshness and consistency?
options: Periodic batch sync every 6 hours from stores to central inventory

Event-streamed inventory (CDC from POS + receiving) into a streaming aggregator that maintains per-(store, sku) on-hand counts; the website reads from this view with optimistic-locking soft-holds during checkout, confirmed against store at fulfillment
Always allow the order, and cancel later if the store can't fulfill
Lock store inventory globally during every online checkout
questions.explanation: Modern omnichannel inventory pairs event-streamed on-hand counts (near real-time) with soft holds at checkout and a final confirmation at fulfillment. Hold-then-confirm reflects the physical reality that the count is approximate until the store associate picks. 6-hour batches cause exactly the $3M/yr oversells. 'Allow and cancel' tanks NPS. Global locking destroys throughput at scale.

questions.prompt: A Flink job tracks per-user session windows with a 30-minute inactivity gap. Most users have a single session per day, but 0.2% are bots with overlapping pseudo-sessions, leading to keyed state for those users growing by 50MB/day each. The job's RocksDB state passed 6TB and checkpoints take 25 minutes. What's the right fix?
options: Allocate more memory to the TaskManagers
Detect bot keys upstream and route them to a separate, bounded-state pipeline; for legitimate users, add a state TTL aligned with the session window so closed sessions are purged
Switch from RocksDB back to the in-memory state backend
Increase checkpoint interval to 1 hour
questions.explanation: Unbounded keyed state is the most common Flink scaling failure. The cure has two parts: (1) state TTL purges per-key state once it's no longer useful, and (2) isolating pathological keys upstream so they don't dominate state size. More memory delays the problem. In-memory backend explodes faster. Longer checkpoint intervals worsen recovery time without fixing root cause.

questions.prompt: An executive dashboard says 'last updated 2 minutes ago' but the numbers haven't moved in 6 hours. The dbt run completes successfully every 5 minutes. What's the FIRST thing to check?
options: Whether the dbt model is materialized as a view (always recomputes) or table (only updates on rebuild)
Whether the source CDC pipeline is delivering new rows, by looking at row counts in the silver layer over the last hour
Whether the dashboard tool's caching layer (e.g., Looker's BI cache, Tableau's extract refresh) is serving stale results
Whether the warehouse credit cap was hit
questions.explanation: 'Pipeline succeeded but data is stale' is almost always upstream: the dbt run completed, but it processed an empty delta because the source feed stalled. Checking silver-layer row counts over time confirms or refutes this in seconds. Materialization type, BI cache, and credit cap are downstream causes that don't explain 'dbt succeeded'. Always isolate where the freshness drops first.

questions.prompt: An e-commerce team is designing a referral program. A user can refer friends; each successful signup gives them a credit; later, when the referee makes their first purchase, both parties get a credit; the campaign manager wants ROI metrics on a per-channel basis. What's the cleanest schema shape?
options: One denormalized 'referrals' table with referrer_id, referee_id, signup_at, first_purchase_at, credits_paid_referrer, credits_paid_referee, channel
Two tables: `referral` (referrer_id, referee_id, channel, status, created_at) and `referral_event` (referral_id, event_type, amount, occurred_at) — modeling state as a series of events
One mega-table that copies fields from users, orders, and channels
Store the program rules in code and don't persist anything
questions.explanation: Referral programs have a sequence of milestones (signup, first-purchase, credit-paid) that can occur over weeks. Modeling as a parent referral row plus an event/state child table cleanly supports adding new milestone types, replaying, computing per-stage funnel, and changing reward rules without schema churn. A single denormalized row makes adding milestones a destructive change. Mega-tables couple unrelated domains. Code-only loses auditability.

questions.prompt: An order-events Kafka topic has 30 partitions keyed by `order_id`. The team wants to repartition to `customer_id` so all of a customer's events land in the same partition for customer-360 stream aggregations. They cannot break the existing order_id consumers. What's the lowest-risk approach?
options: Change the producer's partition key from order_id to customer_id and let existing consumers continue, accepting reordering within order_id streams
Create a NEW topic keyed by customer_id, build a stateless stream that re-keys events from the original topic into the new topic; existing consumers continue on the original topic
Repartition the existing topic in place by running a maintenance script
Add more partitions to the existing topic so each customer's events land deterministically
questions.explanation: The standard 'change keying without breaking consumers' pattern is to land events into a NEW topic re-keyed by the new field, while the original topic continues unchanged. Changing the producer key in place breaks ordering for existing order_id consumers (related events scatter). Repartitioning a topic in place isn't a thing in Kafka. Adding partitions doesn't change the key strategy.

questions.prompt: A buy-now-pay-later (BNPL) lender sees a sudden spike in approvals followed 60-90 days later by defaults from a specific IP range and device-fingerprint cluster. They suspect a fraud ring using stolen identities. What real-time detection pattern is most appropriate?
options: Train a daily batch model and use it for next-day scoring

Stream feature signals (velocity, device-graph linkage, address-graph linkage) into a feature store; score each application against a real-time model; for high-risk scores, route to manual review and link the application's graph for cluster detection
Block all applications from the suspect IP range manually
Wait until defaults occur, then deny new credit to that cluster
questions.explanation: Fraud rings exploit batch-only systems by churning through identities faster than nightly retraining. Real-time scoring with graph signals (shared device, address, phone, even browser entropy) catches rings at application time. IP blocks are easy to evade. Waiting for defaults means the loss is already realized.

questions.prompt: A startup runs Kafka at 23% average broker CPU but spends $48K/month. The cluster has 12 brokers, 3-replica topics, average message size 800 bytes, throughput ~80MB/s. What is the LIKELY root cause of the bill being high?
options: CPU is the bottleneck
Cross-AZ replication and consumer traffic egress fees — each message is replicated to 2 other AZs and consumed by multiple consumer groups in different AZs, multiplying network costs
Brokers are oversized for the throughput
Consumer lag is driving extra compute
questions.explanation: AWS Kafka clusters bill heavily on cross-AZ network: replication fanout (1->3 AZs) plus each consumer group fetching across AZs can multiply traffic 6-10x. CPU is low because brokers are I/O-bound. Right-sizing CPU saves modest amounts. Lag doesn't add cost. The cure is fewer AZs (with availability tradeoffs), rack-awareness, or follower-fetch.

questions.prompt: Your security team retains audit logs for 7 years for SOX. 90 days of recent logs are queried often (incident response), the next 18 months occasionally (audits), and years 2-7 only on subpoena. Current setup: all logs in S3 Standard at $23/TB/month. 500TB total. What tiering strategy maximizes savings without breaking audit retrieval?
options: Move everything older than 90 days to Glacier Deep Archive immediately
Tier: <90 days S3 Standard, 90 days–2 years S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval (milliseconds, $4/TB/mo), 2–7 years Glacier Deep Archive ($1/TB/mo, 12-48h retrieval); use S3 Lifecycle policies
Delete logs after 90 days and rely on backups
Keep everything in S3 Standard — storage is the small part of the bill
questions.explanation: Three-tier maps cleanly onto access frequency: hot tier for incident response, instant-retrieval for periodic audits (~5x cheaper but still milliseconds), deep archive for the long-tail rarely accessed compliance window (~20x cheaper, hours to retrieve). Jumping straight to Deep Archive penalizes the 18-month audit-frequency tier. Deletion violates SOX. 'Storage is small' is rarely true at 500TB.

questions.prompt: Spotify's stream-count aggregator must increment counts as users listen, deduplicate based on `(user_id, track_id, listened_at_minute_bucket)`, and emit hourly aggregates. The pipeline runs Flink. Late events arrive up to 6 hours late from offline-cached listens. What's the cleanest watermark + windowing strategy?
options: 1-hour tumbling windows, watermark = max(event_ts) - 5 seconds, no allowed lateness
1-hour tumbling windows, watermark = max(event_ts) - 30 minutes, allowedLateness = 6 hours, emit a 'final' result after the lateness expires while emitting incremental updates earlier
Processing-time 1-hour windows that ignore event timestamp entirely
1-hour windows with no watermark; rely on consumers to deduplicate
questions.explanation: Spotify-scale music streaming has known straggler windows (offline listens sync hours later). Watermark = -30min handles normal jitter; allowedLateness = 6h plus incremental + final emits gives consumers fast approximate counts AND a final correct number after the cutoff. Zero lateness drops valid listens. Processing-time loses the offline-listen window. No watermark abdicates correctness to consumers.

questions.prompt: A logistics platform tracks package events: 'picked_up', 'in_sortation', 'on_truck', 'delivered', 'exception'. Status updates arrive out of order (sortation centers batch-upload nightly; trucks send real-time). The dashboard often shows 'on_truck' AFTER 'delivered'. What pattern fixes the display?
options: Always trust the most recently received event

Maintain a status state machine per package: each event has a known position in the funnel; advancing requires a forward transition. Out-of-order events that would regress state are stored but don't overwrite the current status
Discard events that arrive out of order
Display all received events as a verbatim timeline with no status field
questions.explanation: Package events have a known forward order (picked_up < in_sortation < on_truck < delivered). A state machine that only allows forward transitions on the public 'status' field, while still storing every event for audit, gives consistent UX even when events arrive out of order. 'Most recent wins' produces exactly the 'on_truck after delivered' UI bug. Discarding loses audit data.

questions.prompt: A feature-flag platform serves 4B evaluations/day. Each flag can have rules: 'enabled for tenant A, OR users in cohort X, OR random 20% rollout in EU'. The CTO wants per-tenant overrides without forking the rule for each tenant. What schema shape is appropriate?
options: One row per (flag, tenant) with the full rule duplicated per tenant

Three tables: `flag` (id, name, default_state), `flag_rule` (flag_id, rule_type, payload, priority), `flag_override` (flag_id, tenant_id, override_state, override_rule_id NULL) — evaluators check tenant_override first, then global rules in priority order
Store rules as a single JSON blob with embedded tenant logic
Recompute rules per request from a Postgres query joining flags to users to tenants
questions.explanation: LaunchDarkly-style flag stores separate the default state, the global rule chain, and the per-tenant overrides. Evaluators apply tenant override first, then fall back to global rules. Duplicating the rule per tenant explodes the row count and creates drift bugs. A single JSON blob makes targeted overrides impossible to query. Joining users-to-tenants at evaluation time misses the 4B/day latency budget.

questions.prompt: A dbt incremental model that processed 5M rows/day for months suddenly fails OOM. The data team confirms volume is unchanged. The model's `unique_key` is `order_id`, materialization is `incremental` with strategy `merge`. What's the LIKELY cause?
options: Snowflake credits exhausted
The `is_incremental()` filter guard was removed in a recent PR, causing the model to MERGE the entire history rather than only the new day
The warehouse was downsized
Network instability between dbt and Snowflake
questions.explanation: The most common cause of 'incremental model uses too much memory after volume is stable' is a missing or broken `is_incremental()` guard, so the model processes the full history every run. Credit exhaustion would surface as a different error. Warehouse downsizing would slow it without OOM. Network errors don't cause OOM. Always grep the diff for the `is_incremental()` macro after such regressions.

questions.prompt: Your B2B SaaS sells to EU and US customers. EU customers' data must reside in EU (GDPR). You currently run a single Postgres cluster in us-east. A large EU customer signs a contract requiring data residency. What's the LEAST invasive design change?
options: Move all customers to EU
Run a second cluster in EU, replicate schemas/migrations through your CI, and route EU customers' queries via a tenant-routing layer that selects the cluster by region attribute on the tenant
Encrypt EU customer data with a tenant-specific key in the US cluster
Ask the EU customer to accept US residency
questions.explanation: Multi-region per-tenant routing is the standard data-residency pattern for SaaS: one shared schema, deployed per region, with tenants routed by a region attribute. Moving all to EU breaks US customers' latency and regulatory profile. Encryption-in-place doesn't satisfy residency requirements—the data still physically resides in the wrong region. Asking the customer to drop the requirement loses the deal.

questions.prompt: A subscription-billing SaaS supports proration: if a user upgrades from Basic ($10/mo) to Pro ($30/mo) mid-cycle on day 10 of a 30-day cycle, they pay $20 × (20/30) = $13.33 for the remainder. You need to bill, refund, and audit. What modeling pattern is right?
options: Mutate the subscription row to the new plan and adjust the next invoice

Append a `subscription_event` per change (upgrade, downgrade, pause, cancel) with `effective_at`; compute invoice line items by walking the events that occurred within the billing cycle and emit per-segment line items
Store only the current plan; do prorations in the BI layer
Issue a full refund and re-bill at the new rate
questions.explanation: Subscription state is inherently time-windowed; modeling each change as an event with `effective_at` lets invoices accurately reflect mixed-plan periods, supports historical audits ('what did they pay last June?'), and gives finance the line-item breakdown that customers expect on receipts. Mutating the current row destroys history. BI-layer math doesn't bill. Refund-and-rebill is operationally messy and surprises customers.

questions.prompt: Your team uses ClickHouse for a customer-facing analytics dashboard. Each tenant sees only their data. p95 query latency is 250ms today. A new tenant onboards with 10x the data of any other and their queries take 8 seconds while affecting everyone else's tail. What is the LEAST disruptive mitigation?
options: Migrate the entire fleet off ClickHouse

Use ClickHouse's resource isolation features (workload manager, query queues, per-user resource limits) and shard the heavy tenant's data onto dedicated nodes/replicas while keeping the rest of the cluster shared
Force the heavy tenant to a 60-second query timeout
Aggregate the heavy tenant's data to higher granularity and accept loss of detail
questions.explanation: ClickHouse has explicit isolation primitives (workload manager, user-level resource quotas, sharding by tenant) that mirror Snowflake's 'noisy neighbor' pattern. Hard timeouts make their dashboard fail rather than slow. Aggregating away their grain breaks the product promise. Replacing the engine is months of work and doesn't address the underlying multi-tenant isolation question.

questions.prompt: Your stream-processing job duplicates records ~0.2% of the time. The Kafka consumer is at-least-once. The downstream is a Postgres OLTP table that funds-transfers should idempotently update. Where is the cheapest place to deduplicate?
options: Enable Kafka exactly-once on the producer and consumer
Use idempotent upserts keyed by the event's natural ID (or a hashed surrogate) in the Postgres write path; tolerate the at-least-once Kafka delivery
Move to a different message bus
Add a deduplication stage in the consumer using a Bloom filter over the last hour
questions.explanation: Effectively-once at the SINK via idempotent upserts (keyed by the event's natural ID) is the standard production pattern. End-to-end exactly-once is achievable but operationally heavy and not required when the sink can be made idempotent. A Bloom filter has false positives and bounded memory. Switching buses is overkill.

questions.prompt: A SaaS support ticket queue is indexed on `(tenant_id, created_at)`. The main query is 'show me OPEN tickets for tenant X sorted by oldest first'. Status is `open` for ~20% of rows. Query performance has degraded as tickets pile up; pg_stat_user_indexes shows the index is used but the heap-fetch is heavy. What is the BEST mitigation?
options: Drop the index and let Postgres pick another plan
Replace with a partial index: `(tenant_id, created_at) WHERE status = 'open'` so the index contains only the rows that matter for the hot query
Increase work_mem
Switch to a hash index
questions.explanation: A partial index keyed on `(tenant_id, created_at) WHERE status='open'` is dramatically smaller, has better cache locality, and lets the planner skip status filtering. Heap-fetch costs come from the index returning many rows that are then filtered. Increasing work_mem helps sorts/joins, not this. Hash indexes don't support range scans on `created_at`.

questions.prompt: OpenTable-style restaurant booking. Tables hold capacities, parties hold sizes and arrival windows, and you must prevent double-booking the same physical table during overlapping reservations. What schema feature is essential?
options: A `restaurants` table with a `seats_available` integer that's decremented per booking
A `reservation` table with a Postgres `EXCLUDE USING gist (table_id WITH =, tsrange(start_ts, end_ts) WITH &&)` exclusion constraint to prevent overlapping bookings on the same table
Application-level locking
Always allow the booking and resolve conflicts at arrival
questions.explanation: Postgres EXCLUDE constraints with GiST are purpose-built for non-overlapping range semantics on (key, range) pairs. The database refuses to insert a row that overlaps an existing one for the same table_id—correct by construction. A decrementing seat counter ignores time. Application-level locks fail under retries and concurrent processes. Resolving at arrival kills hospitality.

questions.prompt: Your Postgres primary in us-east-1 streams asynchronously to a replica in us-west-2 for DR. RPO target is 5 minutes. Average replication lag is 2s, p99 is 1.5min. During a network partition that lasts 8 minutes, the primary continues accepting writes and the replica falls 8 minutes behind. The primary then fails. What is the data exposure?
options: Zero — the replica has all data
Up to 8 minutes of writes can be lost when promoting the replica, exceeding the 5-minute RPO target
5 minutes of data loss regardless
The primary will block writes during the partition
questions.explanation: Async replication means the primary commits without waiting for the replica. During the partition, the primary kept committing while the replica saw nothing. Promotion loses everything that didn't replicate—here, up to 8 minutes—which exceeds the 5-minute RPO. This is exactly the trade-off: async = low latency but RPO floor = max-possible lag. Synchronous replication would have made the primary block; semi-sync would have allowed degraded mode.

questions.prompt: A content-publishing platform has articles that move through states: draft -> in_review -> approved -> published -> retracted. Editors need to see 'who approved what when' and revert to prior versions. What's the right model?
options: An `article` table with a `status` column overwritten on each transition

An `article` table for current state plus an immutable `article_state_transition` log: (article_id, from_state, to_state, actor_id, occurred_at, comment) AND an `article_revision` table for content snapshots keyed by (article_id, revision_no)
Store everything in a single denormalized table with one row per status change
Use a graph database for the workflow
questions.explanation: Workflows benefit from separating current state (for fast reads) from an append-only transition log (for audit + analytics) and from a content revision table (for revert). Status-overwrite loses 'who and when'. A single denormalized table mixes content and workflow concerns. A graph DB doesn't add value over a relational pattern here.

questions.prompt: A mobile game has a global leaderboard with 40M monthly active players. Players' scores change with every match (~50M matches/day). Players want to see their rank and the top 100. What is the most scalable store for this?
options: Postgres with a B-tree index on score, ORDER BY score DESC LIMIT 100 every time
A Redis sorted set per leaderboard (ZADD on update, ZREVRANGE for top-N, ZREVRANK for individual rank), with periodic checkpointing to a durable store
A relational table re-sorted nightly
A graph database keyed on player_id
questions.explanation: Redis sorted sets are O(log N) for inserts/updates and O(log N + M) for range reads, with cheap rank lookups—ideal for high-write leaderboards. Postgres with global ORDER BY won't survive 50M updates/day plus per-player rank queries. Nightly re-sorting is stale. Graph databases don't natively expose rank operations.

questions.prompt: An attribution platform credits conversions to ads viewed within the last 30 days (click) or 1 day (view-through). A user converts at time T. The event stream of impressions/clicks has ~80B events/day. What design lets you compute attribution per conversion at scale?
options: Scan all impressions/clicks for the converting user from the warehouse at conversion time

Maintain a per-user state store (e.g., Pinot/ClickHouse keyed by user_id with a time-bounded window of recent ad events); on conversion, look up the user's recent ad history and apply the attribution model
Pre-compute attribution at impression time before the conversion arrives
Always credit the last ad shown
questions.explanation: Attribution requires the user's recent ad history at conversion time—a key-value lookup pattern with a time-windowed payload. Real-time OLAP engines or specialized stores excel at 'recent N events for key K'. Scanning the warehouse at conversion time is impossibly slow at 80B events/day. Pre-computing attribution before the conversion is impossible because the conversion hasn't happened yet. Last-ad-only is a degraded heuristic, not an attribution platform.

questions.prompt: Pinterest needs to backfill 7 years of pin-impression events from cold storage (Glacier) into a new attribution model. Today's pipeline ingests 12B events/day from live Kafka into Iceberg. The backfill is ~2 trillion events. What's the safest topology?
options: Replay all 7 years through the existing live pipeline at maximum speed

Build a parallel backfill pipeline that writes to a SEPARATE Iceberg table with the same schema, throttled to not impact live; once complete and validated, swap the attribution model to query a UNION of the historical and live tables (or merge via a one-time MERGE)
Restore Glacier to S3 Standard for all 7 years at once
Skip the backfill and start the new model from 'now'
questions.explanation:
The standard 'isolated backfill, swap on success' pattern keeps the live system untouched. A separate target table lets you throttle, validate, and either merge or union without risk to production reads. Replaying through live tooling congests it and risks reordering against current writes. Restoring all of Glacier at once is an instant six-figure surprise. Starting from 'now' abandons history needed to train the new model.

**Why this is the safest topology at 2T-event / 1.2PB scale**

The two real risks are **resource contention** and **blast radius**. The parallel approach addresses both:

- **Isolation of the live path.** Pinterest's production ingest is already at 12B events/day. Replaying 7 years through that pipeline would mean ~200× the daily write throughput against the SAME Iceberg manifest / commit service / Kafka brokers. Most likely outcome: live ingest lag spikes, write conflicts on the live table, eventually a production outage.
- **Cost & throttle control.** Restoring 1.2PB from Glacier Deep Archive to S3 Standard in one shot is prohibitive — restored objects pay Standard storage rate for the whole restore window, retrieval requests rate-limit at the account level, and Bulk tier (cheapest) takes 12–48h per slice. **Wave restoration** (one month's prefix at a time) caps active-at-once spend.
- **Validation before commitment.** A shadow table lets you reconcile against the OVERLAP window: backfill December 2024 → compare to what live ingested for December 2024 → prove the new attribution model is correct. If the backfill logic has a bug, drop the shadow table and start over — no risky `DELETE WHERE` against a 12B/day production table.

**The cutover mechanics (Iceberg-specific)**

Once the shadow `pin_impressions_history` is validated, swap by metadata, not data movement:

1. **UNION ALL view** (safest). Create a view that UNIONs history + live; point the attribution model at the view. No data movement, fully reversible, downstream sees one logical table.
2. **One-time MERGE INTO** in a maintenance window (only if you must have one physical table). MERGE history into live, drop the shadow. Doubles storage temporarily; requires careful concurrency planning with the 12B/day writers.
3. **Iceberg branches**. `ALTER TABLE … CREATE BRANCH backfill`, write into the branch, then `FAST FORWARD main FROM backfill` for an atomic merge of a giant fileset. Powerful but the youngest of the three patterns in terms of ecosystem support.

**Never** do `DELETE FROM live WHERE event_date < cutover; INSERT INTO live SELECT * FROM shadow;` — destructive, concurrency-hostile, irreversible if the INSERT fails halfway, and on Iceberg it spawns position-delete files that bloat metadata and slow live readers until VACUUM completes.

**Why the alternatives fail**

- *Replay through live pipeline* — the most common way to cause a production outage on this class of project. Saturates Kafka brokers and the Iceberg commit service. Even at 'max speed' you're 200× the daily write rate; ingest lag spikes for real-time consumers.
- *Restore all 7 years of Glacier at once* — instant six-figure storage bill (Standard rate × 1.2PB × restore-window-days), plus account-level rate-limiting on restore requests. Most teams' billing alerts fire before the restore even finishes.
- *Skip the backfill* — fails the business requirement. Attribution models need 7y of LTV-relevant history; starting from 'now' loses the long-tail conversion windows that motivate the project.

questions.prompt: Your finance mart's `revenue_recognized_usd` for yesterday is $4.2M, while the trailing 28-day median is $4.1M. The dashboard greens. An hour later, a controller flags that `revenue_recognized_eur` is half of yesterday's normal. What kind of DQ check would have caught this?
options: Schema validation (column types, not nulls)
Anomaly/statistical checks at the SLICE level (per-currency, per-region, per-product), not just at the aggregated mart level
Row count thresholds on the source table
Foreign key integrity checks
questions.explanation: Top-line aggregates often look fine while a slice tanks—classic Simpson-style cancellation. Per-currency / per-region / per-tenant SLO checks catch slice-level regressions. Schema, row count, and FK checks would all pass in this scenario. Build DQ at the most actionable grain you can afford.

questions.prompt: An IoT system has 800K devices on cellular. Connectivity drops at random; a device may go offline for hours and then upload a backlog of 2,000 buffered events at once. What ingestion behavior is essential?
options: Reject any backlog with a timestamp older than 5 minutes
Accept the backlog ordered by event_ts, dedupe by (device_id, event_seq) at ingest, and allow downstream stream jobs to handle late events via watermarks with allowed-lateness
Throttle each device to 1 event/second
Always trust the most recent event timestamp
questions.explanation: Buffered uploads are a fact of life in cellular IoT. The ingestion path must accept the burst, dedupe by per-device sequence numbers (devices emit `event_seq` for exactly this reason), and trust event_ts for downstream windowing. Rejecting old timestamps loses telemetry. Throttling per-device drops legitimate backlog. 'Most recent' loses the rest of the buffer.

questions.prompt: A wearable streams vitals (HR, SpO2) at 1 Hz from 200K users. Cardiologists need real-time alerts on abnormal patterns AND long-term trend analysis. What architecture splits these well?
options: Run both queries against a single OLTP database

Stream raw signals into Kafka; one consumer runs anomaly-detection rules on a rolling window for alerts; a second writes downsampled per-minute aggregates to a long-term time-series store (e.g., Timescale/InfluxDB) for trends; raw 1Hz retained 30 days for re-analysis
Persist every 1 Hz sample in a relational table forever
Drop the data after generating alerts
questions.explanation: Real-time alerting and long-term trend analysis have different latency, retention, and granularity needs. Splitting them at the streaming layer—alert consumer + downsample consumer + bounded raw retention—serves both. OLTP can't ingest 200K Hz. Permanent 1Hz retention bloats cost without proportional value. Dropping forfeits future re-analysis.

questions.prompt: Your hotel-inventory platform supports overbooking (sell 102 rooms for 100 physical rooms because cancellations average 4%). The current schema decrements `available_rooms` on each booking; over-bookings rely on application logic. Audits show race conditions cause both over- and under-bookings. What schema change fixes it deterministically?
options: Add an application-level mutex around bookings

Model inventory as `(room_type_id, stay_night) -> total_capacity, overbook_allowance`; bookings INSERT a row keyed by (room_type_id, stay_night, booking_id); a check constraint or trigger enforces SUM(bookings.qty) <= total_capacity + overbook_allowance on commit
Trust eventual consistency and call agents the morning of
Increase the overbook allowance to 20%
questions.explanation: Deterministic inventory math is best enforced by the database, with capacity and overbook stored as data and bookings as rows that compete for limited capacity. Triggers or constraints make the rule provable, not best-effort. App mutexes fail under multi-process / multi-region deployment. Manual reconciliation is the failure mode you're escaping. More overbooking treats the symptom, not the bug.

questions.prompt: After a Postgres minor-version upgrade, a previously fast query (5s) now takes 4 minutes. EXPLAIN shows the new plan uses a sequential scan where it used to use an index. `pg_stats` confirms statistics are fresh. What's the LIKELY explanation?
options: The index was dropped during the upgrade

A regression in the cost model or a new GUC default in the new minor version changed the planner's preference; tweaking `random_page_cost` / `effective_cache_size` / `enable_seqscan = off` for the query (or pinning the plan with `pg_hint_plan`) restores prior behavior
The data has changed
Disk is failing
questions.explanation: Plan flips after a minor upgrade with healthy statistics often trace to subtle cost-model changes between versions. The pragmatic fixes are to tune session GUCs (random_page_cost lowered when SSDs are present, effective_cache_size right-sized, etc.) or use plan-hinting tools. An upgrade does not silently drop indexes. Data unchanged. Disk would cause errors, not slowness.

questions.prompt: Calendly's scheduling backend must prevent two people booking the same 30-minute slot on the same calendar. Two requests arrive at 09:00:00.000 and 09:00:00.020 for the same slot. What design GUARANTEES one wins without polling?
options: First-write-wins via a UNIQUE constraint on (calendar_id, start_ts, duration) so the second INSERT fails with a unique violation, mapped to a 409 Conflict for the loser
Read availability then INSERT — check-then-act
Cache the calendar and check the cache
Round-robin between two replicas
questions.explanation: Calendar booking is a textbook 'unique slot' problem. A UNIQUE constraint on (calendar_id, start_ts, duration) lets the database adjudicate the race deterministically; the loser sees a unique violation and gets a 409. Check-then-act has a TOCTOU race. Cache-then-check is the same race plus stale cache. Replica round-robin makes it worse.

questions.prompt: A bookmark service caches user-profile data in Redis. After a major release that added a new field to the profile, users see stale profiles missing the new field. The cache TTL is 24 hours. The product team wants the new field visible NOW without bumping deploys. What's the safest invalidation strategy?
options: Wait 24 hours for natural TTL expiry
Bump a cache-key namespace (e.g., include `v=2` in the cache key) so new code reads new keys; old keys naturally expire. Old code paths continue to read old keys until rollout completes.
FLUSHDB to clear Redis entirely
Issue a SCAN + DEL for the user-profile key pattern
questions.explanation: Cache versioning via key namespace is the canonical 'safe invalidation' pattern: new code writes/reads new keys, old code continues unaffected, and old keys age out. FLUSHDB hammers every cached value (including unrelated). SCAN+DEL is slow at scale and creates inconsistent windows. Waiting accepts 24h of staleness. Versioning is reversible and survives partial rollouts.

questions.prompt: A B2C marketplace sees promo-code abuse: bot accounts redeem 'WELCOME10' across hundreds of synthetic accounts, costing $80K/month. The promo logic checks 'has this account used the code before'. What is the minimum schema/logic change to mitigate?
options: Disable all promo codes

Track redemption keyed by (code, redemption_fingerprint) where fingerprint combines account_id, payment_method_hash, device_id, and shipping_address_hash; cap redemptions per fingerprint; queue cases above threshold for review
Increase the code expiration to 30 days
Make the codes shorter so they're easier to type
questions.explanation: Promo abuse is rarely solved by account-only deduplication—abusers spin up accounts trivially. Linking redemptions across stable signals (payment, device, shipping) catches rings. Disabling promos costs growth. Expiration is unrelated. Code length is unrelated. The pattern is 'fingerprint per attempt, cap per fingerprint, review above threshold'.

questions.prompt: Your Kafka topic has 60 partitions. Two consumer groups read it: 'fraud-scorer' (24 consumers) and 'warehouse-loader' (12 consumers). A new requirement says 'fraud-scorer' must scale to 80 consumers. What happens when you scale 'fraud-scorer' beyond 60?
options: Kafka auto-increases partitions to match consumers
20 consumers will sit idle because Kafka assigns at most one partition to one consumer per group; the cap is the partition count
Throughput doubles
Consumers crash
questions.explanation: Within a single consumer group, at most one consumer is assigned to a given partition; consumers beyond the partition count sit idle. To scale beyond 60, you must increase partitions (and accept that historical ordering by key remains preserved only within partitions).
