This is one of the most-asked Kafka scaling questions in production.

questions.prompt: Twitter-style feed fanout: a celebrity with 60M followers tweets. Pre-computing the tweet into every follower's home timeline (push/fanout-on-write) would cost 60M writes per tweet. What is the standard hybrid mitigation?
options: Always pull on read
Hybrid fanout: pull-on-read for celebrities (lookup at feed render time), push-on-write for normal accounts; mix the two sources in the timeline merger
Force celebrities to send DMs
Cap follower counts
questions.explanation: Twitter, Instagram, and similar social services use a hybrid: fanout-on-write is cheap for ordinary accounts and unbearable for celebrities; fanout-on-read flips the math (one fetch per follower at read time). The merger combines pre-built timeline + recent celebrity tweets at render time. Pure pull is too expensive at the read path for non-celebs.

questions.prompt: An Iceberg fact table has 14 days of data partitioned by `day(event_ts)`. A query `WHERE event_ts BETWEEN '2025-06-01' AND '2025-06-03'` is suddenly scanning the entire table after a recent migration. EXPLAIN shows no partition pruning. What is the MOST likely cause?
options: Iceberg metadata is corrupt

The query is filtering on a column that ISN'T the partition column — e.g., the source table was rewritten with a different timestamp column (`created_at`) and the partition transform was lost or remapped
The query engine doesn't support Iceberg
The table was vacuumed too aggressively
questions.explanation: Partition pruning is silently lost when the filter column doesn't match the partition expression (or when the partition spec evolves and the new spec doesn't cover the column being filtered). Iceberg's hidden partitioning hides the transform, but only if the right column is used. Corruption would cause errors. Vacuum prunes old snapshots, not pruning capability.

questions.prompt: Your data lake has 80K Parquet files averaging 8MB each (640GB total). Query engines complain of 'small-files problem' and queries are unexpectedly slow despite low total data volume. What's the right remediation?
options: Re-ingest the data daily as a single huge file
Run a compaction job (e.g., Iceberg `rewriteDataFiles` or Delta `OPTIMIZE`) to coalesce files into ~256MB-1GB targets aligned with engine read-block size; schedule it weekly going forward
Switch from Parquet to CSV
Disable compression
questions.explanation: Small files multiply listing, planning, and per-file overhead in distributed engines. Compaction to the 256MB-1GB sweet spot is the textbook fix, and modern table formats provide first-class compaction. Single huge files lose pruning. CSV is worse on every axis. Compression isn't the bottleneck.

questions.prompt: SOC 2 auditors ask you to prove that no engineer has read raw PII (SSN, DOB) from your customer database outside of an approved support workflow. Today, everyone has SELECT access. What's the right ACCESS-control change to make the audit trivially defensible?
options: Email all engineers asking them not to query PII
Restrict the PII columns via column-level grants or views; create an auditable 'support workflow' service account that requires a ticket-bound role grant; emit access logs to an immutable store
Encrypt the database
Disable SELECT for everyone
questions.explanation: Audit-defensible access control combines column/row-level grants, role bindings backed by tickets, and immutable logging. The principle is least-privilege-by-construction, not by exhortation. Encryption at rest doesn't prevent authorized SELECT. Disabling SELECT breaks the business. Email is unauditable.

questions.prompt: Your ingestion pipeline silently produced ~3% duplicate rows for the last 4 hours after a Kafka rebalance. The downstream warehouse table is append-only. Which mitigations apply? (Select all that apply)
options: Identify the 4-hour window in the warehouse and run a deduplication MERGE / DELETE keyed by the event's natural ID and ingest_ts range
Drop the entire table and re-ingest from the start of Kafka retention
Add a UNIQUE index on the natural ID going forward to prevent future duplicates at write time
Configure consumer to commit offsets BEFORE writing, eliminating duplicates
Add an idempotency-key / upsert path so retries are naturally deduplicated even if the consumer rebalances
questions.explanation: Correct mitigations: dedupe the bad window with a keyed MERGE; add a UNIQUE / idempotent-write path so retries can't double-insert. Dropping and re-ingesting is unnecessarily destructive and risks losing data outside the bad window. Committing offsets before writing produces DATA LOSS instead of duplicates—the opposite anti-pattern. Idempotency at the SINK (upsert) is the cleanest way to absorb at-least-once delivery.

questions.prompt: Your SaaS has noisy-neighbor problems: a few tenants overwhelm shared resources. Which DESIGN choices reliably isolate tenants? (Select all that apply)
options: Per-tenant dedicated virtual warehouses for the largest tenants
Per-tenant resource quotas / workload management in the engine
Sharding the database by tenant_id and routing queries based on tenant attributes
Storing all tenants' data in a single denormalized wide table without any tenant column
Soft caps + queue-based throttling per tenant at the API layer
questions.explanation: Dedicated warehouses, engine-level quotas, tenant-aware sharding, and API-layer queueing all isolate blast radius. Storing data without a tenant column makes tenant-aware filtering, RLS, audit, and isolation impossible. (Per-tenant sharding may overshoot for small tenants—often a hybrid 'jumbo tenants get dedicated shards, small tenants share' design works best in practice.)

questions.prompt: Your monthly BigQuery bill rose 60% in two months. Which investigation steps belong on day one? (Select all that apply)
options: Query INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT for top jobs by bytes scanned in the last 60 days
Identify which tables are being scanned full because of missing partition filters or wrong clustering
Move all data to a different cloud immediately
Audit whether on-demand pricing is being used when slot reservations would be cheaper for the steady-state workload
Drop all materialized views to reduce storage
questions.explanation: BigQuery cost regressions almost always trace to a small number of newly-deployed big-scan queries. Step 1: surface them via JOBS_BY_PROJECT. Step 2: check partition/cluster effectiveness. Step 3: consider slot reservations once steady-state volume justifies them. Cloud migration is months of work for an unrelated symptom. Dropping MVs may INCREASE cost when downstream queries recompute repeatedly.

questions.prompt: Implementing GDPR right-to-be-forgotten across a 5-year-retained event topic and a downstream lakehouse. Which approaches are reasonable? (Select all that apply)
options: Crypto-shred: encrypt PII per-subject with a key in a separate KMS; delete the key on request, rendering historical ciphertext unreadable
Maintain a delete-list table that downstream pipelines join against on every read to filter out forgotten subjects
Rewrite all historical Parquet files in the lakehouse to remove the subject's rows, using Iceberg/Delta MERGE / DELETE
Rewrite the entire 5-year Kafka topic to physically remove the records
Pseudonymize PII at ingest with a reversible mapping that can be destroyed per-subject
questions.explanation: Crypto-shredding, delete-lists for read-path filtering, lakehouse MERGE/DELETE, and pseudonymization-with-destroyable-mapping are all used in practice. Rewriting an entire 5-year Kafka topic is operationally impractical and breaks consumer offsets and ordering. Most teams crypto-shred in the topic while properly DELETEing in queryable systems.

questions.prompt: You're designing a streaming pipeline for ad impressions with 12B events/day, must dedupe and have effectively-once semantics into Snowflake. Which choices belong in the design? (Select all that apply)
options: Idempotent natural keys on each event so re-delivery produces the same row
Kafka producer with `enable.idempotence=true` and `acks=all`
MERGE (upsert) into the Snowflake target keyed by the event's natural ID
At-most-once consumer settings to avoid duplicates
Per-event auto-generated UUIDs assigned at the consumer, included in the deduplication key
questions.explanation: Effectively-once sinks rely on idempotent producers, stable natural keys, and upsert (MERGE) into the warehouse. At-most-once trades duplicates for data loss, which is rarely acceptable. Consumer-generated UUIDs change on each retry and cannot serve as a deduplication key—the UUID must come from the producer (or a deterministic derivation of event content).

questions.prompt: A multi-tenant warehouse mart serves analytics for 800 tenants. Which design choices help BI dashboards remain fast AND tenant-scoped? (Select all that apply)
options: Cluster / partition the marts by tenant_id where the engine supports it
Apply row-level security policies tied to user attributes so queries auto-filter by tenant
Maintain per-tenant pre-aggregations for the highest-traffic dashboards
Store each tenant's data in a separate database with its own credentials managed manually
Skip the tenant_id column to save storage
questions.explanation: Cluster/partition by tenant_id, enforce filters via RLS, and pre-aggregate the heaviest dashboards. Per-tenant separate databases scales operationally to maybe a few dozen tenants, not 800. Dropping tenant_id makes correct filtering impossible. The combination of these three handles 95% of multi-tenant BI workloads.

questions.prompt: Migrating a 400TB on-prem Hadoop / Hive cluster to a cloud lakehouse (e.g., S3 + Iceberg + Trino). Which practices belong in the playbook? (Select all that apply)
options: Start with a non-critical, read-only fact table to validate the engine, file format, and partitioning choices end-to-end
Migrate every table at once on a single weekend
Convert ORC/Parquet to Iceberg in place using Iceberg's `migrate` / `snapshot` procedures, preserving partitioning
Run both systems in parallel for at least one full pipeline cycle (often a quarter), reconciling row counts and key aggregates
Adopt the new query engine before validating the storage layer
questions.explanation: Cloud migrations succeed when run incrementally with reconciliation. Iceberg has explicit migration procedures for Parquet/ORC. A parallel-run window catches semantic differences before cutover. Migrating everything at once is the recipe for missed deadlines and silent data drift. Engine-first / storage-last inverts the dependency: storage choices constrain engines.

questions.prompt: Your stream pipeline runs Flink-on-Kubernetes. Which signals are essential to alert on? (Select all that apply)
options: Consumer lag (per topic, per partition)
Checkpoint duration and failure rate
Job restart count
Number of Kubernetes pods in the cluster total
Watermark progression (e.g., max watermark age behind processing time)
questions.explanation: Lag, checkpoint health, restart count, and watermark advance are the canonical streaming health signals. Total pod count in the cluster is operations-team noise, not a job-level signal. Alerting on watermark progression specifically catches cases where the pipeline looks healthy by other metrics but is silently making no event-time progress.

questions.prompt: Marketplace fraud-ring detection (collusion between buyers and sellers to launder funds). Which signals belong in the feature pipeline? (Select all that apply)
options: Graph features: shared device, IP, payment instrument, or shipping address between buyer/seller
Velocity: number of transactions per pair in last 1h/24h/7d
Anomalous return rates per pair compared to the marketplace baseline
User's astrological sign
Time-of-day pattern matching across pairs (e.g., suspiciously synchronized activity)
questions.explanation: Graph linkage, pair velocity, return-rate anomalies, and synchronized activity are all proven collusion signals. Astrological signs aren't predictive. Fraud ring detection often treats the graph itself as the entity (cluster of related accounts), not individual transactions. Real systems combine these with manual-review queues and labeled chargeback data for supervised model training.

questions.prompt: You discover a 90-day bug in a transformation and must backfill 90 days of corrected data into a warehouse fact table consumed by live BI dashboards. Which approaches LIMIT consumer disruption? (Select all that apply)
options: Backfill into a SEPARATE table and atomically swap pointers (view rename, dbt `ref` swap, or table rename) after validation
DELETE 90 days of data from the live table during business hours and INSERT the corrected rows
Backfill day-by-day into the live table using a partition-level overwrite, allowing readers to see partial progress
Communicate the freeze window and reconciliation plan to BI consumers in advance
Validate fixed-vs-broken row counts and key aggregates before swap
questions.explanation: Atomic-swap, partition-by-partition overwrite, advance communication, and pre-swap validation are all standard. The destructive in-place DELETE+INSERT during business hours is the textbook anti-pattern: dashboards show partial data while it runs. (Note: 'one of the two' between atomic swap and partition overwrite is typical; both can coexist for different table types.)

questions.prompt: A real-estate marketplace lists property addresses worldwide. Which design decisions help analytics + search? (Select all that apply)
options: Normalize structured address components (country, region, city, postal_code, street, unit) into a dedicated `address` table referenced by FK
Geocode addresses to lat/long and store them alongside an H3 / S2 hex index for efficient geo queries
Store full unstructured raw text in a single field and parse on every read
Store country / region codes using ISO standards (e.g., ISO 3166-1 alpha-2) rather than free-text country names
Use the postal code as the primary key
questions.explanation: Structured components + ISO codes + a hex index is the canonical real-estate model. Parsing unstructured text on every read is brittle and slow. Postal codes are not globally unique and not primary key material. (Many warehouses keep BOTH the parsed components AND the original raw text for audit, but rely on parsed columns for queries.)

questions.prompt: A SOC2-audited fintech audits 'who accessed what data when'. Which design choices make audit-log integrity defensible? (Select all that apply)
options: Write audit logs to an append-only store with object-lock / WORM retention so no one (including admins) can modify them
Include a hash chain or signature so any tampering is detectable
Centralize logs across services into a tamper-evident pipeline with a separate access boundary from the systems being audited
Allow application code to delete its own audit entries when records become noisy
Periodically reconcile audit logs against authentication / IAM events for cross-validation
questions.explanation: WORM storage, hash chains, separation of duties, and cross-source reconciliation are the standard audit-log integrity controls. Allowing app code to delete its own entries is the audit-fail anti-pattern. Auditors will explicitly ask 'who can modify these logs?' and 'show me proof none were modified'.

questions.prompt: An impression-deduplication system must handle replays where the same impression event ID may be re-emitted hours later. Which design choices ensure correct counts? (Select all that apply)
options: Stable producer-assigned event_id used as the dedup key end-to-end
Deduplication state per event_id with a TTL (e.g., 48h) so memory doesn't grow forever
Idempotent MERGE into the impressions aggregate so re-emit is a no-op
Random UUIDs assigned by each downstream consumer
Aggregations that depend on processing-time so duplicates near boundaries are absorbed without notice
questions.explanation: Producer-assigned event_id + bounded dedup window + idempotent MERGE is the proven path. Consumer-assigned UUIDs vary across replays and cannot dedupe. Processing-time aggregations don't dedupe—they just hide the problem in arbitrary bucketing.

questions.prompt: Design a Postgres schema for a SaaS feature-flag service with per-tenant overrides. Tables needed: `flag`, `flag_rule`, `flag_tenant_override`. Each flag has a name, default_state, and an ordered list of rules (e.g., 'enable for cohort X'). Each tenant may override a flag's state and/or rule. Provide CREATE TABLE statements with appropriate PKs/FKs/indexes.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE flag (
  flag_id          BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  name             VARCHAR(128) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  description      TEXT,
  default_state    BOOLEAN      NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
  created_at       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  is_archived      BOOLEAN      NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE
);

CREATE TABLE flag_rule (
  rule_id          BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  flag_id          BIGINT       NOT NULL REFERENCES flag(flag_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  priority         INT          NOT NULL,                   -- evaluation order, lower = first
  rule_type        VARCHAR(32)  NOT NULL,                   -- 'cohort', 'percentage', 'attribute_match'
  payload          JSONB        NOT NULL,                   -- rule-specific parameters
  result_state     BOOLEAN      NOT NULL,                   -- state when this rule matches
  is_active        BOOLEAN      NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
  created_at       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  UNIQUE (flag_id, priority)
);
CREATE INDEX ix_flag_rule_flag_active ON flag_rule (flag_id) WHERE is_active;

CREATE TABLE flag_tenant_override (
  flag_id          BIGINT       NOT NULL REFERENCES flag(flag_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  tenant_id        BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  override_state   BOOLEAN,                                 -- NULL means 'inherit, but override the rule set'
  override_rule_id BIGINT       REFERENCES flag_rule(rule_id),
  created_at       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  PRIMARY KEY (flag_id, tenant_id),
  CHECK (override_state IS NOT NULL OR override_rule_id IS NOT NULL)
);
CREATE INDEX ix_flag_tenant_override_tenant ON flag_tenant_override (tenant_id);
key_points: Three normalized tables: `flag`, `flag_rule`, `flag_tenant_override`
`flag.default_state` provides a safe default if no rules match
`flag_rule.priority` with UNIQUE (flag_id, priority) defines evaluation order deterministically
`flag_rule.payload` JSONB allows different rule types without proliferating columns
`flag_tenant_override` PK (flag_id, tenant_id) gives O(1) lookup per evaluation
Override can set either a state OR a rule reference (CHECK constraint enforces at least one)
Partial index on active rules avoids loading retired rules during evaluation
tenant_id index supports 'show me all overrides for tenant X' admin queries
CASCADE on flag deletion cleans up orphan rules/overrides

questions.prompt: Design a Postgres `subscription` schema supporting proration. Tables: `plan`, `subscription`, `subscription_event` (events log: created, upgraded, downgraded, paused, resumed, canceled), and `invoice_line_item` (per billing-cycle line items computed from events). Show CREATE TABLEs with PKs/FKs and a comment on how proration is computed.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE plan (
  plan_id           BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  plan_code         VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  name              VARCHAR(128) NOT NULL,
  cycle_days        INT          NOT NULL,                    -- 30 = monthly, 365 = annual
  unit_price_cents  INT          NOT NULL,
  currency          CHAR(3)      NOT NULL,                    -- ISO 4217
  is_active         BOOLEAN      NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
  created_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE subscription (
  subscription_id   BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  customer_id       BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  current_plan_id   BIGINT       NOT NULL REFERENCES plan(plan_id),
  current_state     VARCHAR(16)  NOT NULL,                    -- 'active','paused','canceled'
  current_cycle_start TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  current_cycle_end   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  created_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
CREATE INDEX ix_subscription_customer ON subscription (customer_id);

CREATE TABLE subscription_event (
  event_id          BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  subscription_id   BIGINT       NOT NULL REFERENCES subscription(subscription_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  event_type        VARCHAR(32)  NOT NULL,                    -- 'created','upgraded','downgraded','paused','resumed','canceled'
  from_plan_id      BIGINT       REFERENCES plan(plan_id),
  to_plan_id        BIGINT       REFERENCES plan(plan_id),
  effective_at      TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,                    -- when the change takes effect
  created_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  reason            TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX ix_subscription_event_sub_effective ON subscription_event (subscription_id, effective_at);

CREATE TABLE invoice_line_item (
  line_item_id      BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  subscription_id   BIGINT       NOT NULL REFERENCES subscription(subscription_id),
  invoice_id        BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  cycle_start       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,                    -- the cycle this line item belongs to
  cycle_end         TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,
  segment_start     TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,                    -- the sub-period within the cycle
  segment_end       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,                    -- segment_end > segment_start
  plan_id           BIGINT       NOT NULL REFERENCES plan(plan_id),
  amount_cents      INT          NOT NULL,                    -- prorated: plan_price * (segment_days / cycle_days)
  currency          CHAR(3)      NOT NULL,
  created_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
CREATE INDEX ix_invoice_line_item_invoice ON invoice_line_item (invoice_id);

-- Proration: when a customer upgrades on day D of a 30-day cycle starting S,
-- close the existing line item at D (amount = old_price * (D-S)/30),
-- open a new line item from D to S+30 (amount = new_price * (30-(D-S))/30).
-- All proration logic operates over `subscription_event` between (cycle_start, cycle_end).
key_points: Plan stores cycle_days + unit_price + currency for proration math
Subscription tracks CURRENT state (denormalized for read speed) plus cycle boundaries
subscription_event is APPEND-ONLY — every change is auditable
invoice_line_item models the SEGMENTS within a cycle: each upgrade/downgrade closes one and opens another
amount_cents stores the prorated amount (price * segment_days / cycle_days)
Indexes on (subscription_id, effective_at) make 'events within this cycle' queries fast
Cycle boundaries belong on subscription so renewal/billing scheduler doesn't need to recompute
Comment block in the schema makes the proration rule discoverable to readers
currency / cycle_days come from the plan, but copied into line items to survive plan-price changes

questions.prompt: Design a Postgres schema for an OpenTable-style restaurant reservation system. Must prevent two parties from booking the same physical table over the same time range. Tables: `restaurant`, `table_resource`, `reservation`. Use exclusion constraints (NOT just app-level locking) to enforce non-overlap.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS btree_gist;

CREATE TABLE restaurant (
  restaurant_id    BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  name             VARCHAR(256) NOT NULL,
  timezone         VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,                  -- IANA tz, e.g., 'America/New_York'
  city             VARCHAR(128),
  created_at       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE table_resource (
  table_id         BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  restaurant_id    BIGINT       NOT NULL REFERENCES restaurant(restaurant_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  label            VARCHAR(32)  NOT NULL,                  -- '4-top by window'
  seats            SMALLINT     NOT NULL CHECK (seats > 0),
  is_active        BOOLEAN      NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,
  UNIQUE (restaurant_id, label)
);
CREATE INDEX ix_table_resource_restaurant ON table_resource (restaurant_id);

CREATE TABLE reservation (
  reservation_id   BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  table_id         BIGINT       NOT NULL REFERENCES table_resource(table_id),
  party_id         BIGINT       NOT NULL,                  -- FK to guest/party table omitted
  party_size       SMALLINT     NOT NULL CHECK (party_size > 0),
  reservation_range TSTZRANGE   NOT NULL,                  -- [start_ts, end_ts) inclusive-exclusive
  status           VARCHAR(16)  NOT NULL DEFAULT 'confirmed',  -- 'confirmed','seated','canceled','no_show'
  created_at       TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  canceled_at      TIMESTAMPTZ,
  -- Prevent overlapping confirmed reservations on the same physical table:
  EXCLUDE USING gist (
    table_id WITH =,
    reservation_range WITH &&
  ) WHERE (status IN ('confirmed','seated'))
);
CREATE INDEX ix_reservation_table_range ON reservation USING gist (table_id, reservation_range);
CREATE INDEX ix_reservation_party ON reservation (party_id);

-- Insertion path: the database refuses any reservation whose range overlaps an existing
-- confirmed/seated reservation on the same table; the loser gets a unique-violation that the
-- application maps to HTTP 409 Conflict.
key_points: btree_gist extension required to combine equality (table_id) with overlap (range) in a GiST index
TSTZRANGE on (start_ts, end_ts) is the correct type for non-overlapping time intervals
EXCLUDE USING gist with the WHERE clause means only ACTIVE reservations participate in non-overlap
Canceled / no-show reservations are kept for history but don't block new bookings
GiST index on (table_id, reservation_range) makes the constraint check fast
Timezone on restaurant, not on reservation — reservation_range is timestamptz which is UTC under the hood
Insertion losers get a clean DB-level error, mapped to a 409 in the API
party_id indexed for 'all reservations for this guest'
Status enum supports the seat / no-show lifecycle

questions.prompt: Design a Postgres double-entry ledger for a fintech wallet. Every transaction MUST balance to zero across debits and credits. Tables: `account`, `ledger_entry`, `transaction`. Provide schema and a check or trigger that enforces the zero-sum invariant per transaction.
key_points: Three tables: account, transaction, ledger_entry — entries reference both account and transaction
Direction column ('D'/'C') + positive amount avoids negative-number sign confusion
Currency column on both account and ledger_entry; in a multi-currency system, NEVER mix currencies within a single transaction (or model FX as an explicit conversion-account transaction)
DEFERRED constraint trigger lets the application insert all entries within a transaction and validate at COMMIT, not per-row
Zero-sum invariant: sum of debits = sum of credits per transaction
Reversal is modeled as a NEW transaction, not a DELETE — the ledger is append-only for auditability
external_ref on transaction provides idempotency for upstream retries
Indexes on (account_id, created_at) support 'balance as of T' and statement queries
occurred_at vs recorded_at preserves business time vs system time

questions.prompt: Design a partitioned + clustered Iceberg fact table for ride-share TRIPS. Volume: ~80M rows/day, retained 36 months. Common predicates: filter by `trip_date`, by `driver_id`, by H3 cell of pickup. Provide the CREATE TABLE statement (Iceberg dialect or generic columns + partition spec + clustering hint).
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE fact_trip (
  trip_id            STRING        NOT NULL,                   -- natural key (UUID from app)
  rider_id           BIGINT        NOT NULL,
  driver_id          BIGINT        NOT NULL,
  vehicle_id         BIGINT,
  product_type       STRING,                                    -- 'standard','xl','black','pool'
  request_ts         TIMESTAMP     NOT NULL,                    -- when rider requested
  pickup_ts          TIMESTAMP,
  dropoff_ts         TIMESTAMP,
  pickup_h3_r9       STRING        NOT NULL,                    -- H3 resolution 9 (≈74K cells/sq km)
  dropoff_h3_r9      STRING,
  pickup_lat         DOUBLE        NOT NULL,
  pickup_lng         DOUBLE        NOT NULL,
  distance_km        DECIMAL(8,3),
  duration_sec       INT,
  base_fare_cents    BIGINT,
  surge_multiplier   DECIMAL(4,2),
  tip_cents          BIGINT,
  total_paid_cents   BIGINT,
  driver_payout_cents BIGINT,
  status             STRING        NOT NULL,                    -- 'completed','canceled','no_show'
  cancel_reason      STRING,
  trip_date          DATE          NOT NULL,                    -- truncated request_ts in driver-local tz
  ingested_at        TIMESTAMP     NOT NULL
)
USING iceberg
PARTITIONED BY (
  days(trip_date),
  bucket(16, driver_id)
)
TBLPROPERTIES (
  'write.target-file-size-bytes' = '536870912',          -- 512 MB target
  'write.parquet.compression-codec' = 'zstd',
  'write.distribution-mode' = 'hash',
  'sort-order' = 'pickup_h3_r9'                          -- file-level sort for geo predicates
);

-- Optional secondary cluster (engine-dependent): cluster by (driver_id, pickup_h3_r9)
-- for queries filtering by driver and pickup neighborhood.
key_points: Partition by days(trip_date) for pruning by date filters (most common analytics filter)
Bucket by driver_id (16 buckets) to avoid skew when a few drivers do many trips, while supporting driver-level filtering
H3 r9 column for geo joins / hex-aggregations (Uber's published pattern)
Sort order on pickup_h3_r9 makes geographic queries skip irrelevant files
Target file size ~512MB avoids small-files problem at this scale
ZSTD compression is the modern default for analytics workloads (better ratio than snappy, faster than gzip)
Distribution mode 'hash' on partition columns reduces shuffle on writes
trip_date computed in driver-local tz so the date pruning matches business expectations (a 23:30 trip in NY belongs to that NY day, not UTC the next day)
Status + cancel_reason support the 'cancelled trip' analytics without separate tables

questions.prompt: Design a Postgres `webhook_delivery` schema. The service receives outbound webhook send-requests and must retry with exponential backoff on failure, give up after N attempts, and let consumers query 'all failed deliveries for endpoint X in the last day'. Include retry math and dead-letter handling.
questions.model_answer:
CREATE TABLE webhook_delivery (
  delivery_id       BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
  endpoint_id       BIGINT       NOT NULL,                       -- FK to webhook_endpoint(s) elsewhere
  event_id          VARCHAR(128) NOT NULL,                       -- the upstream business event being notified
  event_type        VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
  payload_jsonb     JSONB        NOT NULL,
  attempt_count     INT          NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  max_attempts      INT          NOT NULL DEFAULT 8,             -- 2^8 minutes ≈ 4 hours max backoff
  status            VARCHAR(16)  NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',     -- 'pending','succeeded','failed','dead'
  last_attempt_at   TIMESTAMPTZ,
  next_attempt_at   TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),         -- scheduler picks rows where next_attempt_at <= now
  last_http_status  SMALLINT,
  last_error        TEXT,
  created_at        TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  completed_at      TIMESTAMPTZ,                                  -- terminal state timestamp
  UNIQUE (endpoint_id, event_id)                                  -- idempotency: don't re-deliver same event to same endpoint
);

CREATE INDEX ix_webhook_pending ON webhook_delivery (next_attempt_at)
  WHERE status = 'pending';
CREATE INDEX ix_webhook_endpoint_status_time ON webhook_delivery (endpoint_id, status, last_attempt_at DESC);

-- Backoff schedule (computed by worker after each failed attempt):
--   next_attempt_at = now() + INTERVAL '1 minute' * power(2, attempt_count) + random_jitter(0..30s)
-- After max_attempts, status -> 'dead' (the row is the dead-letter record itself).
--
-- Worker query (each tick):
-- SELECT * FROM webhook_delivery
-- WHERE status='pending' AND next_attempt_at <= now()
-- ORDER BY next_attempt_at LIMIT 100
-- FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED;
key_points: Status enum: pending / succeeded / failed (retryable) / dead (terminal)
next_attempt_at + partial index makes the scheduler query a single index range scan, even at billions of rows
UNIQUE (endpoint_id, event_id) ensures we don't enqueue duplicates if the same upstream event fires twice
Exponential backoff math documented in the schema for maintainers
Jitter on backoff avoids thundering-herd retry storms after an endpoint outage
FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED is the canonical 'job queue on Postgres' pattern: many workers can pull non-overlapping batches
(endpoint_id, status, last_attempt_at) index supports 'failed deliveries for endpoint X' admin queries
Dead row is its own dead-letter — no separate DLQ table is required for most scales
attempt_count + max_attempts let endpoints configure their own retry budget

questions.prompt: Design a Postgres `comment_thread` schema for a Reddit-style nested comments system. Must support: fast retrieval of all comments under a post, traversal of replies, and ordering by score. Consider materialized-path or closure-table approaches.
key_points: Hybrid model: parent_comment_id (for direct walks) + ltree path (for subtree queries) + depth (for indent rendering without recursion)
ltree GiST index makes 'all descendants of comment X' a single index range scan, not a recursive CTE
Score column is denormalized (sum from a vote table) and indexed to support 'top comments first' ordering
post_id is indexed first in composite indexes so all queries are post-scoped first — critical at scale
is_deleted flag (not row delete) preserves thread continuity when a parent comment is removed
depth field stored at insert time avoids recomputing it on every render
Partial index on parent_comment_id WHERE NOT NULL skips top-level comments and speeds reply walks
Trade-off vs closure-table: ltree is simpler in Postgres, no extra junction table; closure-table is portable across DBMSes

questions.prompt: Design a Postgres `audit_log` table for SOC2-style change auditing across many entity types. Must support: which entity changed, who changed it, what fields changed, immutable retention. Provide schema with appropriate indexes and a comment on how to enforce immutability.
key_points: Polymorphic (entity_type, entity_id) columns rather than per-entity audit tables — simpler operational model
field_diffs as JSONB captures the before/after of each changed field without column-level audit
Hash-chain columns (prev_log_hash, this_log_hash) make tampering detectable
Immutability is enforced by REVOKING UPDATE/DELETE, not by application convention
Multiple composite indexes match the three primary audit queries: 'what happened to entity X?', 'what did user Y do?', 'all writes in tenant Z'
tenant_id partitioning column for multi-tenant scoping in RLS or per-tenant retention
Partition by occurred_at month enables retention pruning without rewriting active data
Separate schema/database recommendation enforces SEPARATION OF DUTIES — auditors look at a system the audited app can't write to twice
request_id ties audit events back to a single trace / request for cross-system reconstruction

questions.prompt: You're the on-call lead. Your fraud-detection pipeline went from 2-minute lag to 6-hour lag overnight. No deploys in 48h. The pipeline reads Kafka -> Flink -> writes to Postgres + scores via an internal model service. Walk through your incident response in 6-8 steps. End with a runbook decision: when do you escalate vs continue solo?
questions.model_answer:
**Step 1 — Acknowledge + page status.** Take primary on-call ack within minutes. Set a status update channel (incident-specific Slack, page incident commander if 6h lag is beyond your team's solo bar). Communicate to the fraud-ops user channel that lag is elevated and what you currently know.

**Step 2 — Isolate the bottleneck via fundamental signals.** Look at four numbers in parallel: Kafka consumer lag per partition, Flink job restart count + checkpoint duration, Postgres write rate + replica lag, model-service p95/p99 latency. Lag growing linearly with idle consumer CPU = downstream-bound (Postgres or model service). Lag bursty + restarts = upstream or job-level problem. Lag growing with high consumer CPU = pipeline CPU-bound (rare without a deploy).

**Step 3 — Check the obvious externals.** Has a cloud provider issued an advisory for your region? Did a dependent service have an incident overnight? Did infra autoscale (or fail to autoscale) overnight? Often the answer to a 'no deploys' regression is a silent capacity event (a node lost, autoscaling group cap hit, IAM role rotation expiring).

**Step 4 — Probe the slowest hop.** If model-service p99 is up 10x, you've found the bottleneck. Hit `/debug/profile` or equivalent, check GC, check whether the model was reloaded with a slower variant. If Postgres write rate is down, check connection pool exhaustion, lock waits, autovacuum running on the destination table, or replica failover that promoted a slower replica.

**Step 5 — Make the smallest reversible change first.** If model-service is slow and was the latest variant deployed yesterday (even if you said 'no deploys', sometimes a model artifact is hot-swapped without code deploy), roll back to the previous variant. If Postgres is locked by a long autovacuum, raise vacuum cost limit or pin the work. Do NOT scale up Flink unless you've confirmed the bottleneck is in Flink.

**Step 6 — Mitigate to user impact, then fix root cause.** If lag is the user-visible problem and you cannot find the cause in 30 minutes, mitigate: switch fraud scoring to a fallback rules-only path so cards stop being blocked by stale risk; or buy time by spinning up a parallel pipeline on fresh offsets and abandoning the backlog (only if leadership signs off on losing history). Fixing root cause without mitigation is heroic but not the right priority order.

**Step 7 — Reconcile and validate.** Once lag is back down, verify three things: (1) row counts in the destination from the bad window match expectations, (2) no events were lost (compare Kafka offsets read vs rows written), (3) downstream alerts have cleared. Backfill any computed-state windows that were processed under bad conditions if they're used in operating decisions.

**Step 8 — Postmortem.** Within 48h, document: what was the customer impact (transactions delayed or under-scored), what the trigger was, what the detection delay was, why monitoring didn't fire earlier (often watermark progression isn't alerted on while lag is, and these can diverge), and what changes prevent recurrence. Track action items in a tracked queue with owners.

**Runbook decision — when to escalate.** Escalate to incident commander + page additional engineers if: (a) lag exceeds 4h with no identified cause within 30 minutes, (b) customer impact is dollar-quantifiable and growing, (c) you've made the smallest mitigation and lag still grows, or (d) the suspected cause is in a system owned by another team. Solo on-call is correct when the cause is identified within 30 minutes and reversible.
key_points: Starts with acknowledgement + comms BEFORE diving into root-cause (status discipline)
Uses fundamental signals (lag, CPU, restart count, downstream latency) to isolate the bottleneck rather than guessing
Considers 'no deploys' might still mean 'model artifact swap' or 'config rotation' — explicit external-cause check
Picks the SMALLEST REVERSIBLE CHANGE first (rollback model, not scale Flink) before bigger interventions
Mitigates user impact (fallback rules path, parallel pipeline on fresh offsets) before completing root-cause analysis
Reconciliation step: counts match, no data lost, dependent alerts cleared
Postmortem within 48h with concrete prevention action items, not vague learnings
Explicit escalation criteria — when to call for help vs continue solo
Mentions watermark vs lag distinction — a subtle stream-engineer-only signal that often goes unmonitored

questions.prompt: Your Series-B startup needs an MLOps platform for 4 data scientists who work on tabular models (churn, LTV, fraud) and one team training small LLMs for product features. Budget is $5K/month all-in for the platform (compute, tools, storage). Sketch the architecture, the build-vs-buy choices, and how you'd phase it over 6 months.
questions.model_answer:
**Constraints first.** $5K/month means you cannot afford a turnkey commercial MLOps suite (Databricks ML, SageMaker w/ heavy GPUs, etc.) running 24/7. Four data scientists also means the platform must be lean enough that you don't need a dedicated platform engineer. Spend on COMPUTE for training, save on tools by adopting open-source where mature.

**Architecture sketch.**

*Storage layer.* S3 (or GCS) as the data lake. ~$23/TB/mo standard. Land both raw training data and model artifacts here. No fancy lakehouse needed yet — use Parquet with explicit folder conventions and dbt for any transforms. Budget allocation: ~$200/mo for 5–10TB.

*Feature / training data layer.* dbt-managed marts in a small Snowflake account (or Postgres + materialized views if Snowflake is too expensive). Feature lookups for offline training read from the marts; for online inference, replicate the small features into Redis or DynamoDB. Skip a full feature store (Feast, Tecton) until at least two models are in production with shared features. Budget: ~$1,200–1,500/mo for Snowflake at this volume.

*Training compute.* Two tiers: (1) for tabular models, ephemeral EC2/GCP spot instances launched per training job via a thin orchestrator (Prefect or Dagster or just GitHub Actions self-hosted runners on spot). Tabular models train in minutes-to-hours on CPU; don't pay for GPU on these. (2) For small LLMs (under 7B params), a single A10 or L4 GPU in a spot pool, scheduled via the same orchestrator. Budget: ~$800–1,200/mo elastic.

*Experiment tracking + model registry.* MLflow open-source, self-hosted on a small EC2 + Postgres + S3 artifact store. Free except the small EC2 (~$50/mo) and Postgres ($30/mo RDS micro). Tracks experiments, parameters, metrics, and registered model versions. Open-source matches commercial here for one-team use.

*Serving.* Two patterns: (a) Batch scoring as a daily/hourly job that writes scores to the warehouse — covers churn + LTV. Cheap and simple, no separate infra. (b) Real-time scoring for fraud + LLM features: a lightweight FastAPI service on EC2 or Cloud Run that loads the registered model. ~$200–400/mo. Skip a full model server (BentoML, Seldon, KServe) until you have at least three concurrent real-time models with versioning needs.

*Monitoring.* Datadog or Grafana Cloud free tier for service metrics; a small daily job emits model-input drift and prediction-distribution metrics to the same warehouse as training data, so the data team can write dbt tests on the model side. Skip a dedicated ML monitoring SaaS until there's evidence of real drift causing real losses.

*CI / orchestration.* GitHub Actions for CI; orchestrator (Prefect Cloud free tier or self-hosted) for scheduled training/scoring jobs. ~$0–200/mo.

**Build vs buy summary.** BUY: warehouse (Snowflake) because building it eats engineer-months; observability (Datadog/Grafana) because rolling your own is a tax forever. BUILD on open source: MLflow tracker/registry, lightweight model server, drift monitoring as dbt tests. SKIP for now: full feature store, model gateway/A-B testing platform, dedicated experiment-tracking SaaS.

**Phasing over 6 months.**
- *Month 1.* Stand up S3 + Snowflake + dbt + MLflow + GitHub Actions. One reference pipeline (churn) end-to-end: data ingest -> features -> train -> register -> batch-score -> dashboard.
- *Month 2.* Replicate the pattern for LTV. Standardize a project template so new models follow the same shape. Add basic drift checks on inputs.
- *Month 3.* Fraud real-time path: FastAPI service, Redis feature replication, latency SLO. Wire prediction logging back to the warehouse for monitoring.
- *Month 4.* LLM training pipeline: spot GPU pool, MLflow LLM-suitable logging, eval harness. Single LLM in product (a feature like text classification or summarization).
- *Month 5.* Productionize the LLM-serving path: model gateway between product and the model so version swaps don't require product redeploys. Add cost guardrails per-job.
- *Month 6.* Maturity work: shared feature definitions across models, deprecate one-off scripts, write the runbooks. Re-evaluate whether the volume justifies graduating to a feature store or a commercial MLOps suite.

**What goes wrong if you skip phasing.** Most startups in this size class try to build the 'full platform' first (feature store, model gateway, A/B platform) before they have a single model in production. The result is six months of platform building and zero models earning value. Always: model #1 end-to-end on the simplest stack first, then platform the patterns.
key_points: Starts from the BUDGET CONSTRAINT and explicitly names what it forces (no turnkey suite, no dedicated platform engineer)
Concrete budget allocation per layer that sums to ~$5K/mo
Splits storage, feature layer, training, registry, serving, monitoring, CI as distinct layers with specific tools
Names explicit BUY (warehouse, observability) vs BUILD on OSS (MLflow, model server) vs SKIP-FOR-NOW (feature store, gateway)
Differentiates batch scoring (most tabular use cases) from real-time (fraud, LLM) and uses simpler infra for batch
Two-tier training compute: CPU spot for tabular, GPU spot for small LLM — doesn't pay for GPU on tabular
6-month phasing starts with ONE end-to-end model, then templates, then expand to real-time, then LLM, then platform maturity
Calls out the anti-pattern of building the full platform before having a model in production
Pragmatic recommendations to use OSS for tools where commercial doesn't offer enough value at this scale

questions.prompt: Pinterest needs to backfill 7 years of pin-impression events (~2 trillion events) from cold storage into a new attribution model. Current production: 12B events/day live ingestion into Iceberg via Flink + Kafka. The new attribution model lives in a separate dbt project. Sketch how to backfill without disrupting current production AND while keeping the cloud bill bounded. Walk through the design in 6–8 stages.
questions.model_answer:
**Stage 1 — Quantify and scope.** 2 trillion events at, say, 600 bytes after compression = ~1.2PB. Cold storage egress + compute at full speed could easily exceed seven figures if done naively. Get a written cost ceiling from finance before doing anything irreversible. Decide: do we need ALL 7 years, or does the attribution model demand only the last 24 months? Pruning at the front halves the bill.

**Stage 2 — Build an ISOLATED backfill stack.** Stand up: (a) a separate Iceberg table `pin_impression_backfill` with the same schema and partitioning as production (`days(event_date), bucket(N, user_id)` or equivalent); (b) a SEPARATE Spark or Flink batch job (not the live streaming job) that reads from cold storage S3 + writes to the backfill table; (c) its own warehouse / cluster / namespace so it cannot starve the live pipeline. The goal is hard isolation: nothing the backfill does can degrade live ingestion.

**Stage 3 — Stage cold data to warm tier first.** Glacier Deep Archive retrieval is hours and per-byte expensive. Bulk-restore historical partitions to S3 Standard or Glacier Instant Retrieval in MONTH-sized batches, process them, then expire back to cold. Don't restore all 7 years at once or you'll be paying S3 Standard rates for petabytes for the duration of the backfill.

**Stage 4 — Process in time-ordered chunks with explicit checkpointing.** Backfill OLDEST to NEWEST one month at a time. After each month, validate row counts vs the source archive's manifest, sample-check a small partition, and CHECKPOINT progress (e.g., write a partition-level high-water mark to a state table). On failure, you resume from the last completed month, not the start. Reverse order is also defensible (newest first means the attribution model can start incremental work earlier), but newest-first means you commit to either backfilling everything or accepting an awkward gap.

**Stage 5 — Use cheap compute aggressively.** Spot/preemptible instances are 70% cheaper. Backfills are idempotent and re-runnable, so spot reclamation only costs you the running task, not the data. Pick a cluster size that finishes a month in 3–6 hours so you're sensitive to cost-per-month and can tune. Use ZSTD compression on the output to keep the warm copy small.

**Stage 6 — Reconcile against the live pipeline at the overlap.** Backfill writes to a separate table. Where the backfill window overlaps with live production (say, the most recent 30 days), reconcile counts per partition + sample-validate a small set of pin_ids. The overlap PROVES the backfill pipeline produces the same answer as live for the same input — if it doesn't, the rest of the 7 years is suspect.

**Stage 7 — Atomically swap or union.** Two paths to make the data visible to the new attribution model: (a) a one-time MERGE INTO the production table for old partitions where production has no data — simple but risks accidentally touching live partitions; (b) keep the backfill table separate and have the dbt attribution model UNION the historical table with the live one in its source layer. Pinterest-scale teams typically prefer (b) because it keeps the backfill reversible: if a defect is found, you drop the historical table and re-run, with zero risk to live data.

**Stage 8 — Decommission cleanly.** Once the attribution model is validated end-to-end on backfilled + live data, you can either: (1) keep the historical table as a permanent source (cheapest, no further migration); (2) merge into a single production table during a planned maintenance window if downstream tooling can't handle the union. Update the data catalog, document the backfill in a runbook, and define the retention policy for the warm-tier copy created during processing.

**Cost-control levers throughout.** Process in month-sized batches so you can stop and renegotiate after each batch. Always restore-process-expire rather than restore-all. Default to spot. Compress to ZSTD. Reconcile incrementally so a defect doesn't waste a full re-run.

**What goes wrong if you skip the isolated stack.** The most common failure mode is teams sharing the live Kafka/Flink cluster between backfill and production, then production degrades or fails when the backfill spikes. Hard isolation at the cluster level is non-negotiable.
key_points: Quantifies the scope (~1.2PB, billions of dollars potential if naive) and demands a cost ceiling before starting
Asks whether ALL 7 years are needed or whether the model only needs a shorter window — pruning at the front saves more than any optimization
Isolated backfill stack: separate table, separate cluster, separate warehouse so backfill cannot disrupt production
Staged cold-warm-cold restoration pattern to avoid paying for petabytes of warm storage for months
Processes oldest-first in month-sized batches with explicit per-batch checkpointing for restartability
Uses spot / preemptible compute aggressively because backfills are idempotent
Reconciles against the live pipeline at the overlap window — the overlap proves correctness
Prefers UNION over MERGE for the cutover because it keeps the backfill reversible and isolates blast radius
Plans for the decommission step, not just the active backfill phase
Calls out the anti-pattern of sharing infrastructure between backfill and production (the #1 source of incidents on this kind of project)

questions.prompt: You inherit a 2-year-old data platform: Airflow on a single EC2, Postgres data warehouse (8TB and growing), ~400 dbt models, Looker, no schema registry, no data contracts, no PII tagging, 4 outages in the last quarter (mostly Airflow OOM, two from a dbt model that grew unbounded). Engineering leadership asks for a 6-month modernization plan that survives a $300K/yr budget and a team of 3 engineers. Sketch the plan in phases.
questions.model_answer:
**Frame first.** Budget $300K/yr and 3 engineers means roughly one platform engineer's salary equivalent for vendor tools and roughly two engineers' time for migrations. You cannot replatform everything in 6 months; pick targets where the pain-to-effort ratio is highest. The four outages in a quarter tell you the immediate goal is RELIABILITY, not feature parity with a state-of-the-art platform.

**Phase 0 (week 1) — Triage and stabilize.** Don't start a migration with the platform actively on fire. Move Airflow to a larger instance (or two with high availability) immediately. Add per-DAG resource caps so a runaway dbt model can't OOM the orchestrator. Add disk monitoring on Postgres. Triage the dbt model that grew unbounded — most likely a missing `is_incremental()` guard. These are days of work, not months, and they bring the operational MTBF up while you plan.

**Phase 1 (months 1–2) — Reliability infrastructure.** Three workstreams in parallel: (1) migrate Airflow to a managed offering (MWAA, Cloud Composer, or Astronomer) or to a modern orchestrator (Dagster, Prefect) on managed infrastructure. Self-hosting Airflow with a 3-person team is a poor use of time. (2) Tighten dbt with model-level checks: required-tests on every model, freshness sources, partition checks via dbt-utils. (3) Stand up basic observability: a `dbt run` log table, alert on any model running >2x its baseline duration, alert on data-freshness SLO breaches.

**Phase 2 (months 2–4) — Plan the warehouse migration, don't execute it yet.** 8TB Postgres for analytics is fine today but won't be at 30TB. Run a bake-off between Snowflake and BigQuery on the heaviest 5 dashboards: load a sample, replicate the dbt models, measure cost and latency, then pick. The migration itself is Phase 3; in Phase 2 you're building the decision evidence and proving the platform team can stand up the new warehouse on a parallel track without touching production.

**Phase 3 (months 4–6) — Migrate the warehouse incrementally.** CDC from Postgres into the chosen warehouse (Debezium, Fivetran, or the native connector for whatever you picked). Move dbt models in groups: starting with the marts that have the WORST cost or latency in Postgres, since those benefit most. Run BOTH warehouses in parallel for at least one full reporting cycle, reconciling key aggregates. Cut Looker over per-mart as confidence builds, not in a single switch.

**Phase 4 (months 4–6, in parallel with Phase 3) — PII / governance baseline.** Stand up: (a) a tag-based PII registry (column-level tags in dbt's schema.yml or a real catalog like DataHub if budget allows the OSS self-host effort). (b) Row-level / column-level masking policies in the new warehouse, applied via roles. (c) Access reviews for the top-50 sensitive tables. Schema registry can wait beyond 6 months if you don't have streams — don't add what you don't need.

**What NOT to do.**
- Don't introduce a streaming layer just because it's modern. There's no evidence the business needs sub-hour latency, and the operational cost is real.
- Don't introduce a data lakehouse (Iceberg/Delta) alongside the warehouse migration. That's a doubled cognitive load on a 3-person team. Land in the warehouse first; consider lakehouse when warehouse storage cost forces the question.
- Don't pick a 'data mesh' decentralization model. With a 3-person platform team, centralization is the right control structure for the next 2 years.
- Don't try to retire Looker mid-migration. Tooling changes during platform migrations cause double the change-management work and double the risk.

**Cost rough budget.**
- Managed Airflow / orchestrator: ~$30K/yr.
- Warehouse (Snowflake/BigQuery) at 8TB + dbt-heavy workload: ~$120K/yr at this volume with discipline; can balloon to $300K+ without it.
- Data catalog / observability tools if commercial: ~$60K/yr.
- Remaining ~$90K/yr buffer for surprises, training, and a quarter of contractor help on the migration peak.

**Success criteria at month 6.**
- Zero Airflow OOM outages in the last 60 days.
- Top-10 dashboards loading from the new warehouse, others migrated or scheduled.
- Column-level PII tagging on at least the top 100 tables and access reviews completed quarterly.
- A documented runbook for adding a new mart that any of the 3 engineers can follow.
- Total platform spend within the $300K envelope.

**What's deliberately deferred to year 2.** Schema registry (only when streams arrive); lineage tool (only when the catalog content is stable); ML feature platform (only when there are 3+ models); cost attribution at team level (only after the spend baseline stabilizes in the new warehouse).
key_points: Frames the plan against the CONSTRAINTS ($300K, 3 engineers) and explicitly de-scopes work that won't fit
Phase 0 is days-of-work stabilization to stop fires before starting migration — do not migrate from a burning building
Phase 1 moves Airflow OFF self-hosted (3-person team shouldn't run Airflow) and adds tightening / observability on existing stack
Phase 2 runs a deliberate warehouse bake-off rather than picking a vendor based on hype
Phase 3 migrates incrementally with parallel-run reconciliation, not big-bang cutover
Phase 4 adds governance basics (PII tagging, column-level masking, access reviews) on the new warehouse
Explicitly names WHAT NOT TO DO — streaming, lakehouse, mesh, Looker replacement — because that's where 6-month plans typically derail
Provides a realistic cost decomposition that sums within budget
Sets success criteria with measurable outcomes (zero OOMs, top-10 dashboards migrated, PII tagging on top 100 tables)
Explicitly defers year-2 items (schema registry, lineage tool, ML platform, cost attribution) to keep scope honest

questions.prompt: A Kafka consumer writes synchronously to a Postgres OLTP table; the table is the bottleneck. Which design change MOST decouples the consumer's throughput from the database's per-write latency?
options: Increase the number of Kafka partitions
Switch the consumer to ASYNC batched writes — consume a batch from Kafka, fire all inserts without waiting per-row, commit the Kafka offset only after the batch's ACK returns
Reduce the Kafka consumer's `max.poll.records`
Move the consumer pods to faster nodes (more CPU)
questions.explanation:
**Async batched writes** are the canonical pattern. The consumer reads N records, dispatches them all to the DB in parallel (or as a batch INSERT), and only commits the Kafka offset once the DB confirms. While those writes are in flight, the consumer can already be polling the next batch.

- More partitions adds *parallelism* but each consumer thread still blocks on each write — doesn't help if the DB itself is the bottleneck.
- Smaller `max.poll.records` makes the problem *worse* — you commit and poll more often, paying the per-batch overhead more times.
- Faster CPUs are irrelevant: the consumer is in `wait()`, not burning CPU.

The corollary: async writes mean you must handle partial-batch failures explicitly. The Kafka offset can only advance once you know which records made it. Implementations: an in-memory queue with bounded depth + ACK callbacks, or a transactional outbox if you need exactly-once semantics across the consumer and sink.

questions.prompt: A Kafka topic is keyed by `user_id`. One user — call them 'big tenant A' — produces 40% of the volume. Their writes to the downstream sink are slow (their target rows are heavily locked). This is causing the ENTIRE partition that holds A's records to lag, blocking OTHER users in the same partition. What is this called and what fixes it?
options: Cross-partition contention — fix by adding more brokers

Head-of-line blocking — A's slow writes hold up other users' records that share A's partition. Fix: re-key by a finer-grained value (e.g., `hash(user_id, session_id)`), or shunt A's records to a dedicated DLQ/lane processed separately
Consumer rebalance storm — fix by tuning `session.timeout.ms`
Producer back-pressure — fix by enabling idempotent producers
questions.explanation:
**Head-of-line blocking** is the standard term: in a partition (or any FIFO queue), one slow record at the head of the queue stalls everything behind it, even though the other records are healthy. Kafka guarantees in-partition order, so within a partition you can't skip A's records to process B's.

Fixes, in order of operational complexity:

1. **Re-key to a finer grain** so A's volume spreads across many partitions instead of one. Trade-off: you lose strict per-user ordering — accept this if the use case can tolerate it (analytics usually can; financial ledgers usually can't).
2. **Isolate the hot key** into its own dedicated consumer group / topic / pipeline. Big tenants get their own lane. Common at Snowflake, Databricks, Uber.
3. **DLQ on slow-write threshold**: records that don't drain within N seconds get shunted to a retry topic with a slower SLA, so the main pipeline keeps moving.
4. **Per-key worker pool inside the consumer**: dispatch records to N parallel writer threads keyed by something downstream of the Kafka partition. Within one consumer, A's writes don't block B's. (You lose strict partition order; same trade-off as #1.)

More brokers (option 0), rebalance tuning (option 2), and idempotent producers (option 3) are all unrelated: they don't address the fact that one key's slow downstream is blocking other keys in the SAME partition.

questions.prompt: You add a circuit breaker on the consumer: when the sink's p99 write latency exceeds 5s for 30s, the breaker trips and the consumer fast-fails inserts (which then get retried later from a DLQ). The breaker activates during an incident. What happens to the lag?
options: Lag drops to near-zero because the consumer is no longer blocked

Lag continues to grow — the breaker doesn't make data process faster; it just shifts the queue from 'in-flight DB writes' to 'Kafka offset + DLQ depth' so the consumer stays responsive and the sink gets a chance to recover
Lag is unaffected — the breaker only changes error rates, not throughput
Lag spikes higher because retries are now serialized through the DLQ
questions.explanation:
A circuit breaker is **not** a performance optimization. It's a containment pattern: it stops the consumer from making a struggling sink worse (the 'death spiral' where retries DDoS a slow DB). The lag doesn't disappear — it relocates to a place where you can see it:

- **Before the breaker**: lag lives as 'in-flight DB writes' + 'Kafka consumer waiting on COMMIT'. Hard to alert on; looks like 'slow consumer.'
- **After the breaker trips**: lag lives as 'Kafka consumer offset' + 'DLQ depth'. Both are first-class metrics you can chart and SLO on.

The right way to design around this:

1. Circuit-break the sink so the consumer is *honest* about the constraint instead of silently piling up.
2. Failed records land in a retry queue / DLQ — never drop on the floor.
3. Auto-scale consumers AND the sink based on lag-on-source SLO.
4. Accept that 'the lag was visible at the source' is the goal — not 'the lag disappeared.'

Option 0 misunderstands what a breaker does; option 2 is wrong because errors → DLQ → backlog ARE throughput changes; option 3 conflates serialized retries with the breaker's main effect.

questions.prompt: You're designing a Kafka → downstream-sink consumer that must degrade gracefully when the sink slows down. Which patterns are reasonable parts of the design? (Select all that apply.)
options: Async / batched writes so the consumer doesn't block per-record
Bounded in-memory queue between consumer and sink, with the consumer pausing Kafka polling when the queue is full
Naked exponential-backoff retries with no upper bound and no breaker
A sidecar circuit breaker that fast-fails writes when the sink's p99 crosses a threshold; failed records go to a DLQ
A separate per-tenant 'big-customer' lane / topic so one heavy tenant can't head-of-line-block the shared pipeline
Lag-on-source SLO + auto-scaling consumers so backlog drains once the sink recovers
questions.explanation:
All five selected patterns are standard parts of a production-grade Kafka-to-slow-sink pipeline:

- **Async batched writes** decouple per-record sink latency from consumer throughput.
- **Bounded internal queue + pause polling** gives Kafka clean back-pressure and prevents OOM.
- **Circuit breaker + DLQ** stops the death spiral where retries DDoS a struggling sink.
- **Per-tenant lanes** prevent head-of-line blocking from one heavy customer.
- **Lag SLO + auto-scaling** treats lag as the durable signal — accepted, alerted, drained — rather than something to hide.

The **wrong** pattern is option 2 (naked exponential backoff with no breaker / no upper bound). At scale this is the canonical anti-pattern: when the sink is struggling, the consumer hammers it with retries that take *longer* per attempt; the sink slows further; retries pile up; eventually the consumer OOMs or the sink falls over. Always pair retries with a breaker, a budget, and a DLQ.

questions.prompt: Your isolated `pin_impressions_history` shadow table is validated. The live table `pin_impressions_live` is taking 12B events/day. Which Iceberg cutover mechanic is SAFEST for switching the attribution model to read history + live?
options: `DELETE FROM pin_impressions_live WHERE event_date < cutover; INSERT INTO pin_impressions_live SELECT * FROM pin_impressions_history;` — one big atomic transaction
Create a SQL VIEW: `CREATE VIEW pin_impressions AS SELECT * FROM pin_impressions_history UNION ALL SELECT * FROM pin_impressions_live;` and point the attribution model at the view
Run `MERGE INTO pin_impressions_live USING pin_impressions_history ON (id) WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...` to physically copy 1.2PB into live
Run `CALL system.rewrite_data_files('pin_impressions_live')` first to compact, then ingest the backfill through the live pipeline
questions.explanation:
**UNION ALL view** is the safest cutover for three reasons:

1. **No data movement.** The view is metadata-only. You don't rewrite 1.2PB; you don't pay storage twice; the commit is instant.
2. **Fully reversible.** Drop the view and the attribution model sees only the live table again — rollback in seconds.
3. **Isolation preserved.** The live writer (12B/day Flink job) only touches `pin_impressions_live`; the shadow table stays read-only.

**Option 0 (DELETE + INSERT)** is the canonical destructive anti-pattern: if the INSERT fails halfway, you've lost historical data with no atomic rollback. On Iceberg it also spawns position-delete files that bloat metadata and slow live queries.

**Option 2 (MERGE INTO)** works correctly but COPIES 1.2PB into the live table — doubling storage temporarily, generating commit conflicts with the live writer, and taking many hours during which readers see partial state. Acceptable as a LATER one-time consolidation in a maintenance window AFTER the UNION view is stable, NOT as the cutover itself.

**Option 3** (rewrite_data_files) is a compaction operation, useful for hygiene but unrelated to cutover.

The production pattern: ship the UNION view to enable the new attribution model immediately, validate over a few weeks, then OPTIONALLY do a one-time MERGE in a low-traffic window if you need single-table semantics.

questions.prompt: You need to restore 1.2PB from S3 Glacier Deep Archive to feed the backfill. Restoring everything at once requested it in one batch would take days, hit account-level rate limits, and bill at S3 Standard rate for every restored byte for the duration of the restore window. What's the right pattern?
options: Submit a single Bulk-tier restore request for all 1.2PB and wait — Glacier handles the rate-limiting transparently

Restore in monthly WAVES: trigger Glacier restore for one month's S3 prefix → wait 12–48h for Bulk-tier completion → process the wave → expire from S3 Standard → trigger next month. Active-at-once data and cost scale per-wave, not per-total.
Use S3 Storage Lens to pre-warm the data without paying for restore
Skip Glacier and re-collect 7 years of impressions from upstream sources
questions.explanation:
Three properties of Glacier Deep Archive force wave restoration at this scale:

1. **Restored objects pay Standard storage rate** for as long as the restore window persists (`Days` parameter in the RestoreObject call; minimum 1 day, often configured to 5–14 for processing slack). 1.2PB × Standard rate × 14 days = ~ $300K *just* for the storage during the restore window, before any processing.
2. **Account-level rate-limiting** caps how many concurrent restore requests you can submit. Submitting millions at once queues them against your account quota for hours.
3. **Bulk tier** (the cheapest at ~$0.0025/GB) takes 12–48h per request. Standard tier is 3–5h; Expedited is 1–5 min but costs ~30× more.

Wave restoration: process months in sequence so active-at-once volume stays small. Use Bulk tier; explicitly set a short restore window (say 3 days, just enough to process the wave); kick off the next wave when the current one is in flight. Total cost scales with WAVE SIZE × NUMBER OF WAVES × per-GB retrieval, not 1.2PB × storage-window-days.

Option 0 (single batch) is the cost disaster from the question's premise. Option 2 (Storage Lens) is an inventory analysis tool, not a retrieval mechanism. Option 3 abandons the requirement (the historical data only exists in Glacier).

Bonus rule of thumb: at petabyte scale, request the SHORTEST restore window your processing pipeline can handle. Every extra day of restore window = ~$20K in Standard storage for 1PB.

questions.prompt: Which approach to applying a 7-year backfill is MOST dangerous to the live production system? (All four have been proposed in real meetings.)
options: Build a separate shadow table, validate against the live-overlap window, then expose via a UNION ALL view
Restore Glacier in monthly waves and process slice-by-slice on an isolated Spark cluster running on spot instances
Run `DELETE FROM pin_impressions_live WHERE event_date < cutover; INSERT INTO pin_impressions_live SELECT * FROM shadow_table;` against the production table as a single big transaction
Run the backfill at 1/10th rate through the EXISTING live Flink pipeline, accepting that live ingest lag will grow temporarily
questions.explanation:
Two of the four options are dangerous (#2 and #3). The MOST dangerous is **#2: DELETE+INSERT on the live table**.

Failure modes:
- **The DELETE is unrecoverable** if the INSERT fails partway. You've just lost months/years of historical data with no atomic rollback. Iceberg's `ROLLBACK TO SNAPSHOT` can save you, but only if you catch it BEFORE the next live commit overwrites the snapshot.
- **Position-delete files bloat the metadata** until the next VACUUM / compaction completes. Live queries slow down; manifest reads get expensive.
- **Concurrent writers** (the 12B/day Flink job) hit commit retries or write-conflict aborts during your massive DELETE.
- **Snapshot isolation** means readers see partial state during the transaction window. If it spans hours, BI dashboards and ML feature jobs are reading polluted data for hours.

**#3 (replay through live pipeline at 1/10 rate)** is dangerous in a different way — it saturates Kafka brokers and the Iceberg commit service. But it's RECOVERABLE: pause the backfill, let live catch up. The data isn't lost.

**#0 and #1** are the canonical safe patterns: isolation + wave restoration + UNION view cutover.

The discipline: NEVER apply destructive operations to a live production table when an isolated parallel approach is available. The handful of incidents in this class of project that make it to the post-mortem deck are almost always #2.

questions.prompt: For the 2-trillion-event Pinterest backfill, you need a compute engine to read Glacier-restored Parquet, run the new attribution logic, and write to the shadow Iceberg table. Spark Batch vs Flink Batch — which is the standard choice and why?
options: Flink Batch — it's faster than Spark for stateless transformations and the unified runtime is more modern
Use the EXISTING live Flink streaming job by replaying historical Kafka offsets — reuses existing infra

Spark Batch — embarrassingly-parallel + idempotent + restartable matches Spark's batch sweet spot; Spark-on-K8s with spot/preemptible nodes is mature; AQE (Adaptive Query Execution) coalesces small partitions and re-plans skewed joins at runtime, which matters because Glacier-restored Parquet has uneven file sizes; Iceberg connector + partition-overwrite semantics are most battle-tested on Spark
Trino / Presto — it's the fastest SQL engine and supports Iceberg natively
questions.explanation:
**Spark Batch** is the canonical choice for this profile. Three reasons:

1. **Workload shape**: bounded input, stateless transformation per partition, idempotent (rerun a failed partition without side effects), restartable. That's textbook Spark batch.
2. **Spot/preemptible economics**: Spark-on-Kubernetes with spot nodes has mature operator support (Apache YuniKorn, Volcano, the Kubeflow Spark operator). Backfills are interruption-tolerant, so spot can cut compute cost 60–80%. Flink Batch on spot works but the ecosystem is streaming-first.
3. **AQE handles Glacier-restored unevenness**: Adaptive Query Execution coalesces small partitions at runtime, swaps in broadcast join when one side is small, re-plans skewed joins. Glacier-restored Parquet often has wildly uneven file sizes (some months were heavier than others); AQE eats this without manual tuning.

**Option 1 (Flink Batch)**: improved dramatically — the unified BATCH execution mode is real and used in production at Alibaba, Stripe, others. But the deployment / cluster-manager / monitoring story is still streaming-first. For a one-time petabyte backfill on spot, Spark wins on operator maturity.

**Option 2 (replay through live Flink)**: the anti-pattern from ds-237. Saturates the live system.

**Option 4 (Trino)**: excellent for interactive SQL over Iceberg but not designed for batch-WRITE throughput at petabyte scale. Trino's write path is the weakest of the major OLAP engines; it would buckle on the 1.2PB write side of the backfill.

Pinterest's actual public talks on this topic confirm: live = Flink streaming, big backfills = Spark batch on Kubernetes.

questions.prompt: In a star schema, what is the primary advantage of denormalizing dimension tables?
options: Reduces storage cost significantly
Simplifies queries and improves read performance
Enforces referential integrity
Enables faster writes
questions.explanation: Denormalized (flat) dimension tables reduce the number of joins needed for analytical queries, making them simpler to write and faster to execute — the core tradeoff in Kimball's star schema approach.

questions.prompt: You need to track a customer's address history AND query their current address efficiently. Which SCD type is best?
options: SCD Type 1 (overwrite)
SCD Type 2 (add row)
SCD Type 3 (add column)
SCD Type 0 (retain original)
questions.explanation: SCD Type 2 adds a new row with effective/expiry dates on each change. A `WHERE is_current = TRUE` filter retrieves the current address. Type 1 loses history; Type 3 only tracks one prior value.

questions.prompt: In Kafka, what happens when a consumer's `auto.offset.reset` is set to `earliest` and the consumer group has no committed offset?
options: Consumer reads from the latest message only
Consumer reads from the beginning of the partition
Consumer throws an exception
Consumer waits for a new message to arrive
questions.explanation: `earliest` tells Kafka to start from offset 0 (the beginning) when no committed offset exists for the group. `latest` would skip all existing messages and only read new ones.

questions.prompt: You partition an events table by `event_date`. A query filters on `user_id` only. What is the likely performance impact?
options: Fast — partition pruning kicks in
Fast — Bloom filters handle user_id
Slow — full table scan across all partitions
Error — cross-partition queries are not allowed
questions.explanation: Partitioning only prunes partitions when the filter matches the partition key. Filtering by `user_id` on a date-partitioned table scans all partitions — a classic partition design mismatch.

questions.prompt: What is a data contract in the context of a data platform?
options: A legal agreement between data vendor and buyer
A schema-level SLA between a producer and all downstream consumers
A row-level encryption standard
A replication agreement between two databases
questions.explanation: A data contract is a formal agreement (schema, semantics, SLA, ownership) between a data producer and its consumers. It makes breaking changes explicit and shifts responsibility to the producing team.

questions.prompt: Designing an ETL pipeline for 10 million rides/hour with real-time dashboards, late-arriving data, deduplication, schema evolution, and failure recovery — which stack is the modern production standard?
options: Kafka → Spark Structured Streaming → Delta Lake with watermarks for late data, MERGE for deduplication, mergeSchema for evolution
RabbitMQ → AWS Lambda → PostgreSQL with triggers for deduplication
Batch SFTP every hour → Hadoop MapReduce → Hive with daily partitions
Kafka → Flink → Cassandra for real-time, plus nightly Spark batch for historical analysis
questions.explanation: Kafka absorbs burst ingestion, Spark Structured Streaming with watermarks handles late-arriving events within a configurable window, Delta Lake's MERGE deduplicates by ride_id atomically, and Delta's mergeSchema handles column additions without pipeline restarts. This stack is production-proven at Uber, Databricks, and Lyft scale. Flink+Cassandra (D) is valid but lacks native ACID deduplication and schema evolution.

questions.topic: data lake file format / table format
questions.prompt: For an 8PB data lake growing 15TB/day, which file format and table format combination best balances query performance, cost, and operational manageability?
options: CSV in S3 with daily folder partitions — simple but no predicate pushdown or compaction
Parquet + Apache Iceberg — columnar compression, hidden partitioning, partition evolution, time travel, and automated compaction via rewrite_data_files
ORC + Hive-style partitions — good compression but no partition evolution or ACID support
Avro + schema registry — optimal for streaming ingestion but poor for analytical queries
questions.explanation: Iceberg on Parquet provides columnar compression (reducing 15TB/day to ~3-5TB), hidden partitioning prevents partition skew from poor key choices, partition evolution allows changing strategies without full rewrites, and time travel supports debugging and rollback. The rewrite_data_files + expire_snapshots procedures handle ongoing compaction. Iceberg is production-standard at Netflix, Apple, and LinkedIn for petabyte-scale lakes.
